Plan: call the 400 to see a turn, and fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear draw (heart, 7/8 for a straight, or a pair).

I just paired the 5 on the turn (5h) for bottom pair in a three‑way pot (board Kc 9h 4d 5d). I’m out of position and both opponents have shown willingness to call/continue — this is a marginal holding with limited improvement odds (two remaining 5s + three remaining 6s = 5 outs ≈ 11% to make trips/two‑pair on the river). Betting here would build the pot with a weak hand and expose me to raises from Kx/stronger holdings; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me see a free river or call a small turn stab. Plan: check to control the pot, call a small bet to see the river, but fold to significant turn aggression. Re‑evaluate on the river for either a thin value bet or a check to showdown depending on the runout and opponent actions.

I'm holding QK with top pair (Kings) and a decent Queen kicker on a K♣ 9♥ 4♦ 5♦ board. Minni has checked twice (both preflop action and turn), clearly showing weakness. Flash called my flop bet from the button, likely with a weaker pair or draw. The 5♦ on the turn is a neutral card that doesn't significantly change the dynamics. I should continue my aggression with a turn bet of 750 (roughly 36% of pot) to extract value from weaker holdings while maintaining disciplined pot control. This sizing is consistent with my flop bet and keeps both opponents in range without over-committing. Minni's repeated weakness indicators make this a profitable continuation.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker. Claude is leading again on the turn after donk-betting the flop into two players. While this line often indicates top pair or better, the bet size of 750 into a 2100 pot (roughly 35% pot) gives me pot odds of approximately 4:1 to continue. Being on the button gives me position for the final street. While I must be wary of Minni acting behind me and Claude's perceived strength, folding middle pair for this price on a relatively static board would be too conservative. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate based on the final card and my opponents' actions.

I have bottom pair (5h) with 5h6h on Kc 9h 4d 5d in a three‑way pot. Claude leads the turn for 750 and Flash calls — both are committed enough that this is not an easy bluff spot. Pot is 3600; calling 750 requires ~17.2% equity (750 / (3600+750)). I only have 5 clean outs (two remaining 5s + three remaining 6s) → ~10.9% to improve on the river, well below the break‑even. Raising as a bluff in a multiway pot here is reckless and unlikely to fold both opponents. Calling is a negative‑EV call by the numbers and by the line. I fold to preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot.
No thought provided

On the river, the board is Kc-9h-4d-5d-7s. I have a pair of Nines with a Ten kicker. Claude has led all three streets after calling from the big blind pre-flop. His river bet is 1000 into a 3600 pot, which is quite small (less than 30% of the pot). While his lead into the pre-flop aggressor (me) usually indicates strength (like a King or a slow-played set), the pot odds here are very favorable. I need to be right about 18% of the time to break even on a call. Given the small sizing, Claude could be overvaluing a weaker pair, or he could be making a small 'blocking bet' with a hand that I beat. I have middle pair, and folding for this price would be exploitable. I will call to see the showdown.

Button with 6c5h in a 3-handed pot is a marginal but playable hand — connected and has good postflop potential with position. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and narrow the field rather than limp and invite isolation raises from Claude who frequently plays aggressively. A standard open to ~3x the big blind (300) gives me fold equity and a comfortable size to play postflop in position. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if I get one or two calls I’ll use position to c-bet or probe based on the flop texture.

Folding 75o from the small blind facing a raise. This is a weak, gapped hand with poor equity. While I have a healthy stack and Minni's button raise is relatively wide, calling requires 250 chips into a 450 pot against an opponent I don't need to play against. The hand has no strength, no connectivity, and no high card value. Even accounting for pot odds (1.8:1), my equity against Minni's raising range is approximately 25-35%, making this a clear fold. Optimal strategy is to release weak hands from early position and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd 2d. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude has folded. In a three-handed game, opening ranges from the button are typically wide. While Q2 suited is a weak hand, the pot odds (200 to call into a total pot of 450) and the fact that the hand is suited make it a marginal but acceptable defend in the Big Blind to see a flop. I will call the raise and see how the board develops, being cautious due to my weak kicker and being out of position.

I have hit top pair with a flush draw on this flop. While my kicker is weak, the addition of the diamond draw makes this a very strong hand in a three-handed context. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to her. This allows her to continue with her bluffs or wider range. If she bets, I have a clear continue, likely a call to keep her range wide or a check-raise depending on the bet sizing and the flow of the game, but for now, checking is the best start.

I opened preflop and got one caller; pot is 650. Flop Qs Td 6d — I hold 6c5h, so I’ve got a pair of sixes (weak kicker) on a two-diamond board with two overcards present. Flash’s check shows weakness or a draw. As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative — this hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards and diamond draws. I’ll make a small c-bet (~1/3 pot) to charge flush draws, get value from worse pairs and hands like 66/55/44 or draws, and keep the pot controllable given my marginal strength. If I face a sizable raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (pot-control or barreling only on safe turns).

I have top pair and a flush draw on a board of Qs-Td-6d. Minni's small continuation bet of 225 into 650 (roughly 35% pot) is standard for a pre-flop aggressor. While my kicker is weak (2), the combination of top pair and a diamond draw gives me significant equity against Minni's range. Calling is a strong play here as it keeps her bluffs in, avoids overplaying a weak kicker if she has a dominant Queen (like AQ or KQ), and allows me to see a turn card cheaply. I can reassess on the turn, especially if a diamond or a deuce appears.

On the turn, the 9h is a semi-coordinated card that completes some straight draws (like J8). I still hold top pair with a flush draw, but my kicker is extremely weak (2). Being out of position against Minni, checking is the most sound strategy. It allows me to control the pot size and see how she reacts to the turn card. If she bets, I have more than enough equity with my top pair and flush draw to call and see the river. If she checks behind, I get a free card to potentially improve to a flush or stay ahead with my pair.

I have second pair (6 with a 5 kicker) on Qs Td 6d 9h. Flash called my small flop c-bet and then checked the turn — that line is consistent with a medium-strength holding (Qx/Tx/9x), a slow-played pocket pair, or a missed/partial draw. The 9 on the turn is a relatively dangerous card: it hands many calling ranges (pairs of 9, some J8 straights, etc.) a clear improvement versus my weak pair. With position and a marginal made hand I prefer pot control rather than building a bigger pot I can’t comfortably protect. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and gives the opportunity to call or fold a single river bet depending on sizing. Plan: check now; call small river bets (roughly <= ~1/2 pot) for pot odds and fold to large, polarized bets; if Flash checks back I will re-evaluate and consider a small river value bet only on very safe cards.

I have successfully completed a flush on the river with the 4 of diamonds. The board is now Qs-Td-6d-9h-4d, and I hold the Qd 2d. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely has a hand with showdown value but not a monster, such as a Queen with a better kicker (KQ, AQ) or a medium pocket pair. While it is possible she checked a high diamond draw on the turn, my Queen-high flush is very strong in a three-handed game. I will bet 700 into the 1100 pot to extract value from her marginal made hands (like a Queen or a straight) that might feel obligated to call given the turn went check-check.

I have second pair (6s) with a weak 5-kicker. Line: I opened preflop, made a small c‑bet on Qs Td 6d which Flash called, both checked the 9h turn, and he now bets 700 on the river 4d (pot was 1100 before this bet). Calling 700 to win 2500 requires ~28% equity (700/2500). Flash’s line (call small flop, check turn, medium/large river bet) is weighted toward value hands — Qx, Tx, 9x that improved on the turn, or a diamond that just made a flush — and the river bet size (~64% pot) is polarized enough that many of his combos beat my second pair. Raising as a bluff is expensive and risky (would commit significant chips with poor blockers). I’m folding to avoid getting called by hands that dominate me and to preserve stack for better spots.

Folding Q6o preflop. While 3-handed poker calls for wider button ranges, this hand is below average in equity (~40%) and requires marginally better odds to profitably call. As the chip leader with a healthy stack early in the tournament (Hand 46/150), there's no urgency to play marginal hands. Better spots will arise against the blinds. Conservative, disciplined play preserves equity and allows me to exploit future situations with stronger ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h4s, which is a very weak hand even for short-handed play. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and Minni in the Big Blind. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, 74 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and doesn't play well post-flop if called. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8s 4s. While the hand is suited, it is quite weak for an opening raise even in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds, and I want to avoid playing marginal hands out of position or in bloated pots against the chip leader (Claude). I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have 5♥10♣ in the small blind heads-up vs Claude. This is a marginal hand, but in heads-up play it’s correct to be aggressive and try to seize the initiative rather than limp and play out-of-position. Folding would concede 50 chips but is too passive; calling 50 leaves me OOP with a weak hand. I’ll make a standard min-raise to 200 total to apply pressure and either pick up the blinds or play a defined pot with fold equity. If Claude calls I’ll c-bet favorable/dry flops and fold to strong 3-bets or large turn aggression with this hand.

Hand #47: I hold 8c4d (one of the worst starting hands in poker) in the Big Blind position. Minni raised to 200 from the Small Blind, and Flash folded. I need to call 100 more chips.

This is a clear fold situation:
1. 8-4 offsuit has minimal equity against a raiser's range
2. I'm out of position post-flop, making the hand even weaker
3. I have a healthy 19,675 chip stack with no short-stack pressure
4. Minni (9,500) has raised from the Small Blind, indicating some strength or aggression
5. Even accounting for positional advantage, this hand will have significant negative EV in a raised pot

I'm 46 hands into the tournament and have been selective with my starting hands. Folding weak hands out of position is fundamental solid poker strategy. The small 100-chip call is not worth the marginal situation that follows.

I’m on the button with 9♥6♠ — a weak, but playable hand in late position facing only the blinds. With the pot 150 and only 100 to call, I want to seize position and apply pressure rather than limp. Claude (SB) has shown aggression in previous hands and Flash (BB) defends sometimes, so I’ll make a clear opening that preserves fold equity but doesn’t commit a lot of chips. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) to steal the blinds and define ranges. Plan: fold to a significant 3-bet (this hand plays poorly out of line), but if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet selectively on dry/favorable boards (~50–60%) and proceed cautiously vs heavy action.

I'm holding QT offsuit in the small blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the button with a relatively short stack (9500). This is a reasonable spot to defend with a decent hand. QT offsuit is playable and can make strong hands postflop (top pair, straight draws, etc.). With my healthy stack (19625) and favorable pot odds (need ~35% equity to call), calling 250 more is appropriate. I'm out of position but I get to see the flop and play postflop poker against an exploitable button raiser. A flat call also keeps the pot manageable and allows me to play in position on later streets. Flash will have to decide on the big blind action next, potentially creating a multiway pot or simplifying the situation.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Five offsuit. Minni opened from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 chips to see a flop in a pot that will be 900 chips. This gives me excellent pot odds (22.2% equity needed). While A5o is not a premium hand, it performs reasonably well in 3-handed play. I will call to see a flop and play cautiously out of position.

I'm holding QT offsuit on a K-4-J flop in a 3-way pot (900). While I have no made pair, I have a powerful open-ended straight draw (8 outs to any Ace or 9 for the Broadway straight). This is a classic spot to apply aggression with a drawing hand. At 19,375 chips, I have sufficient stack depth to apply pressure. A bet of 500 (roughly 55% of pot) accomplishes several things: (1) generates fold equity against Minni's wide button-raise range and Flash's big blind defense, (2) puts me in position to take down the pot immediately or get value when I improve, and (3) gets aggressive value from weaker holdings that might call (like mid-pair combos). The relatively short stacks of both opponents (Minni 9500, Flash 10225) make them less likely to play back aggressively with marginal holdings, which favors my aggressive line with equity. I act first with position advantage remaining for future streets.

On a board of Ks-4c-Jh, my Ace-high has very little showdown value and limited potential for improvement. Claude leading out (a donk bet) into both the original raiser and me suggests he has at least a piece of this board, such as a King, a Jack, or a straight draw like QT. Given that Minni (the pre-flop raiser) is still behind me and the board is quite coordinated, calling with Ace-high is too risky and lacks sufficient equity. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I raised preflop with 9♥6♠ from the button and completely missed this flop (K♠ 4♣ J♥). Claude (SB) called preflop and now leads out 500 into a 1,400 pot. I have no pair and essentially no reliable backdoor draw — only ~6 clean outs to pair by the river. Calling 500 to win ~2,400 requires about 20.8% equity; the naive chance to hit a pair by the river (~24%) overstates my real equity because many of those pair-outs still lose to Kx, Jx, or bigger pairs Claude is likely betting. Raising would be a pure, high-risk bluff against a deep and capable opponent. Given my earlier plan to proceed cautiously when faced with meaningful aggression, the +EV play here is to fold and preserve my ~95bb stack for better spots.

Folding 8-4 offsuit on the button. While position is excellent in this 3-handed game, 8-4o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even against short stacks (Flash: 10,175, Minni: 9,400), this hand lacks both high card strength and playability. I need to only get involved with reasonable holdings from the button, and this hand doesn't meet that threshold. At 49 of 150 hands, there's plenty of time to accumulate chips with better spots. Fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am in the small blind with 4d 7c, which is a very weak hand. Claude has already folded from the button. Although it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop, this hand has poor playability and I will be out of position against Minni for the rest of the hand. It is more prudent to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 6c 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Given that there are two aggressive players in the blinds (Minni and Claude), this is a clear fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous hand.

Heads-up from the small blind with A♠7♠ — a suited ace that plays very well post-flop and gains a lot of value when I seize the initiative. Only 50 to call, but opening to a standard 3xBB (300 total) gives me fold equity, defines the pot, and lets me play the flop in position. Claude is deep and can 3-bet, but I can fold to a very large 3-bet or call a reasonable sized 3-bet to realize equity; vs a call I’ll continue aggressively in position. Raise to 300 to maximize equity and pressure the big blind.

Calling with Ad4c in the Big Blind against Minni's raise. I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (need ~33% equity) and maintain positional advantage postflop. The Ace provides reasonable equity against a SB raising range, and I can control the hand on subsequent streets. With a healthy 20,175 chip stack, this is a reasonable decision to see the flop.

I raised preflop and am now in position with A♠7♠ on A♥7♦4♠ — top pair with a decent kicker and a backdoor spade possibility. Claude called my 3x preflop, so his range is wide and contains many worse A‑x and medium pairs that will pay off a modest bet. Betting here gains value from worse aces and pair+draw hands, protects my hand against potential turn/river equity, and keeps the initiative in position. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (300 into the 600 pot) to extract thin value while still folding to clear strength (check‑raise/large raise) and reevaluate if he just calls.

Calling Minni's flop bet with a strong pair. Ad4c makes top pair (Aces), and my 4-kicker is respectable. I have excellent pot odds (3:1), requiring only ~25% equity. Against Minni's typical SB range, my hand is likely best or has strong equity. With a healthy 19,975 chip stack, calling to see the turn is the profitable play. I can control the hand postflop and potentially win a larger pot if Minni continues betting.

I hold A♠7♠ on 7♦-4♠-A♥-8♣ — two pair (Aces and Sevens). Claude called my preflop raise and my flop c-bet, so his range is wide and contains many worse A‑x hands and medium pairs that will pay a value bet. The turn is a relatively dry card that doesn’t complete obvious straights or flushes, so betting for value and to deny a free river card is correct. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to extract from worse aces and pairs while keeping worse hands and draws in to call; if Claude check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate for sets/full houses, but a large portion of his range will call a sizing like this. Bet 800 into the 1200 pot (roughly 2/3 pot).

I'm holding Ad4c on a board of 7d 4s Ah 8c, giving me top pair with a respectable kicker plus a second pair (Aces and Fours). Minni is showing consistent aggression throughout the hand, raising pre-flop, the flop, and now the turn. The pot is currently 2000 and I need to call 800, giving me pot odds of 3.5:1, requiring only ~22% equity to call profitably.

My hand analysis:
- I have two pair (Aces and Fours), which is a very strong holding
- Minni's range likely includes AK/AQ/AJ, 76/78 (two pair possibilities), or potentially a set (77, 88, AA)
- My hand dominates most of her premium holdings
- Even against likely hands like AJ, AQ, or AK, I'm ahead
- The only concerning holdings would be a set (77, 88, AA) or A8

With stack sizes being favorable (I have 19,675 vs Minni's 8,150), I can comfortably call and evaluate the river. The pot odds are excellent and my hand has strong equity in this spot. Raising here could overplay my hand given the uncertainty of her holdings, and calling allows me to control the pot while gathering more information.

This is a straightforward call given the odds and hand strength.

I have A♠7♠ — two pair (Aces and Sevens) on a fairly dry river (7♦-4♠-A♥-8♣-Q♣). Claude cold‑called preflop and then called my flop and turn bets, a passive/calling line that typically contains worse A‑x, medium pocket pairs and the occasional slow‑played set. He checked the river, showing some hesitation/weakness; I should be trying to extract thin value rather than just checking down. A half‑pot value bet (~1/2 pot = 1,400 into 2,800) is sized to get calls from worse aces and medium pairs (and to charge missed draws) while not overbetting and folding out only the very weakest hands. There are a few combos that beat me (sets, A8, rare straights), but they’re limited — the sizing balances extracting value and minimizing fold equity loss. If he check‑raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (that line is consistent with very strong holdings), but the straightforward play here is a 1,400 value bet.

Calling with two pair (Aces and Fours) on the river. Minni's aggressive betting line is consistent with strong Aces (AK/AQ/AJ), sets, or A8. With 3:1 pot odds, I only need ~25% equity to call profitably. My hand dominates most value hands in her range and has significant equity against her full distribution. This is a straightforward value call with excellent odds and a strong holding.

I open‑raise to seize the button advantage and apply fold equity. JcTh is a playable, connected hand that performs well in position — good for stealing the blinds and for postflop play if called. With stacks ~123bb effective and the pot only the blinds (150), a standard 3x BB open to 300 is the right sizing: big enough to fold out many blind defenders and isolate heads‑up, but small enough to leave room to maneuver postflop. Calling 100 would be passive and invite multiway pots with worse odds; a min‑raise to 200 is too weak. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll usually fold JTo, but I’ll call or continue versus smaller 3‑bets depending on opponent and sizing, then c‑bet favorable flops and control the pot on coordinated/overcard boards.

I'm holding AK (King of Hearts and Ace of Spades), one of the premium hands in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and I'm in the small blind.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: AK is a premium hand that plays well in all situations - strong pre-flop and excellent post-flop playability
2. Position: I'm in small blind facing a button raise, which is a favorable situation to apply pressure
3. Opponent tendencies: Minni has been aggressive throughout the session with frequent raises
4. Stack dynamics: All three stacks are relatively healthy with no short-stacked concerns
5. Aggression factor: Raising here isolates Minni and allows me to potentially take down the pot pre-flop or build a pot I can win with strong equity

A raise to 800 total (750 additional) is a standard 3-bet size that applies meaningful pressure while keeping the pot manageable. This capitalizes on the premium nature of AK and takes the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4d, which is a very weak hand. Minni opened from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position against two aggressive actions, this is an easy fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with JcTh and Claude 3‑bet to 800 from the small blind. With ~120bb effective stacks and the pot at 1,200, calling 500 more (total commitment 800) gives ~29% immediate pot odds and leaves an SPR ≈ 6.6 — a good spot to play postflop in position. JTo has solid playability in position (can realize equity, use position to barrel or fold to aggression, and has useful blockers). Claude has shown regular preflop aggression from the blinds, so his 3‑bet range is likely wide enough to justify calling. A 4‑bet would be a thin bluff that unnecessarily bloats the pot; folding would be too tight. I'll call and use my positional advantage to navigate the flop.

Betting on the flop with AK that flopped top pair with nut kicker. The board (Kc Td 2h) is relatively dry and favorable for my hand. I 3-bet pre-flop, so continuation betting maintains consistency and builds the pot with my strong holding. This is a standard continuation bet of approximately 60% of the pot, which is both value-oriented and keeps the pot manageable.

Flop Kc Td 2h — I hold JcTh: one pair (tens) with a decent Jack kicker, and I’m in position. Claude’s 1,000 into a 2,700 pot is a fairly small c-bet from an aggressive player who 3‑bet preflop, so his range is wide (AK, Kx, AQ, AJ, some Tx, and a lot of bluffs). Calling is the best line: the price (~27–30% pot odds) is favorable, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, preserves my positional advantage, and lets me control the pot on later streets. A raise to 2,000 is too thin and needlessly bloats the pot with a vulnerable pair; folding is overly tight. I’ll call the 1,000 and re-evaluate on the turn — proceed for value if I improve or opponent shows weakness, and be prepared to fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss.

I hold AK and flopped top pair with the nut kicker (Ace) on the King. The board is now Kc Td 2h 5d. The 5d on the turn is a complete blank and doesn't improve most of Minni's likely holdings (QQ-JJ, AQ, AJ, KQ, KJ, etc.). 

I've been aggressive pre-flop (3-bet to 800) and on the flop (bet 1000), so continuing with a turn bet maintains consistency and extracts value from Minni's calling range. The turn is an excellent card to continue betting as it didn't improve the board for their range.

Given Minni has now called a 3-bet and a flop bet, they likely have a moderate hand like middle pair, AQ, or KJ. My hand is well ahead of this range. I'll bet approximately 55% of the pot (2000 chips) to balance between extracting value and keeping Minni in the hand, while also setting up pot sizing for river decisions. This bet size is standard and maintains pressure.

I call. I have top pair (tens) with a decent Jack kicker and I’m in position against an opponent who 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop/turn — his range includes Kx/AK/AQ/Tx and some bluffs. The turn 5d doesn’t change much and I’m getting ~26% to call (2000 to win ~7700), which is well within a reasonable calling threshold with a hand that has showdown value and blocks some K/Ax combos. A raise here would bloate the pot with a marginal hand and play into his value-heavy line; folding is too nitty given the pot odds and my positional advantage. Plan: call, see the river and either extract value or fold to clear, heavy aggression if he shows strong strength.

On the river with AK (King of Hearts and Ace of Spades), I hold top pair with the nut kicker. The board is Kc Td 2h 5d 9c - a blank river that doesn't complete many draws or improve most holdings.

Analysis:
1. Hand strength: My AK is the second-best possible hand (only beaten by KK, QQ/JJ rarely, or straights with 87). Top pair with the ace kicker is extremely strong.
2. Opponent profile: Minni called aggressively through pre-flop (calling a 3-bet), flop, and turn (calling 2000), indicating they have a hand with some value - likely a pair or draw.
3. The 9c river is a complete blank that doesn't meaningfully improve most of Minni's likely holdings (pairs, AQ, AJ, draws).
4. Minni checked to me on the river, suggesting they're either giving up on a marginal hand or considering a check-call with something weaker than AK.

Betting strategy: A value bet of 3500 (~45% of pot) extracts maximum value from Minni's calling range while remaining reasonable in size. This hand is far too strong to check through. Even if Minni has a mid-pair or weaker ace, we're well ahead and should bet for value.

Call 3500. I have top pair (Tens) with a decent Jack kicker and I'm in position. Claude 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop and turn — his line is value‑heavy (Kx, AK/AQ, some AT/Tx) but also contains bluffs from a wide 3‑betting range. The river bet of 3500 gives me roughly 24% pot odds (3500 to win ~14.7k), so my hand only needs to be ahead of ≈24% of his river range to make a +EV call. Raising is thin here — it will get called or reraised by the hands that already beat me (Kx, better Tx, two‑pair) and I don't have the spare equity to credibly bluff a larger sizing. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my showdown value. I call 3500 and plan to show down; calling leaves me with ~5050 behind to continue applying pressure post‑hand if needed.

I'm on the button with 7d-6c in a 3-handed game. While this is a weak hand, I'm in the strongest position at the table. Both opponents are severely short-stacked: Minni has only 4,950 chips (roughly 50BB) and Flash has 10,025 chips (roughly 100BB). I'm the chip leader with 24,875. 

In 3-handed play, the button has significant positional advantage, and I should be opening wider ranges, but 7-6o is marginal. Calling here is superior to folding because:

1. I get to see a flop in position against two short stacks
2. If I hit (pair of 7s, 6s, or a draw), I can play for value
3. If I miss, I can fold to aggression or use position to take down the pot with a c-bet
4. Raising would likely fold out Minni (who is desperate) and give Flash better odds to call

Calling 100 more to see a flop is the optimal approach here - it's positionally sound and keeps both short stacks in the hand where they're less likely to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of fives (5h 5c). Claude limped from the button, which often indicates a wide or marginal range. Minni, in the Big Blind, is currently short-stacked with about 50 big blinds after losing a large pot in the previous hand. Raising here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or isolate one player. A raise to 400 is a standard size to put pressure on both the limper and the short stack in the big blind.

I'm in the big blind with 9♣?9h/3s (9h 3s), a very weak offsuit hand. Flash (SB) has raised to 400 and Claude called, creating a multiway pot; the pot is 600 and it costs me 300 to continue. Versus two opponents and out of position postflop, 9‑3o has very poor equity and poor playability — very unlikely to realize value or make profitable bluffs. Preserving my 4,950 stack for better spots and folding is the prudent play here.

I'm on the button with 7d-6c facing a raise to 400 from Flash (SB). While this is a marginal hand, several factors support calling: (1) I have significant positional advantage post-flop as the button; (2) I'm a heavy chip leader (24,775 vs Flash's 9,675), giving me flexibility to take marginal spots; (3) Flash's short stack means if I hit on the flop (pair of 7s, 6s, or a 7-6 draw with potential to make a straight), I can win chips quickly; (4) Getting 3:1 odds (300 to call, 900 total pot) is reasonable for 7-6o which has approximately 30-40% equity against Flash's typical SB raising range. Folding would be overly tight in this spot given my chip advantage and positional edge. Raising would be over-aggressive with such a weak holding and risks unnecessary chips when I can achieve my goal (flop value or take down the pot with position) via a call.

I have flopped a set of fives, which is an extremely strong hand on this relatively dry 7-5-T board. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard and likely to be called by any Ten, any pair, or straight draws like 89 or 68. Claude has a large stack and has been active, so I want to start building a pot immediately to maximize value. A bet of 450 (half pot) is a good sizing to keep his marginal hands in while charging draws.

I have top pair (7d paired with 7h on the board) with a marginal 6-kicker. The flop 7h-5s-Td is relatively dry and favors my hand. 

Key factors supporting a call:
1. **Hand strength**: Top pair has reasonable showdown value and is likely ahead of many hands in Flash's desperate raising range
2. **Pot odds**: I'm getting 4:1 odds (450 to call into 1,800 total) - excellent for top pair
3. **Stack dynamics**: Flash has only 9,225 chips (92BB), making him desperate. A short-stacked opponent's flop bet could range from premium hands to complete air
4. **Positional advantage**: I'm on the button and can control the action on future streets
5. **Flexibility**: Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to reassess on the turn with full information
6. **Risk management**: I have 24,475 chips as the chip leader, so I can afford to take this marginal spot

Raising here would be over-aggressive with a medium-strength hand against a desperate opponent who might fold too much (meaning I miss value) or just get it in against a stronger hand. Folding would be unnecessarily tight given my odds and positional advantage.

Call 450 and play the turn in position.

I have a set of fives on a board that is becoming increasingly coordinated. The six of hearts on the turn introduces a potential straight (8-9) and a heart flush draw. However, I still have a very strong hand that warrants betting for value and protection. I want to charge any draws and get paid by top-pair hands like AT, KT, or QT. A bet of 1,200 into the 1,800 pot is a strong sizing that puts pressure on Claude's range while allowing me to control the pot size if the board gets even scarier on the river.

I have two pair (7s and 6s) on a board of 7h-5s-Td-6h. This is a strong made hand with excellent showdown value.

Key factors supporting a raise:

1. **Hand strength**: Two pair is a premium holding on this board. Flash's likely turn-raising range (given his desperate 8025 chip stack and aggressive play pattern) includes: draws like 89, overcards (AK/AQ), weaker pairs, or bluffs. I'm ahead of most of this range.

2. **Stack dynamics**: Flash has only 6825 chips remaining after his 1200 bet. Raising to 2400 puts significant pressure on him. If he calls, he'll have roughly 5625 left with a 5400 pot, making the river essentially a shove situation anyway.

3. **Value extraction**: By raising now while ahead, I build the pot and increase expected value. If Flash has overcards or a weaker holding, he may fold. If he has a strong hand, he'll likely shove regardless, and I have two pair which is well-positioned.

4. **Position and aggression**: I've called passively through the flop and turn. A raise now shows strength and takes control of the action, which is important against a desperate, aggressive short-stacked opponent.

5. **Risk management**: Even if Flash shoves the remaining 6825, I'm likely a significant favorite with two pair against his probable holdings. My remaining 22825 chips provide ample cushion.

Raising to 2400 (min raise) is the optimal play here—it applies maximum pressure while building the pot with the strongest hand postflop so far.

I have a set of fives on a board that has become quite coordinated. While the six of hearts on the turn completes a possible straight for 98 or 84 and introduces a flush draw, my hand is extremely strong. Claude's min-raise to 2400 could indicate a straight, but it could also be a semi-bluff with a draw or a value bet with two-pair (like T7 or 76). Even if I am currently behind a straight, I have 10 outs (the remaining 5, three 7s, three 10s, and three 6s) to improve to a full house or quads on the river. Given my stack size relative to the pot, calling and seeing a scary river card would put me in a difficult spot. By shoving now, I maximize value from two-pair hands and draws while ensuring I see the river. Claude has a massive stack and may be willing to call with a wider range of hands.

Flash is all-in for 9,225 total (I need to call 6,825). With two pair (sevens and sixes) on a board of 7h-5s-Td-6h, this is a straightforward call. Analysis: (1) Hand strength: Two pair is a premium holding with excellent showdown value. Flash's all-in range from the small blind in a desperate 8,025-chip stack includes draws (89 for OESD), overcards (AK/AQ), weaker holdings, and bluffs - most of which I have significant equity against. (2) Pot odds: I'm getting roughly 2:1 odds (calling 6,825 to win a 13,425 pot). With two pair, my equity against Flash's likely all-in range easily exceeds the 33% required to justify the call. (3) Equity analysis: Against typical short-stack shoves (draws, overcards, weaker pairs), I'm a significant favorite. Even in bad spots like facing a made hand (TT+), I have outs to improve. (4) Stack considerations: If I win, I'll have ~28k chips and be in control of the final table. If I lose, I'm still in decent shape with ~15k. This is a mathematically sound spot with a premium hand against a desperate opponent's wide range. Call and let it play out.

I am on the button with Q8o. It's a three-handed game. Q8o is a decent hand to play from the button, especially since the small blind (Minni) has a relatively short stack. Raising here is standard to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I'll make a standard 3x open to 300.

Q♥6♣ is a marginal hand out of position, but Flash (button) opened to 3bb and is often wide/aggressive. Folding 50 already posted is too tight in a 3-handed spot with 49bb effective; 3-betting with Q6o is unnecessarily polar and will put me in tough spots if called or 4-bet. Postflop I’ll proceed cautiously OOP: fold to heavy action on dry boards, but continue with top-pair, good draws, or when I can apply pressure on favorable textures. If Claude 3-bets preflop, I’ll likely release Q6o.

I'm holding 5s-Jd from the Big Blind with two opponents already invested. Flash raised to 300 from the button (standard aggression), and Minni called 250 from the small blind. While I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds on the 200 call, I need to consider the broader context: (1) 5-Jack offsuit is a weak hand with limited equity against a raising range, (2) I'll be out of position postflop against two opponents, (3) I have a healthy stack (14,700) and should be selective about marginal spots, (4) Minni's small blind call suggests some hand strength. With weak hand strength and positional disadvantage, this is a clear fold. I'll preserve my chips for stronger opportunities where I have better equity or position.

I have top pair (6s on board + my 6♣) with a mediocre Q kicker on a low, fairly dry 5♣-6♠-2♣ flop. Button opened wide, so a lot of his range are overcards or weaker pairs that I want to charge — while the only hands that currently crush me are sets (22,55,66) or higher pocket pairs. On a flop of 5c 6s 2c, I have Queen-high with no draw. Minni, who has a short stack of 4400, has led out for 250 into a pot of 700. I’m on the button with Ks6h (about 53bb). This hand is marginal but much stronger in position — it blocks some strong Kx combinations and can steal the blinds. With only the blinds acting behind and a pot of 150, I want to take the initiative and avoid playing a multiway pot out of position. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold K6o; if I get one caller I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards.

Minni raises to 300 from the button with a short stack of 5,050 chips. I'm in the small blind with K5o, a weak hand that plays poorly out of position.

This is a straightforward fold - K5 is simply too weak from the small blind facing a raise, even against an aggressive short-stacked opponent.

In the Big Blind with 9c7c, I am facing a button raise from Minni to 300. The pot is currently 450 and it costs 200 more to call. I am in the Big Blind and called a pre-flop raise from the Button. The flop comes A-T-2 with two hearts. This board is very favorable for Minni's range as the pre-flop aggressor. I opened from the button and am facing one caller. A half-pot c-bet (~50%) is the right mix of pressure and pot control: it folds out many unpaired hands and keeps the pot manageable if called. I’ll bet 325 into 650. Plan: fold to a strong check-raise/shove (villain showing heavy value or strong draws); if called proceed cautiously — barrel only when I pick up meaningful equity (King or heart) or face passive action.

On a board of Ace-Ten-Deuce, Minni's continuation bet as the pre-flop aggressor is standard and very likely to have connected with her range. Given the pot odds of 3:1, calling with 9-high against a range that likely contains many Aces, Tens, and better high-card hands is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Pocket deuces from the button in 3-handed play is a standard raising hand. I have excellent position and will act last post-flop, which is crucial for a marginal hand like pocket deuces. At 14,650 chips, I can comfortably make this play. I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four of hearts. Claude, on the Button, has opened with a raise to 300. While suited, Qh4h is a weak hand to play out of position against a Button range. I'm on the big blind with K♥8♥ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 more (300 total). I have K♥8♥ on an A♦ J♠ 3♠ flop — no pair, no heart on board, just K-high. I will check and concede the initiative: if Claude checks back we can see a cheap turn and reassess; if he fires, I’ll fold to standard continuation bets (only consider calling a very tiny probe ≤ ~1/3 pot as a float with a concrete plan to fire/realize equity on safe turns). I have pocket deuces on a board of Ad-Js-3s after opening from the button. This hand doesn't justify aggressive action when I'm vulnerable to her likely holdings (pairs, ace-high, broadway cards).

I paired the 8 on the turn (K♥8♥ on A♦ J♠ 3♠ 8♣). Betting a small amount now (~1/3–1/2 pot) is the best play: it extracts value from worse hands, charges spade and broadway draws, and doesn’t bloat the pot out of position. Minni checked the flop and is now making a modest turn raise to 250 into a 900 pot, giving me 3.6:1 odds. While pocket deuces is the weakest pair, the pot odds justify a call. The small bet size (about 28% of the pot) suggests she may not have a premium holding like AA or a set. In 3-handed play, her range is wide enough that my pair has reasonable showdown value against many of her non-premium hands. Calling also keeps the pot manageable and allows me to make an informed decision on the river based on the final card and her action. If I raise here with such a marginal hand, I'm likely only winning against weak holdings she'd fold, while getting called by hands that dominate me. The goal is to see the river economically with a pair that could easily be best.

Board: A♦ J♠ 3♠ 8♣ K♦. I hold K♥8♥ — one pair, kings with an 8 kicker. Claude checked the flop and merely called my small 250 turn probe; that line is consistent with a lot of medium-strength hands (Ax, Jx, medium pairs) and some weak holdings. Many of those hands (all Ax and Jx, some Kx with better kickers, and two‑pair combos) beat my pair. A river value bet only works if he calls with worse hands (Qx, Tx, small pairs), but given his passive line to this point those callers look thin. Conversely a bet will get called by stronger hands and can be raised by his best hands, putting me in a tough spot OOP. With pot = 1150 and my stack = 5150, the cleanest +EV play is to check and see if he blinks or gives up — I’ll only call a reasonable bet but fold to a large river raise. Checking realizes my showdown equity without bloating the pot against a range that often dominates me.

River play with pocket deuces. Minni raised 250 on the turn into the 8c and now checks the river after the K arrives. Her action pattern is revealing: she checked the flop, raised the turn (possibly hitting with the 8 or semi-bluffing), but now checks the river. This river check after a turn raise typically indicates either a weak hand or uncertainty about hand strength. With pocket deuces—the weakest possible pair—I have limited value. If I bet, I'm either winning against hands she'd fold (which I beat anyway) or getting called by hands that dominate me (better pairs, ace-high, etc.). My pair likely has showdown value given her range width and her obvious hesitation (turn raise followed by river check). Betting risks losing extra chips without generating meaningful value. Checking allows me to see the hand at showdown, minimizing loss on my weakest holding while potentially winning against her range. Given her 5,150 stack is relatively short, she's playing some marginal hands, and her river check suggests this is one of them.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (3s). In a 3-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. Q4o is a weak hand, especially out of position against Flash's button raise. Although I'm facing 2.25:1 pot odds (needing to call 200 to win 450), my hand quality doesn't justify continuing here. I’m on the button with 2h3c — a very weak, unsuited connected hand. With Ac7h in the small blind facing Minni's raise to 200, I have a premium starting hand. At hand 57/150 in the tournament, position and stack sizes matter. This is better than folding a strong holding or overcommitting pre-flop without seeing the flop texture.

I have 5h 6d in the Big Blind. Minni raised to 200 from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. I only need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot that is currently 500. Betting 400 on the flop with Ac7h against K-7-8 board. I have a solid pair of sevens with ace kicker and a backdoor flush draw. I have an open-ended straight draw (5-6 on a K-7-8 board), needing a 4 or a 9 to complete my hand. Claude has led into two players for 400 into a 600 pot. The pot odds are currently 1000 + 400 = 1400, and I need to call 400, which gives me 3.5:1 on my money. I prefer calling over raising here because Claude's lead into two players suggests strength, and I want to see a cheap turn card without risking a large three-bet from Minni, who is still to act behind me.

I opened preflop as a button steal with 2♥3♣ and the flop K♥7♠8♥ completely misses my hand. Claude leads with a 400 bet and Flash calls — that line is consistent with a real made hand (Kx, 8x, a pair + backdoor, or a strong draw) or at least enough resistance that my weak backdoor heart + tiny straight outs have nearly zero equity. On the turn with Ac7h on a board of Kh-7s-8h-9s, I'm in a somewhat precarious position. I have a pair of sevens with an ace kicker—a moderate holding that faces uncertainty. The 9s turn card is particularly dangerous for my hand, as it completes several possible draws for Flash: Q-T and J-T make straits, and two-pair is also possible. Flash called my flop bet of 400 with a 19,150 stack, suggesting he likely had a draw or strong enough hand to continue. By checking the turn, I accomplish several strategic goals: (1) I gather information about Flash's hand—if he checks, I likely have the best hand and can see the river cheaply; if he bets, I can reassess with real data; (2) I avoid committing more chips with a hand that's potentially dominated; (3) I preserve my stack (13,400) while remaining flexible for a river decision. Minni is no longer in the hand. This is a solid risk-management play given the threat the turn card presents and the uncertainty of Flash's holdings.

On the turn, the 9s completes my straight (5-6-7-8-9). Claude, who led on the flop, has now checked to me. This check often indicates a one-pair hand like a King that is now wary of the coordinated board or a completed draw. While a higher straight (J-T) is possible, I must bet for value against his likely range of top pairs, two pairs, or sets. On the turn with Ac7h against Flash's raise to 850 on a board of Kh-7s-8h-9s, I'm facing a critical decision. I have a pair of sevens with ace kicker, which is a moderate but vulnerable holding.

Pot odds analysis: Pot is 2250, facing 850 to call. Pot odds are 2.65:1, requiring ~27% equity. My hand likely has sufficient equity against Flash's turn raise range despite the dangerous board.

Flash's range assessment: His raise after my check suggests strength—likely Q-T or J-T (straight possibilities), K-x (top pair or better), or a semi-bluff with a strong draw. Against these hands, my pair of sevens with ace kicker should have meaningful equity (30-40%).

Strategic considerations:
1. Stack size (13,400 remaining) allows this call without committing too much
2. River card is still unknown—an ace, flush card, or unpaired card all provide new information
3. Calling enables river decisions based on concrete outcomes
4. Folding sacrifices fold equity and future winning chances unnecessarily

While the board is dangerous (9s completed straight draws), the pot odds justify calling. If an unfavorable river arrives, I can reassess further action then. This maintains flexibility and avoids over-folding marginal but playable hands in tournament poker.

River decision with Ac7h on a board of Kh-7s-8h-9s-9d. I have a pair of sevens with ace kicker facing Flash who raised the turn to 850 and then checked the river. This river check is telling—it suggests either (1) Flash missed his likely straight draw (Q-T or J-T), or (2) he has a modest holding like middle pair that he's checking down. My 7-7-A is vulnerable against straights and top pair combinations, but it has meaningful showdown value against missed draws and weaker holdings. Betting would only gain value if Flash is folding or if I'm ahead against marginal hands, but the risk of facing a raise or losing to a made hand makes this unappealing. We're early in the tournament (57/150), and stack preservation matters. Checking back to showdown with my reasonable holding is the prudent play—I avoid committing additional chips to uncertainty while maintaining the flexibility to fold on the river if Flash bets aggressively. With a pair and decent kicker, seeing the showdown is the appropriate risk-management approach here.

On the river, the 9d pairs the board, which is a significant card. While I still hold a straight (5-6-7-8-9), it is now the lowest possible straight on a board of Kh-7s-8h-9s-9d. Any player holding a Ten (such as KT, QT, JT, or T8) now holds a higher straight (6-10). Furthermore, the pairing of the Nine means that any player with a Nine has trips, and hands like 77, 88, or K9 have improved to a full house. Claude led the flop and then check-called the turn, which often indicates a strong one-pair hand like a King or a slow-played monster. By checking the river, Claude is likely looking for a cheap showdown with top pair or perhaps trapping. Since I have solid showdown value with my straight but are unlikely to get called by many worse hands—and are very vulnerable to a check-raise from better hands—checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and minimize risk.

Calling 100 chips on the button with Q8o is justified by favorable position and pot odds (1.5:1). With position post-flop and reasonable stack sizes against a small blind raise and desperate big blind, I can play my hand efficiently based on flop action. The low entry cost and button leverage make this a standard play.

I have been dealt pocket nines (9s, 9d) in the small blind. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Claude has limped from the button, which often indicates a wide, speculative range. Minni is in the big blind with a relatively short stack of approximately 60 big blinds. I have A♥4♠ in the big blind facing a 500 open from the small blind (Flash) with Claude cold‑calling. With Q8o on the button facing a raise to 500 from Flash (small blind) and a call from Minni (big blind with a short stack of 5,550), I need to reassess my hand strength.

Q8o is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant calling a raise in most situations. While I'm getting 2.75:1 pot odds (400 to call vs 1,100 pot), this doesn't justify calling with a weak holding against an aggressive raise and a call from the big blind.

2. I'd be out of position post-flop

4. Minni's short stack means they could be playing wide, but Flash's raise suggests strength

This is a standard fold. I should be selective about the hands I play, and Q8o doesn't meet the threshold for continuing against a raise in this configuration.

I have pocket nines, but the flop of Ace-King-Four is extremely unfavorable. Both an Ace and a King are present, which are heavily represented in the pre-flop calling range of the Big Blind. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I could consider a continuation bet, but on such a coordinated board that hits a defender's range so well, checking is the safer, more pot-controlling play. I want to see how Minni reacts to this flop before committing more chips with a hand that is now likely second-best or worse.

I have two pair (Aces and Fours) on Kc Ad 4c — a very strong hand heads‑up but vulnerable to sets (AA/44/KK) and to giving free cards to club/runner draws. Flash checked, so I should charge worse A‑x, Kx and small pairs and deny equity to backdoor club draws. Bet for value and protection: ~3/4 pot is large enough to extract and protect while leaving room to fold to an obviously strong raise. If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn; if faced with a big, committed raise I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a polarizing jam or call a modest raise and re‑assess).

The flop of Ace-King-Four is very unfavorable for my pocket nines. After I checked, Minni bet 800 into a 1900 pot. Minni's range for calling a raise from the Big Blind includes many hands containing an Ace or a King (like A-T, A-J, K-Q). Since there are two overcards to my pair, I am likely behind. Continuing would require me to call nearly half the pot with a hand that has very few ways to improve. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 3c 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although I am the current chip leader, there is no strategic reason to open-raise with such poor connectivity and high-card value. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up from the small blind with Kd7c. With only the big blind to face, I want initiative and fold equity rather than playing this marginal hand passively out of position. Raise to 300 (3x the BB) is a standard open size here: it pressures a wide BB defending range, keeps the pot manageable with 66BB effective stacks, and avoids the exploitability of a minimum raise. Plan: if Claude calls, c-bet on favorable flops and proceed cautiously vs strong resistance; fold (or re-evaluate) to a large 3-bet unless pot odds or a specific read justify continuing.

With 6c 9h in the Big Blind against Minni's raise to 300, I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 600). While 6-9 offsuit is not a premium hand, several factors make a call reasonable: (1) The pot odds require only ~25% equity to break even; (2) It's heads-up, improving my equity versus Minni's wide raising range; (3) Minni has a short stack (6,350), making this a good spot to realize postflop equity; (4) I have position and can play well on the flop; (5) My stack (12,350) is healthy enough to see a flop and fold if I miss badly. Given Minni's aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament and the favorable pricing, calling is superior to folding this weak hand.

I opened preflop and got a caller. Flop 5c-4c-Jd with Kd7c gives me K-high and a backdoor club runner-runner — nothing made but I'm in position. This is a relatively dry board that misses a large portion of the BB’s calling range (many broadways, weak pairs, suited connectors), so a continuation bet should win a lot right now and charges any draws. I size to ~1/2 pot (300) to apply pressure and get value from worse Kx/Tx-type hands while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously vs real strength or a check-raise.

On the 5c 4c Jd flop, I'm facing Minni's raise to 300. While the pot odds are favorable (1200:300 = 4:1, needing ~20% equity), my hand strength is extremely poor. 6c 9h offsuit has virtually no made hand—just 6-high with no realistic draws on this board. The flop is unfavorable for me: J-high with a pair already present, limiting my ability to improve. Against Minni's flop raising range, which includes overcards, pocket pairs, and various hands with more board connectivity, my equity is almost certainly below the 20% needed to justify a call. Although Minni is short-stacked (6,050), this doesn't change my hand's weakness. The preflop call at 3:1 odds was marginal but defensible with position; however, her flop action indicates strength or desperation, and my hand doesn't have the raw equity to continue. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

I’m on the Button with 5♣6♥ — a speculative, connected but offsuit hand that plays well in position. With only the blinds to beat and a healthy stack (≈69 BB) I should open a wide range to take the initiative and pick up dead money. I’ll raise rather than limp: sizing to 3x the big blind (300) gives more fold equity and discourages loose defense from the big blind compared with a bare min-raise. If called I’ll play the flop in position; if faced with a large 3-bet I’ll fold to avoid playing dominated postflop out of position.

Holding A5s from the small blind in a 3-handed game. Minni (button) opened to 300 with a short stack of 6,650. I'm getting 1.8:1 pot odds (450 in pot, 250 to call). A5s is a playable hand in short-handed play with good potential - ace-high plus flush draw capability. Calling allows me to see the flop in position relative to Minni's raise, and I can evaluate postflop play based on the board texture. Flash (big blind) still has to act. This is a marginal hand not strong enough to 3-bet profitably, but decent enough to play given the pot odds and hand potential in a 3-way game.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 5s, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni raised from the button and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a 700 pot), 2-5 offsuit has very poor playability and will be dominated by almost any two cards my opponents are holding. Playing out of position against two players with this hand is a losing proposition in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I'm holding As5h on a Kd2h9h board in a heads-up pot against Minni (short stack 6,650). My hand has moderate potential with a backdoor flush draw (9 hearts) and an ace, but ace-high is vulnerable. The king likely hits Minni's button-raising range. By checking first, I maintain flexibility: I can get a free card if Minni checks back, or evaluate her bet sizing if she bets. Given Minni's short stack commitment and the marginal nature of my holding, checking allows me to make an informed decision on her action rather than forcing the issue with As5o. If she bets, I can evaluate the pot odds and her likely holdings to decide on calling or folding.

I raised preflop with 5♣6♥ and I’m in position on a K♦ 2♥ 9♥ flop. I have no pair and only a remote backdoor heart possibility, so my hand’s raw equity is low, but as the preflop aggressor I can use positional advantage and fold equity to take the pot. Claude’s cold‑call range from the small blind is wide (Kx, pocket pairs, broadways, suited connectors), so some hands will fold to pressure. I’ll make a small–to–medium continuation bet (~40% pot) to deny equity to overcards and non‑made hands while keeping the pot controllable if called. Bet 300 into the 700 pot. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll check the turn and re‑evaluate, folding to significant aggression unless I improve (pair or clear runner‑runner help). If he check‑raises the flop or shows strong aggression, I’ll fold — my hand has too little showdown equity to continue.

Heads-up against short-stacked Minni (6350 remaining after her flop bet). Current pot is 1000, and I need to call 300, giving me 3.3:1 pot odds. I need approximately 23% equity to break even.

With As5h on Kd2h9h:
- I have a gutshot straight draw to broadway (4 outs with 3s)
- I have a backdoor flush draw with 9 remaining hearts
- Ace-high plays well in heads-up situations
- Combined, I estimate roughly 25-28% equity against Minni's likely range (which could include marginal kings, flush draws, or continuation bets)

This hand has multiple ways to improve, favorable pot odds, and Minni's short stack means she's committed. On the turn/river, if I hit an ace, make a flush draw, or hit broadway, I'll have strong equity to win. A call is justified by the pot odds and hand potential in this spot.

On the turn with As5h vs Kd2h9h8s, Minni has checked, indicating weakness. I'm heads-up against her short stack (6,350 remaining after her flop bet). My hand has developed significant equity: I have a gutshot straight draw to broadway (any 6 or 7 gives me a strong hand - 8 outs), plus an ace that plays well heads-up, plus a backdoor flush draw. With approximately 35-40% equity against Minni's wide button-raising range, a semi-bluff bet is justified. Betting 400 applies pressure while keeping Minni in a difficult spot - she's forced to either fold marginal holdings or commit further with a weakly checked range. Given her short stack and my draw strength, this bet accomplishes multiple objectives: wins pots with fold equity, builds the pot when I have equity, and positions me well for the river. This is consistent with aggressive play in short-handed situations.

I checked the turn as planned and Claude now bets 400 into the 1700 pot. My 5♣6♥ has no pair — my meaningful outs are four 7s for a straight and the remaining 5s/6s for pairs (about 10 raw outs, ~21% to improve). Calling 400 to win ~2100 requires ~19% equity, so this is roughly a break‑even/+EV call given raw outs. I’m in position, so calling preserves the ability to fold to heavy river aggression or to exploit weakness on the river by bluffing. A raise to 800 would be an overcommit with virtually no showdown value and is likely to be called or 3‑bet by better hands; folding surrenders too much equity given the pot odds. Plan: call 400, see the river, and fold to big river pressure unless I hit or can credibly bluff.

Checking on the river with As5h (ace-high) on Kd 2h 9h 8s Jd. My semi-bluff on the turn with multiple draws (gutshot to broadway + backdoor flush) didn't complete. The river didn't help my hand. By checking, I reach showdown with ace-high value while allowing Minni to make a decision from her short stack. If she checks, I win the pot. If she bets, the situation changes and I can evaluate. Given that I've already been aggressive on the turn, a check here is prudent with a marginal holding that didn't improve.

I have 5♣6♥ and never improved on K♦ 2♥ 9♥ 8♠ J♦. Claude checked the river after calling my flop c-bet and betting the turn — that line looks like pot control/weakness rather than a huge value hand. Checking back hands with zero showdown value hands me a near-certain loss at showdown; a small-to-medium river bluff can win the pot or at least fold out a portion of his marginal/capped range. The pot is 2,100; a bet of 700 (~1/3 pot) has a breakeven fold frequency of ~25% (700/(2,100+700)). Given his line, it’s reasonable he folds more than that frequency with hands like A-high, Qx, missed Broadway combos, or small pairs. Plan: bet 700 to apply pressure; if he calls I’ll muck; if he raises it shows strength and I fold. This is a low-variance, +EV bluff to convert position and fold equity into chips.

Folding ace-high on the river. While I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds requiring ~20% equity to break even, my As5h against Minni's aggressive line (check-raise flop → call turn → bet river) likely has only 15-18% equity. She's most likely holding a pair or better. With 90 hands remaining and a healthy stack, this is a marginal spot not worth the call. Her check-raise flop and turn call suggest genuine strength rather than a bluffing line, making this a clear fold.

3d 8c is one of the weakest hands in poker - a weak offsuit combination with no connectivity or high card strength. While the button is generally a favorable position for stealing blinds, this particular hand lacks the strength needed for a profitable raise against reasonably-stacked opponents (Flash has 20,750, Minni has 7,950). A fold preserves my 11,150 stack for better opportunities and is the mathematically correct play with such a poor holding. I'm at hand 61 of 150, so there will be plenty of better spots ahead.

9-7 offsuit is a marginal hand in the Small Blind. Since Claude has already folded on the Button, I am only facing Minni in the Big Blind. While I have a significant chip lead, 9-7o is generally too weak to raise profitably into a sticky opponent, and calling often leads to playing a weak hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my stack advantage.

I am on the button with 5s 2h, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, opening with such a low-equity hand is unnecessary and mathematically unsound. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my stack depth.

I’m in the small blind with 3♠4♥ heads-up vs the big blind. The pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call (100 total committed). This is a weak, low-card offsuit hand and not worth open-raising out of position into a single competent opponent, but the price to see a flop is excellent. I’ll complete to see a cheap flop, keep the pot controllable, and re-evaluate on the flop — folding to heavy aggression if I miss, or continuing if I pick up equity or a good read.

I'm in the Big Blind with KdJh against Minni's small blind limp-call. This is a favorable spot for aggression. Minni's call of 50 from the SB suggests a weak hand - likely she's either looking to get lucky or testing with a marginal holding. KJ is a strong hand in heads-up/near-heads-up play and should be played aggressively here. A raise to 300 (3x the current bet) puts meaningful pressure on Minni without overcommitting my stack. She either folds (winning the pot immediately) or calls with a likely weaker hand where I have equity. This type of positional aggression in the Big Blind against a limper is a fundamental high-level poker play. Given the tournament stage (hand 62 of 150) and healthy stack sizes, this is the profitable long-term decision.

I already completed to 100 intending to see a cheap flop, but Claude just raised to 300 which puts 400 in the pot and requires me to call 200 (I’d be committing 300 total). Calling 200 into a 400 pot means I need ~33% equity to break even, and 3♠4♥ OOP has very poor equity against a typical BB raising range and is frequently dominated. I’m out of position and facing a competent/aggressive opponent from prior hands, so this is not a profitable spot to gamble or to peel with a marginal hand. A raise is nonsense with garbage. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

3-handed on the button with K♦6♦ and ~80bb effective. This is a profitable spot to open: wide button range, good position and K6s has decent playability (flush/KT potential and can hit top pair). Pot is 150 and calling 100 is passive — min-raising to 200 is too small and gives opponents better odds. I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB (300) to seize the initiative, maximize fold equity and deny cheap flops. If I face a large 3-bet from either player I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll play postflop aggressively when the board fits my range and pot-control or float when it doesn’t.

6s2s is the second-worst starting hand in poker (only 7s2o is worse). While Minni is short-stacked at 7700 and likely raising a wide range from the button, I have no hand equity here. The pot odds don't justify playing a hopeless hand - I'd need to call 250 into a 450 pot (1.8:1) with a hand that has minimal playability. With 11200 chips and 100 hands remaining, I can afford to fold marginal/unplayable hands. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding 95o. Minni raised from the button, and Claude folded. 95o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand against an active player. Although I have a significant chip lead and the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, 95o realizes its equity poorly. There is no need to play a weak hand out of position here.

Playing A2o from the button with a short-stacked big blind (Minni at 8,050 chips). While A2 is nominally weak, button position provides significant fold equity advantages. Minni is vulnerable with a small stack, and raising here accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) wins dead money immediately in many cases, (2) applies pressure to Minni who may be forced to fold a playable hand, (3) isolates against Flash with any action. A 250 raise (2.5x BB) is standard aggression from the button and is sized appropriately to pressure shorter stacks while managing my own chip preservation. This is a profitable exploitation of position and Minni's stack vulnerability in a 3-handed game.

I have Ace-Five suited (Ad 5d) in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Claude raised from the Button, a position where players typically open with a wide range of hands. Rather than calling and playing out of position, I will 3-bet (raise) to 850 to take the initiative, put pressure on the opener, and potentially win the pot immediately or isolate Claude.

I’m on the big blind with 4♣6♦ (weak, unsuited). Claude opened to 250 and Flash 3-bet to 850 — I’m facing a 750 call into a 1,200 pot (calling would require ~38.5% equity). 4-6 offsuit is dominated by a 3‑bettor’s range and performs poorly OOP against two opponents. A 4‑bet bluff to 1,450+ would commit too many chips with little fold equity against these stack sizes and player profiles. Folding preserves my 8k+ stack to pick better spots postflop or against single opponents.

Facing Flash's 3-bet from the small blind after Minni folded. While my original button raise was designed to exploit position and Minni's short stack, the dynamics have shifted significantly now that Flash has 3-bet to 850. A2o is a marginal hand that plays poorly postflop out of position against Flash's aggressive range. Key considerations: (1) I'm out of position heads-up against Flash, (2) A2o has weak hand strength (~40% equity against a reasonable 3-bet range), (3) I'm not getting correct odds to call (need 33% equity but have ~40%, which is marginal), (4) Flash has a stack advantage (19,800 vs 10,950), limiting my fold equity, (5) shoving 4-bet would commit me with a weak hand against a player who just showed aggression and confidence. In 3-handed poker, wider ranges mean 3-bets are more frequent, but A2o still doesn't have sufficient equity to warrant building a pot. Folding preserves my chip stack and avoids playing a weak hand out of position in an unfavorable matchup.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. While I have a significant chip lead, this hand is too weak to open with, especially since both Minni and Claude have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my stack advantage.

I have 9♦2♦ (9h2d) in the small blind, heads-up vs the big blind. This is a very weak, unsuited hand and I don’t want to build a big pot OOP by raising. However the price to see a flop is tiny: pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more (25% break-even). Versus a wide BB defending range my hand has enough raw equity to justify a cheap call. Plan: call to see the flop cheaply, fold to significant continuation aggression unless I pair or pick up a credible draw. I avoid a raise because Claude has shown a tendency to defend/call preflop and I’d be out of position with a junk hand.

I'm in the big blind with 5-9 offsuit, a marginal hand. Minni called to complete her small blind. Flash folded from the button, so I'm heads-up against Minni. With such a weak hand, checking is the prudent play rather than raising - this allows me to see the flop efficiently while preserving my stack. My hand has some potential with straight possibilities (any 6, 7, or 8 makes me a straight draw). I'll evaluate on the flop based on texture and Minni's action. At 10850 chips deep in a 150-hand tournament, I should avoid unnecessary aggression with weak holdings.

I called preflop cheaply with 9♣2♦ (9♥2♦) to see a flop, but this flop (K♦ J♣ 4♥) completely misses my hand — no pair, no straight or real flush draw. I’m out of position versus Claude and the pot is small (200). Betting here would bloat the pot with a very weak hand and invite raises or calls from better pairs (K/ J) and strong turn plays. Best line is pot control: check and concede to a reasonable c-bet. If Claude checks back I get a free turn card; if he leads, I’ll fold unless I pick up a pair or a clear multi-street draw. Therefore: check.

With 5-9 offsuit on a K-J-4 board, I have no made hand and no meaningful draws. Minni checked, showing weakness, but I have zero equity here. Betting would be a marginal bluff without fold equity justification. Checking allows me to see the turn card cheaply - any 3, 6, 7, or 8 would give me a straight draw. Preserving my 10,850 chip stack is crucial in a 150-hand tournament when I'm in the big blind with a weak hand.

Check. I have 9♥2♦ — no pair and no meaningful draw on K♦ J♣ 4♥ → A♣. I’m out of position and the pot is small (200) relative to my stack, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a pure bluff that can be raised or called by better hands (A‑x, K‑x, J‑x, or even a turned Ace). Claude checked the flop, showing weakness or a slow‑play; checking to the river preserves pot control and lets me avoid committing chips with virtually no equity. Plan: check and fold to significant aggression on this street; consider bluffing only on a very favorable river card or folding to continued pressure.

I'm in the big blind with 5-9 offsuit on a K-J-4-A board. This is a completely whiffed hand with zero equity and zero draws - no straight possibilities exist with these cards on this board. Minni has checked the flop and turn, indicating weakness herself. The pot is minimal at 200 chips, and I have nothing to gain by betting here. Betting would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity prospects, especially since Minni could easily have a hand like a pair or ace-high. With 10,850 chips in a 150-hand tournament, I should preserve my stack by seeing the river for free. A check allows me the cheapest possible way to complete this hand while minimizing variance with a marginal holding.

I have 9♥2♦ — no pair and absolutely no meaningful draw on A K Q J 4. Both streets were checked, so the pot is small (200) and my only realistic chance to win is a river bluff. I’ll make a small, half‑pot bluff: bet 100. A 100 bet only needs ~33% fold frequency to be +EV (break‑even = 100/(200+100) ≈ 33%), which is attainable given Claude checked flop and turn (shows weakness or a marginal holding). A larger bet would demand much higher fold equity and risks more when called. If Claude folds I scoop 200; if he calls I can muck and preserve most of my stack — good risk‑reward for this situation.

This is the river with a final board of 4h-Jc-Kd-Ac-Qs. I hold 5s-9c, which is completely whiffed - no pair, no straight, no draw whatsoever. My best possible hand is ace-high, which loses to any pair or better. Minni checked the flop and turn, then raised to 100 on the river. While the pot odds (4:1) are mathematically attractive, my hand has virtually zero showdown value against most reasonable holdings. In a 150-hand tournament where stack preservation matters significantly, folding a garbage hand on the river to a raise is the correct play. This keeps my 10,850-chip stack intact for higher-equity situations where I can apply fold pressure with better cards.

I'm on the button with Q♣8♠ (Q8 offsuit) in a 3-way pot. In 3-handed play my opening range should be wide; Q8o is marginal but very playable in position and is a good candidate to steal the blinds. I want to take the initiative and use a standard open size that fits my table image (3x BB / common raises seen), so I'll raise to 300 total. If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold (Q8o is often dominated); if called I can use position to see flops cheaply and continue on favorable textures.

8-Jack offsuit is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant involvement from the small blind facing a button raise. Key factors: (1) I'm out of position with Flash (big blind, 20.9k stack) still to act - he could 3-bet and put me in an awkward spot with only 10.8k chips; (2) 8Jo has limited equity against Minni's wide button-raising range and even less if Flash gets involved; (3) My stack is healthy enough to be selective - I should fold weak broadway cards in tough positions and wait for premium hands or better opportunities; (4) Minni's short stack (7.85k) means she may be playing marginal hands, but that doesn't improve my hand's playability out of position. The disciplined fold preserves chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Minni has raised from the button. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to defend with such a weak range. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With 2s-Jd on the button, I have a very weak hand. While my position is strong, the hand itself has minimal value and poor playability. Looking at the tournament status, I have a healthy stack of 10800 and should be selective with my entries. Minni is short-stacked at 8200, which suggests aggressive play is likely from the blinds. This isn't a hand worth defending or raising with - it plays poorly both in and out of position post-flop, and there's no value in calling 100 chips just to see a flop with a marginal hand. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with QhTs. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. QTo is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x open.

I’m in the big blind with 8♦6♦ facing a 3x SB open to 300. This is a suited, connected hand with good playability postflop and with stacks deep enough (I have 8,200 vs his 20,600) to realize implied odds. The pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more (total commitment 300), which gives ~33% break-even equity — reasonable for 8♦6♦ vs a typical SB opening range. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to 3-bet light; I’ll call and see a flop, plan to continue with pairs/draws and be disciplined to fold to heavy aggression when I miss.

On this 3h-9d-7d flop, I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) and a backdoor straight draw. Minni, in the Big Blind, is likely to have a wide range of hands that hit this board, including various pairs, straight draws, and flush draws. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and pressure. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced sizing that can get folds from weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable if I am called or raised.

I have 8♦6♦ on 3♥ 9♦ 7♦ — that’s two diamonds in hand with two diamonds on board (four to a flush) plus an open-ended straight draw (5 or 10 completes).
