Unique outs = 15 (9 diamonds + 6 non-diamond 5s/10s) → ~31.9% to hit the turn and ~54% to hit by the river. Pot is 900 and Flash’s bet is 300, so my break-even equity to call is only 25% (300/(900+300)). With strong equity and a small c-bet sizing, flat-calling out of position is the correct line: it keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity, and avoids committing more chips to a potentially stronger made hand. I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn — continue aggressively if I hit (flush/straight) or if he shows weakness; fold or proceed cautiously if he applies heavy pressure on a blank turn.

On the turn, the Queen of diamonds is a bit of a double-edged sword. It gives me top pair (Queens), but it also completes a potential diamond flush and some straight draws. Since I am first to act, I have to decide whether to check-call or continue betting for value. Given that I've improved my hand significantly, I want to get value from hands like mid-pairs (9x, 7x) or straight draws (J-10, 8-6) that might still be in Minni's range. A bet of 600 (half the pot) is a standard continuation that allows me to extract value while keeping the pot at a manageable size if she has completed a flush.

Turn: Q♦ completes my flush — I have a five-card diamond flush with 8♦6♦ on 3♥ 9♦ 7♦ Q♦. The only hands that currently beat me are rare two-diamond hole cards that contain a diamond higher than my 8 (A♦x♦, K♦x♦, J♦x♦, 10♦x♦). Flash’s line (preflop open, flop c-bet, turn small-ish barrel of 600 into a 1800 pot) is consistent with both bluffs and value hands, and his sizing gives me very good pot odds to call (600 / (1800+600) = 25%). I don’t want to inflate the pot and run into the small subset of two-diamond combos that dominate me, but I do want to keep worse hands and bluffs in for value. Calling is the highest EV, lowest-risk line here: it realizes my equity, keeps weaker made hands and bluffs in the pot, and leaves room to re-evaluate the river. Plan: call the 600 and re-evaluate on the river — extract more value if he shows weakness or size down if he turns out to have a superior diamond.

On the river, the King of Clubs is a scare card. While I hit top pair on the turn, the King now makes my hand second pair. This board is quite coordinated with a possible diamond flush and a straight (J-10). Minni has called two streets, suggesting she has a piece of the board or a draw. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, as most 9x or 7x hands will fold, and I'll only be called by a King, a Queen with a better kicker, a straight, or a flush. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize losses against hands that improved on the river or already had me beat.

I have 8♦6♦ on 3♥ 9♦ 7♦ Q♦ K♣ — I made a Q-high diamond flush on the turn. Only hands that can beat me are other two-card diamond combos that produce a better flush (primarily A♦x♦, K♦x♦ and some combinations with high diamonds that outrank my 8♦). Flash’s line (open pre, c-bet flop, small turn barrel, then checking the river) looks like a range that contains both value hands and missed/medium hands; his river check is more indicative of weakness/spot-control than a clear value shove. Pot = 2400 and I’m facing a checked pot, so a modest value bet extracts from one-pair/second-pair hands and missed rivers while keeping the risk vs the very small number of dominating two-diamond combos limited. I’ll bet small-to-medium for value (≈1/3–1/2 pot). Bet 800 chips: it’s big enough to get called by worse hands, but small enough to avoid bloating the pot if I’m up against a superior diamond — if he raises large, I’ll assume a better flush and fold.

The river King of Clubs is an overcard to my pair of Queens and the board is coordinated with potential flushes and a completed straight (J-10). However, Minni's bet of 800 into a 2400 pot gives me 4:1 pot odds, requiring me to be correct only 20% of the time to break even. Minni's small sizing could indicate a thin value bet with a weaker Queen, a blocking bet with a middle pair, or a missed draw. Given the excellent price and the strength of a pair of Queens in a blind-vs-blind battle, folding would be too tight. I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with J5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand even for short-handed play. While I am the chip leader, there is no need to enter the pot with a sub-optimal hand, especially since Minni and Claude have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and play post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better opening opportunity.

Heads-up from the small blind with 8♦7♥ and deep stacks (~100bb). Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see a flop, but 87o is a playable, connected hand that gains value by taking the initiative. I want to deny the big blind correct odds, build the pot when I hit, and maintain fold equity — so I open-raise to the minimum standard (200 total). Plan: c-bet favorable flops and apply pressure; pot-control or check behind on A/K heavy boards. If Claude 3-bets large I will fold this hand to preserve my stack.

Facing a min-raise from the Small Blind with K4o in the Big Blind. Several factors support calling: (1) Excellent pot odds of 3:1 - I need only ~25% equity to call profitably; (2) The min-raise from Minni suggests weakness rather than strength, making this a vulnerable spot to attack postflop; (3) Heads-up dynamics allow for wider calling ranges, and K-high has decent showdown value against a min-raising range; (4) My out-of-position disadvantage is mitigated by the weak raise signal. Raising K4o would be too aggressive with marginal hand strength, but folding to 3:1 odds against a suspicious min-raise would be overly cautious. I'll take a flop and apply postflop skills to navigate the hand from there.

Check. The flop 5♦-4♥-4♦ is a low, paired board that actually helps the big blind's calling range (pocket pairs, 4x, 5x, and many hands that called my SB raise). I'm out of position and I don't have a made hand — only a weak backdoor diamond possibility and a gutshot/paired-card outs (a 6 to complete 4-5-6-7-8 straight and pairing my 7 or 8). Roughly ~15–22% equity to improve by the river. Fold equity for a c-bet here is thin and barreling OOP into a caller-heavy range with a marginal hand is costly. I want to control the pot and realize my equity, so I check to see a turn. Plan: if Claude checks back I get a free turn; if he fires a small bet (roughly ≤1/2 pot) I can call to see my outs, but I will fold to large overbets or raising aggression unless I improve (pair/straight/flush) or get a clear read of weakness.

Betting trip fours on a dry board against an opponent showing weakness. Minni's pre-flop min-raise and flop check indicate a likely weak hand. A 225-chip bet extracts value from weaker holdings while maintaining enough incentive for her to continue. The pot of 400 makes this a reasonable c-bet sizing from BB. Three of a kind is too strong to check, and this size punishes her weak line while keeping the hand playable.

I checked the flop to control the pot OOP. Claude’s small bet (225 into 625 ≈36% pot) gives me ~26.5% break-even pot odds (call 225 to win ~850). My 8♦7♥ has a gutshot to a straight (four 6s), pair outs (three 7s + three 8s) and a small backdoor diamond chance — roughly ~10 clean outs ⇒ ~38% to hit by the river (even if some of those results can be second-best). Raising OOP with this marginal holding risks bloating the pot against a calling-heavy BB range and reduces my ability to fold if I miss. I’ll call 225 to see the turn, realize my equity, and then fold to large turn aggression if I don’t improve (or continue/apply pressure if I do).

I just paired my 7 on the turn (I hold 8♦7♥; board 5♦-4♥-4♦-7♣). This is a marginal, middle pair with an 8 kicker and I'm out of position. Claude’s small flop c-bet into the ~400 pot represents a wide range that includes trips (44), single pair (5x, 6x), and a lot of calling hands; many of those hands still beat me (notably trips or a turned/set) or can check-raise/bluff on later streets. Betting OOP here risks bloating the pot and getting exploited by a check-raise or by hands that already have me dominated; the thin value I’d extract from 5x/6x/Ax isn’t worth that risk. I want to control the pot and realize equity, so I check the turn and plan to evaluate the river. If Claude bets a small-to-medium size on the river I will call for value/protection; I will fold to large aggression unless I improve. If he checks back I can consider a small value bet on the river or a showdown depending on the card.

Betting trip fours on the turn after Minni checks twice shows weakness. With the 7♣ turn card and her passive line (min-raise preflop, flop check-call, turn check), she likely has a weak/medium hand like a small pair, ace-high, or busted draw. A 425-chip turn bet extracts maximum value from her weaker holdings while remaining reasonable relative to the 850-chip pot. Trip fours with king kicker is premium and should be played for value against her checking range. This sizing keeps her in the hand while building the pot with my strong equity advantage.

Turn recap & hand strength: I hold 8♦7♥ and the turn is 5♦-4♥-4♦-7♣ — that gives me two pair (7s and 4s). Pot math and range: the pot is 1,275 and Claude bet 425, so calling 425 requires ~25% equity to break even. Claude called preflop and fired a small flop bet then a larger turn bet — his line includes both value hands (4x trips, pocket 5s, pocket 7s/full houses, overpairs) and a fair number of c-bets/floaters. Assessment: two pair is good versus a lot of his continuing range (overcards, single pairs, floats) and has enough equity vs that range to justify a call. Raising OOP here bloats the pot, invites a shove from the few hands that already crush me (trips/full houses/quads), and sacrifices my ability to fold cheaply if I run into strength on the river. Plan: call 425 to realize equity and keep the pot manageable; re-evaluate on the river. If the river is a blank I will look to get value from weaker hands; if the river pairs to 4/5/7 or he shows heavy aggression I will respect that line and fold as appropriate.

I hold 8♦7♥; the final board is 5♦-4♥-4♦-7♣-2♠. I’ve turned two pair (7s & 4s) but I’m out of position and a fair chunk of Claude’s continuing range (any 4, 55, 44, sometimes 77+) beats me. Claude called pre, barreled the flop and turn — a line that contains both value (sets/full houses/pocket pairs that improved) and a lot of floats/one-pair hands (A5, K5, small paired hands, missed draws). The river 2♠ is a blank relative to my hand, so I should try to extract thin value from single-pair hands and floats while avoiding bloating the pot against the small subset of hands that crush me.

Plan: make a small-to-medium value bet (~40% pot) to get called by worse one-pair hands and river floats but discourage big check-raises from trips/full houses. If Claude calls I expect to be ahead often; if he check-raises very large or jams, I’ll fold (too many combos beat me). Bet 700 into the 1,700 pot to price in calls and keep the hand manageable.

Calling with trip fours and King kicker on the river. Minni's weak line (min-raise pre-flop, check-flop, check-turn) followed by a river raise suggests either a delayed bluff or an attempt to trap with a full house. However, the probability she has exactly 55 or 77 (the only hands that beat me) is low given the passive action. With 3.43:1 pot odds requiring only 22.6% equity and an estimated 85%+ win rate, this is a profitable call. The river raise appears to be either a bluff or an overplay of a weaker made hand.

Raise to 300 (3x BB). On the button with J9o in a 3-handed game this is a standard open: good position, a playable holding, and solid fold equity versus the blinds. A 3x sizing keeps the pot manageable with ~87bb effective stacks and lets me play the flop in position. Calling concedes fold equity and invites multiway pots with a marginal hand, while folding is too nitty.

I'm holding QQ, a premium pocket pair, in the small blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the button with 8,450 chips remaining. With only 250 to call and a strong hand, I should be playing aggressively here. The goal is to build the pot with my strong hand while applying pressure before the big blind acts. A raise to 800 total (500 more on top of Minni's 300 raise) accomplishes this - it's substantive enough to narrow the playing field and extract value, while not overcommitting given Stack sizes. QQ has excellent win rate against most ranges here, and I want to play for value early in the tournament while maintaining a healthy chip stack.

I am holding King of Spades and Eight of Diamonds in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. K8 offsuit is not a strong enough hand to cold-call a 3-bet, especially when I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two active opponents. Claude's 3-bet indicates a strong range or at least a polarized aggressive strategy that dominates my current hand. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with Jh9c and Claude (SB) 3-bet to 800. In a 3-handed game my position is a big advantage and J9o is worth seeing a flop against a likely wide SB 3-bet range. The pot is ~1200 and I need 500 more to call — that’s about 500/(1200+500) ≈ 29% pot odds, a reasonable price to realize equity and use position post‑flop. A minimum 4‑bet to 1300 here is awkward: it bloats the pot with a hand that’s often dominated and doesn’t block enough of the villain’s value hands. I’ll call (total 800) and play the flop in position; fold to a large 4‑bet shove, and on the flop continue only with clear equity (pair/top pair, strong draws) or take a controlled check‑fold line versus heavy aggression.

QQ is an overpair to the 6-6-2 flop, making this a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni's calling range from the button likely includes AK, AQ, smaller pairs, and broadways - hands I'm ahead of. With his relatively short stack (7,150), I should extract value with a solid continuation bet. A 700 bet applies appropriate pressure (building the pot to 2,400) while keeping him engaged with drawing hands or weaker holdings. This bet size balances value extraction with keeping him in the hand on a dry board where I'm almost certainly ahead.

Claude 3‑bet preflop and is barreling a dry, paired flop (6d 6h 2c) for 700 into a 2400 pot. I hold Jh9c — no pair and no meaningful draw (only runner‑runner hearts). Calling costs ~700/(2400+700) ≈ 22.6% of the pot, but my raw equity versus a 3‑better’s c‑bet range (overpairs, sets, strong Ax, some bluffs) is well below that. A raise would be a pure bluff without good blockers and is likely to be called or get shoved on. I’ll fold and preserve chips for a better spot rather than invest more with essentially no equity.

Raising to 300 with pocket sixes on the button. This is a strong position to apply pressure on two opponents, particularly the short-stacked big blind. A standard 3x raise applies aggression, gives me the initiative, and sets up for continuation play. Pocket pairs have good equity against most ranges and I want to keep the pot manageable while defining opponent ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 7s, a weak suited gapper. Claude has raised from the Button, and playing this hand out of position against him is generally unprofitable. Given the strength of my hand and my disadvantageous position for the remainder of the street, folding is the most disciplined play.

7♦4♣ is a clear fold. It’s a weak, offsuit, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated by a button’s opening range (pairs, Axs, broadways). I’m out of position postflop and the hand has very poor playability. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires ~31% equity (200/(450+200)≈30.8%), and 7-4o does not have sufficient equity versus a standard open to justify defending. 3-betting as a bluff with this garbage is unnecessary risk with ~78BB effective — better to preserve the stack for a much stronger spot.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game on the button. It lacks the high-card value or connectivity to justify an open-raise. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up vs the big blind (Button folded). I have pocket eights in the small blind — a strong, playable pair that wants to take the initiative preflop rather than limp and play out of position. With 50 to call and a pot of 150, a standard 3xBB sizing (raise to 300 total) extracts value from worse hands and narrows the field, while leaving room to fold or call a 3-bet without committing my stack. Effective stacks are deep (7800 vs 13300), so this sizing balances value and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 and re-evaluate on a 3-bet or postflop action.

I'm in the Big Blind with 4h Ts, one of the weakest possible hands in poker - unpaired, unconnected, low cards. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind, showing strength. With such a poor hand, I have no realistic way to win a contested pot, and folding now preserves my 13,300 stack for better spots. My 100 BB is already committed, and losing an additional 200 to call with this trash hand would be poor bankroll management. The pot odds don't justify playing this hand out of position against a raiser. I should fold and wait for more favorable opportunities.

Button facing SB(Claude) and BB(Flash) with 4♣7♦ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Pot is 150, to call 100, min-raise 200. Although the button is a good stealing position, this holding has very poor postflop playability and is easily dominated or crushed multiway. A limp (call 100) often creates a bloated multiway pot where my equity is low; a small open invites calls or a 3-bet from opponents who have shown willingness to defend/raise. With a healthy stack and many hands left, I avoid marginal, high-variance spots and conserve chips for better opportunities. Fold.

8-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker and should be folded in virtually all situations. Despite having positional advantage as the small blind aggressor (now that Minni folded on the button), the intrinsic weakness of this hand makes it unprofitable to contest. Flash in the BB will have a much wider range, and I'm unlikely to win this pot either pre-flop or post-flop with such a weak holding. At this stage of the tournament (hand 72 of 150) with a relatively even chip stack (13,250 vs 18,650), preserving chips for premium situations is the correct strategy. I'll fold and wait for better spots.

I'm on the button with J-4 offsuit (both hearts), facing 100 to call with blinds at 50/100. While button position is valuable, J-4 is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop against multiple opponents. 

Looking at the stack dynamics: I have 13,250, Flash has 18,750, and Minni has 7,850. We're only at hand 73 of 150, so there's sufficient time to find premium hands. J-4 doesn't meet the threshold for play here - it lacks both high card strength and meaningful drawing potential (it's not even a suited connector).

From the button, I should be playing stronger hands. This is a clear fold situation.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 5s, a very weak hand. Claude has folded, leaving only me and Minni in the Big Blind. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, 65o performs poorly out of position, especially against an opponent like Minni who has shown a tendency to be sticky and aggressive in previous hands (e.g., Hand #51, Hand #67). Given my chip lead, there is no need to play a marginal hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined move here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K8o is a standard opening hand from the Button in a 3-handed game. With the chip lead, I want to apply pressure to the shorter stacks in the blinds. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with the table's previous play and allows me to take control of the pot early.

Fold. A♣4♠ is a marginal offsuit ace and I'm out of position to a button open with the big blind still to act. The pot is 450 and it costs 250 to continue; calling would leave me OOP to the raiser (and often the big blind), making post‑flop play difficult and my equity against two ranges is below what the price requires. A tiny 3-bet to the min (500) won't reliably isolate and a larger 3-bet with A4o is thin and could get 4-bet. With ~79 BBs I can wait for a much better, positionally sound spot — folding preserves my stack and avoids a dominated/marginal situation.

I'm in the big blind with T6o (Ten-Six offsuit of diamonds), facing a button raise from Flash to 300. This is a weak, unconnected hand that lacks strong equity. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (450 pot, 200 to call = 2.25:1), my hand equity against Flash's button raise range is likely only 25-30%, making this call unprofitable long-term. Being out of position compounds the problem. With 76 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy 13,150 stack, I should be selective and fold weak hands from the BB when facing aggression from late position. This is a clear fold.

I’m on the button with Js7s (suited J7). With ~79 BB effective and two players in the blinds, this is a speculative but positionally strong hand — good for stealing the blinds and for multiway-implied odds when I hit. The table standard has seen 3x attempts from the button, so opening to 300 (3x) makes sense: it maximizes fold equity, often isolates to one defender, and lets me play postflop with initiative. Calling 100 leaves me in frequent multiway spots where J7s loses equity; a min-raise to 200 is too small and invites more calls/3-bets. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and realize spade/straight equity; fold to large/committing 3-bets (or re-evaluate if a small sizing gives correct pot odds), and otherwise play aggressively in position.

Calling with JT from small blind. Minni appears short-stacked and raising wide from button (likely steal attempt). JT has reasonable equity against Minni's range and plays well postflop with position and straight potential. Pot odds (1.8:1) are favorable. My medium stack allows controlled play on subsequent streets.

I am in the Big Blind with Ah 5d. Minni (Button) raised to 300 and Claude (Small Blind) called. The pot is 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds, which is excellent for a hand like Ace-five offsuit, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. My hand has decent equity against the likely ranges of both players, and folding would be too tight given the price. Raising (3-betting) is risky against two opponents with a weak kicker, so calling to see a flop is the most balanced and profitable play.

Betting 450 with JTd on 7-9-Q board. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) with broadway cards and reasonable equity. Being out of position with two opponents, taking the initiative with aggression is crucial. Minni's short stack and loose raising pattern from button makes him a good fold candidate. Flash is more solid but will struggle with most hands here. This bet amount (~50% pot) applies sufficient pressure while maintaining flexibility on later streets. JT has both immediate equity and strong draw potential against likely ranges.

On a coordinated flop of 7d-9c-Qc, I have completely missed with Ace-five offsuit. Claude has led out into two players (myself and the pre-flop raiser), which often indicates at least a pair or a strong draw on this type of board. With no pair, no draws, and another player (Minni) still to act behind me, there is no reason to continue. The pot odds are 3:1, but my equity against a leading range is far below the required 25%. Folding is the only disciplined option.

I have J♠7♠ — bottom pair (7s) with a decent Jack kicker, acting in position after Claude’s 450 into a 1350 pot. Calling 450 to win ~1800 requires only ~25% equity, so this is a cheap continue. Claude called preflop and is c-betting a wide range that contains many hands I beat (bluffs, worse pairs, some Kx/Ax and low pairs that will fold on later pressure) as well as hands that currently beat me (Qx, 9x, bigger pairs). In position I can control the pot size, see a turn card, and fold to large turn aggression if necessary or extract value from worse hands. Raising to 900 is too thin with only bottom pair — it will get called by better hands and unnecessarily bloats the pot. Plan: call now, reevaluate on the turn (be cautious to clubs/overcards and continue for value or fold to heavy action).

Continuing aggression on the turn with my OESD (8 outs to the nuts). Minni's short stack (7,200) and flop call suggest a medium-strength hand, not a monster. His turn check shows weakness. A 900 bet applies strong pressure on this vulnerable short-stacked opponent while I maintain excellent equity with my straight draw. This sizing builds the pot proportionally and forces a critical decision from Minni given his remaining chips. My bet range credibly includes both strong hands and premium draws like JT, making this a sound continuation that maximizes value and fold equity.

I have J♠7♠ — bottom pair with a decent kicker, in position. The turn A♣ is a scary card: it’s an overcard to my pair and it brings a three-club board (so two-club combos in villain’s hand now have a made flush). Claude (SB) is a caller preflop who c-bet the flop and now fires a small turn bet of 900 into 2700. That sizing gives me ~25% pot odds to call (900/(2700+900)=25%). Folding here would be too nitty given the cheap price; raising is too thin — it bloats the pot and will be called by A/Q/flush/sets that already beat me. I’ll call the 900 to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and use position to fold to heavy river action or extract value if an okay river appears or villain slows down.

River check with missed straight draw. I have zero showdown value (Ace-high J-T is the worst hand essentially). Minni has called aggressive action on both flop and turn, indicating he has a made hand or was semi-bluffing. Betting as a bluff is too risky given he's already committed significant chips and demonstrated strength. Checking allows us to go to showdown at minimal additional cost. If Minni somehow has a missed draw or bluff, he'll likely check back. This is a prudent line to minimize further losses when my hand has no equity.

Pot is 3600, my stack ~6300. I have J♠7♠ — bottom pair (7s) on 7♦ 9♣ Q♣ A♣ 2♠. Claude called preflop and led flop+turn (900 into the pot) and now checks the river. His line is consistent with a range that includes many hands that beat me (A×, Q×, 9×, or a club flush) and some bluffs. Very few worse hands will pay off a river bet, so betting is thin EV and raising would just get me called/raised by better hands or fold out only the hands I already beat. Checking keeps the pot under control, preserves most of my stack, and lets me go to showdown for my marginal pair. I check.

Raising to 300 from the button with Ts9d. This is a standard aggressive raise (3x BB) that leverages my positional advantage and the quality of my hand. The Ten-Nine connector can make straights and has decent equity potential. I'm building the pot in position with the best seat at the table, which is a core profitable poker strategy. Minni is short-stacked, so they may fold; Flash's response will give me valuable information.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh 6h. The Button (Claude) has raised to 300. While Qh 6h is a suited hand, it is quite weak to play out of position against a Button open. Calling would put me in a difficult spot for the rest of the hand, especially with Minni in the Big Blind still to act. Given my healthy stack lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands in poor position. Folding is the most prudent play.

QcJh (QJo) in the big blind vs a standard BTN open to 300 is a defendable, playable hand but not strong enough to commit with a big 3-bet. A min 3-bet to 500 is too small to generate reliable fold equity and a larger 3-bet would overcommit OOP with a marginal holding. I'll call to 300 (200 more) to see a flop cheaply, keep my range balanced, and evaluate board texture — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy pressure when missed.

Check. With QcJh on Kd‑8d‑6d I completely missed: no pair and no diamond on a three‑diamond board. Claude’s button opener includes lots of diamonds and Kx, so the flop heavily favors his range. Betting OOP here would be an ambitious bluff into a range that has lots of safe calls and strong hands — I’d be risking a big chunk of my stack with very poor equity. I’ll check to control the pot (650) and evaluate his action. Plan: fold to medium/large bet (>= ~1/2 pot), consider calling a very small probe (<= ~1/3 pot) to see a turn, and re‑assess if he checks back (look for a turn to bluff or realize equity).

Minni checked after calling my pre-flop raise, indicating weakness. I have an open-ended straight draw (any 7) plus overcards (T and 9), giving me meaningful equity and multiple outs. Standard continuation bet sizing of ~350 applies pressure, wins the pot immediately if Minni folds (likely given their short stack), and maintains my aggressive image from the pre-flop raise. This is +EV with either the best hand now or strong drawing potential.

I completely missed this three‑diamond flop with QcJh (no pair, no diamond). Claude’s button range contains a lot of diamonds and Kx, so the board heavily favors his range. His 350 into a 1000 pot (~35%) is a small probe — borderline but cheap enough to see a turn. Raising OOP with a marginal hand is too risky and folding outright gives up too much equity for the price. I’ll call to see a turn cheaply and keep pot control. Plan: fold to a large turn barrel (≈≥1/2 pot or a raise); if he checks or shows weakness on the turn I can consider a bluff or delayed aggression, but won’t commit without improving.

Check. The turn Jack gave me a marginal pair (JhQc = pair of Jacks with a Q kicker) but the board (Kd‑8d‑6d‑Js) remains very hostile — three diamonds on the flop and a King make Claude’s button range (preflop opener + flop c‑bet) likely full of diamonds, Kx and the occasional set. Betting OOP here would be thin: I get called by diamonds and dominated Kx hands and only fold out pure air; raising is reckless with just one pair. I’ll check to control the pot (pot=1350, my stack 9250), see Claude’s action, and preserve fold equity. Plan: call a small probe (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot ≈ ≤400) to realize showdown equity, fold to a large bet or raise (≈≥1/2 pot ≈≥675), and if he checks back re-evaluate the river for a small value bet or a delayed bluff depending on the runout.

Minni has checked the turn, indicating weakness or a marginal holding. I'm in position with a strong semi-bluff: I hold an open-ended straight draw (any Q or 7 makes a straight - 8 potential outs), plus additional outs with any T or 9 for overcards. The turn card (Js) appears to have missed Minni based on their check. With a short-stacked opponent (9250 chips) who just checked to me in position, betting is the profitable play for multiple reasons: (1) I can win the pot immediately if they fold, (2) if called, I have legitimate drawing equity to improve on the river, and (3) my aggressive image from the pre-flop raise and flop c-bet supports a turn bet. Sizing at 650 (~50% of pot) is appropriately aggressive without overcommitting my stack. This is standard +EV poker in position against weakness.

I hold JhQc on Kd‑8d‑6d‑Js — I improved to a single Jack (no diamond) but the board and action strongly favor Claude. He opened BTN, c‑bet a small probe on the three‑diamond flop and now barrels 650 on the turn into the ~1350 pot; that sizing sequence is consistent with Kx, sets or diamond‑heavy value combos that dominate my hand. Calling 650 into a 2000 pot (final pot 2650) requires ~24–25% equity; versus Claude’s likely value/heavy‑diamond range my true equity is well under that. There’s also the real risk of a raise to 1300 which would commit me out of position with a marginal one‑pair hand and poor blockers. I’ll fold and preserve a healthy 92‑BB stack — I would have called a much smaller probe (~≤400) but this turn bet is big enough to concede.

I am holding 8h 2s on the button. While I have the chip lead and it's a 3-handed game, this hand is statistically one of the weakest possible starting hands with very poor equity and playability post-flop. Both opponents have healthy stacks and have shown willingness to fight for pots recently. There is no reason to attempt a steal with such a low-quality hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I’m in the small blind with Q♣3♥. Against only the big blind, calling an extra 50 to complete to 100 is very cheap — the pot will be ~200 after my call, so I’m getting about 4:1 (need ~20% equity). Q3o is a weak, OOP hand and not worth inflating the pot with a raise, but it has enough equity to justify a call at this price. Plan: complete to see the flop, play cautiously — check/control the pot if I miss and fold to significant aggression, continue only with top pair/two pair/sets or strong draws. If Claude 3-bets to a large size preflop I’ll fold rather than get involved OOP with this hand.

With K6o in the BB against a SB call from Minni (after Flash folded), checking is optimal. This marginal hand doesn't warrant preflop aggression. By checking, I see a flop cheaply and maintain flexibility. If I hit a king, I can extract value post-flop where I'll have position to react to Minni's action. This approach controls the pot size while keeping my hand's weak kicker manageable.

I completely missed the flop (Q♣3♥ on A♣ T♦ K♠). I only have a one-card Broadway draw (four Js = ~16% to make the straight by river). I'm out of position and the pot is only 200, so betting as a bluff is thin and calling a realistic c-bet is a losing proposition: to break even on the call I would need a bet ≤ ~39 chips (200 * 0.164 / (1-0.164)), which is unrealistic in this game. Plan: control the pot — check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Claude checks back I take the pot or re-evaluate on the turn; if a J hits I can proceed, otherwise I won't invest OOP with this marginal hand.

Betting for value with top pair. Minni's check indicates weakness, and I should capitalize on this with a reasonably-sized bet that extracts value from weaker holdings while protecting against draw completion on later streets. K6o has sufficient showdown value to merit this play.

I completely missed this flop (A♣ T♦ K♠) with Q♣3♥. I only have three clean outs to a queen and a tiny backdoor straight possibility, so my actual equity vs Claude’s likely c-bet range (Ax, Kx, Tx, pairs, some broadways) is well below what’s required. Claude’s 125 into the ~200 pot (pot now 325) means I need roughly 125/(325+125) ≈ 27.8% equity to break even on a call — I don’t have that and I’m out of position. With a healthy stack and no strong reason to gamble OOP, I fold to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with 8♦3♦ — a very weak but suited hand. In a 3-handed game the button can open very wide to steal the blinds and play postflop in position; limping is suboptimal because it invites multiway pots where 8-3s has little equity. I’ll open to a standard ~3xBB (300) to get fold equity and either take the blinds or play heads-up in position. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, but if called use position to c-bet favorable flops or float and realize equity.

6-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. I'm in the small blind facing a raise from Minni on the button, with Flash still to act in the big blind. My stack of 12,250 is healthy enough that I don't need to gamble with marginal hands. Even if I call the 250, I'm heavily dominated in any flop scenario, and I risk facing a re-raise from Flash. The correct play is to fold this unprofitable hand and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2c, which is one of the weakest possible hands. Minni has raised from the button, and while they could be stealing, 9-2 offsuit is not a hand worth defending with even at a discount. Folding is the best way to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

JT suited from the button is a strong holding that warrants aggressive play. Position is optimal, and both opponents have relatively short stacks. A raise to 300 applies pressure, shows strength, and gives me the opportunity to win the blinds immediately or play in position if called. This is a standard button raise with good equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 5h. Claude has raised to 300 (3x) from the Button. While 7h 5h is a speculative suited connector, playing it out of position against an aggressive Button raiser with a 3x sizing is generally a losing proposition in the long run. The pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450) are not quite enticing enough given the poor equity realization of the Small Blind. Additionally, Minni in the Big Blind is still to act and could potentially squeeze. I will preserve my stack and look for a more advantageous situation.

Pocket eights in the big blind facing a 3bb button open (300). With deep stacks (~92bb) I prefer to 3-bet for value/isolation rather than call OOP and let the button dictate the action. A raise to 1000 total is big enough to charge broadways and suited connectors and to gain fold equity, while leaving plenty of chips for postflop play if called. Plan: if Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll c-bet dry flops and proceed cautiously on ace-high or very draw-heavy boards, re-evaluating vs aggression.

Folding JT suited to Minni's 3-bet to 1000. While JT is a solid hand, this is a marginal spot:

1. **Hand strength vs range**: JT suited faces a significant range disadvantage. Minni's 3-bet range from the big blind likely includes premium pairs (AA-KK), strong aces (AK, AQ), and possibly broadway hands (QQ-JJ). JT has roughly 40-42% equity against this range, which is below the 65.8% pot odds required (need to call 700 to win 2050).

2. **Position disadvantage**: Post-flop I'll be out of position with Minni acting first, limiting my ability to control the hand with marginal equity.

3. **Effective stacks**: Minni's stack of 8,300 is moderate. If called, we could easily escalate into a large pot with a marginal holding.

4. **Tournament equity**: At hand 79/150 with a healthy 11,950 stack, I can afford to be selective. Preserving chips for premium hands in better spots is strategically sound.

This is a disciplined fold of a decent but not premium hand in an unfavorable situation.

On the button in a 3-handed game, Qd 7s is a marginal hand. While it can occasionally be used as a stealing hand, both Minni (SB) and Claude (BB) have shown significant aggression and willingness to defend their blinds in recent orbits. Given my current chip lead and the fact that Q7o plays poorly against a 3-bet or on many low/middling textures, I will choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.

Heads-up vs Claude in the big blind with KcTh and a deep ~96bb stack. Calling 50 is passive and gives the BB the initiative; KTo is a hand I want to play for value and fold equity heads-up. I’ll raise to 300 (3xBB) to apply pressure, narrow Claude’s range, and take the initiative post‑flop. If Claude 3‑bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold unless sizing/reads justify a call); if he calls I’ll C‑bet appropriate flops and use position/aggression to extract value.

Calling with A9o in the big blind against Minni's SB raise. The hand has reasonable equity, I'm getting 2:1 pot odds, and I'll have position postflop to leverage my advantage. The ace is strong and the nine provides additional equity. This is a standard profitable call given the circumstances.

I have KcTh on Ks Jc 8c — top pair with a reasonable kicker after I opened to 300 pre‑flop and Claude called. Claude’s calling range is wide (Kx, Qx, pocket pairs, suited connectors), so I’m ahead of a lot of it but vulnerable to sets or two‑pair (Jx, 88, KJ/K8). I want value and to deny free cards to overcards and straight/club backdoor combinations, so a standard c‑bet of roughly half the pot makes sense. Betting 350 (~58% pot) charges worse Kx, Qx, Tx and many draws while keeping the pot controllable against very strong holdings; if Claude check‑raises large I’ll give credit to sets/two‑pair and fold, otherwise I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

Calling 350 into a 950 pot (2.7:1 odds) with A9o on Ks-Jc-8c. Hand has approximately 35-40% equity with a top card, multiple draws (gutshot to broadway straight), and good position for turn play. Pot odds exceed the equity threshold, making this a profitable call. Minni's bet pattern suggests a strong hand but not necessarily premium, and I retain flexibility for the turn action.

I have top pair (KcTh) on Ks Jc 8c 5s. Claude cold‑called my pre and flop c‑bet, so his calling range is wide (worse Kx, Qx, Tx, medium pocket pairs, some backdoor/turn draws) and he’s less likely to have shown a very strong hand (JJ/88/KJ) by just calling the flop, though those hands are possible. The 5s is a relatively blank turn that helps my hand more than it helps his range. I want to extract value from worse kings/Queens/Tens and deny equity to single‑card straight combos, so I’ll continue barreling. A bet of ~60% pot (800 into 1300) is big enough to get value and fold equity while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. If Claude raises large on the turn I’ll give credit to a set/two‑pair and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river based on the card and his tendencies.

Calling the 800 bet. I need 27.6% equity to break even on pot odds of 3.625:1, and my A9 has approximately 35-40% equity against Minni's range on this K-J-8-5 board. With multiple outs including broadway straight (any Q), pairs (any 9 or A), and favorable stack size, this is a mathematically profitable call. Minni's aggressive line doesn't necessarily indicate a premium hand, and I have good equity to continue profitably.

I have KcTh (top pair) but the river Ac is a very hostile card — it pairs Ax hands in Claude’s range and, combined with the two clubs on the flop, completes some club flushes for any two‑club holdings. Claude cold‑called my preflop open and then called both my flop and turn bets without ever showing aggression, which suggests a sticky, showdown‑oriented range (Kx, Jx, medium pocket pairs, and some club draws). Betting here would mainly get called by worse Kx/Qx/Tx hands but will also get value‑bet by (or concede value to) any Ax or two‑club flush — and I have low fold equity because he’s been calling down. Pot is only ~2.9k and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to risk a big chunk of my stack on a thin value bet or to get raised by the rare two‑pair/set. Best line is to check and take it to showdown: I lose cleanly to A‑pairs/flushes but scoop the rest and avoid being exploited on a card that heavily favors his calling range.

Value betting the river with a pair of aces. The Ace of Clubs completed on the river, giving me a strong hand. Minni's check after aggressive play on flop/turn suggests weakness or a marginal hand. A 700 bet (24% of pot) extracts value from his range while maintaining balance. This sizing encourages calls from hands like KK, KJ, KQ, AJ, etc., and represents a solid value extraction spot. My pair of aces is likely the best hand and should win most of the time.

I checked the river as planned because the Ace of clubs is a very hostile card — it improves Ax hands and completes club flushes. Claude’s line (cold‑call preflop, call flop and turn, then a very small bet of 700 into ~3600) looks like a thin value/blocking bet or a weak/medium holding trying to get to showdown, not a clear monster. Raising here has poor fold equity (many Ax/flush combos still call or raise) and risks committing a big portion of my stack with a marginal hand. Calling 700 gets the correct showdown with top pair, preserves stack flexibility, and has good pot odds to call given his likely wide, sticky calling range. I’ll call to reach showdown rather than fold or bluff‑raise.

I'm on the button with 2♥4♦ — a very weak hand, but I have position and ~75 BB effective. The pot is 150 and the minimum raise is 200. Opening a standard button steal is preferable to folding or passively calling: a 3x-ish raise (to 300) applies meaningful fold equity against the blinds while keeping the pot controllable if called. Both opponents have shown they can fold to aggression sometimes, and if I face a 3-bet I will fold this junk hand. If I get called, I'll proceed cautiously postflop — using position to c-bet selectively and releasing without a pair or a real draw. Raise to 300 to try to pick up the blinds or play postflop in position; fold to significant resistance.

Folding KT offsuit from small blind in a 3-way pot. While the hand has some value, being out of position against two opponents makes it difficult to play profitably. My medium stack allows me to be selective, and this marginal hand doesn't warrant committing chips. Better opportunities will arise.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four suited. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude has folded. The pot is currently 450, and it costs me 200 more to call. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.25 to 1) and the fact that we are playing 3-handed, K4s is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard Button open. I have a significant chip lead over Minni, which allows me to play post-flop with some flexibility.

On a paired board of [2c, Jh, Jd], I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. As the defender in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the Button. This allows me to see how they react to the board texture before deciding whether to continue with my overcards and backdoor equity.

Flop: 2c Jh Jd. My hand 2h4d gives me two pair (Jacks and Deuces). This is a very strong holding on a fairly dry board — I beat all single-pair and high-card hands; only a Jack (trips/quads) or pocket deuces (full house) currently beat me. Flash checked; with one opponent I should be extracting thin value and denying free cards. A bet around 60% of the pot (~400 into 650) will get called by worse (A/K/Q, non-J pairs, many floats) but keeps the pot controllable in case of a check-raise. If Flash check-raises large I will seriously re-evaluate (often behind to trips/full house and will fold to heavy action); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and react to their sizing and the turn card.

I have King-high with a backdoor club flush draw on a paired board of [2c, Jh, Jd]. Minni's continuation bet of 400 into 650 is a bit on the larger side (around 60% pot), but on a paired board like this, the aggressor often bets a wide range of hands. With the pot odds being approximately 2.6 to 1, I have enough equity with my overcard and backdoor potential to call one street and see how the turn develops. I am currently the chip leader, which gives me the flexibility to make this speculative call against Minni's shorter stack.

The turn card is the Queen of Diamonds, which connects with a significant portion of the pre-flop raiser's range (hands like AQ, KQ, or QJ). I am currently holding King-high with a club draw that has not yet materialized. As the defender in the Big Blind, the most standard and cautious play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to gauge Minni's interest in the pot after the board has become more coordinated. If she checks back, I might have the best hand or a good opportunity to realize my equity on the river.

Turn: board 2c Jh Jd Qd. My hand (2h4d) is two pair, Jacks and Deuces — a very strong holding heads‑up but vulnerable to trips/quads or a hidden full house (Jx or pocket 2s/JJ). Flash’s line (call my ~400 c‑bet on the flop, then check the turn) looks like a medium‑strength or floating range rather than an ultra‑strong monster, so I expect many worse one‑pair hands and some mid pocket pairs in their calling range. I should be extracting thin value and denying free cards rather than checking back. I’ll bet ~60% pot to charge worse pairs and protect against any turn/river equity. Size: 900 chips. If Flash check‑raises large I will re‑evaluate and fold most of the time (they’re usually showing trips/full house). If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the river based on the card and sizing.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more coordinated with the addition of the Queen of Diamonds. I am holding King-high with no direct draw; the backdoor club draw did not improve as there is only one club on the board. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up her flop continuation bet with a second barrel of 900 into a pot of 1450. This bet represents considerable strength on a paired board where the Queen likely improved her range (hands like AQ, KQ, or even QJ). Given that I have almost no equity against her value range and my King-high is unlikely to be the best hand at showdown, calling 900 (nearly 40% of the current pot) is not a profitable play. I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

2-6 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While position on the button is favorable, the hand quality is simply too poor to justify any action. Flash (SB) and Minni (BB) have already posted blinds, and I would need to invest 100 chips to see a flop with a hand that plays very poorly post-flop. Even with positional advantage, playing weak hands out of position costs value in the long run. This is a straightforward fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit (Ad Jc), which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. Since it has been folded to me, I will put pressure on the Big Blind (Minni) by raising. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot or win the blinds immediately. Minni has about 81 big blinds, so there is still plenty of room for post-flop play if she calls.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind while I’m in the big blind with Q4o. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 (need ~33% equity). Q4o is a weak, often-dominated hand vs a SB open (lots of Ax/Qx/Kx in their raising range) and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. A min‑raise to 500 would give very little fold equity and a larger 3‑bet is too speculative with this holding. With a healthy stack (~81 BB) there’s no need to gamble marginally — fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button in a 3-handed game, K4o is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and utilize positional advantage. With the largest stack at the table, I want to maintain aggression and look to pick up the blinds or play the pot in position.

A♥7♥ is a playable, high-card suited Ace in a 3-handed pot. Button open to 300 is likely a wide raising range; I don't want to play a dominated multiway pot out of position if I can avoid it. I prefer to 3-bet and take the initiative — size to 1,000 total (about 3.3x the open) to generate fold equity versus the button and to put pressure on the big blind so we often get it heads-up. If I face a large 4-bet I will respect it and fold; if called I'll C-bet appropriate A-high or draw-friendly flops and proceed cautiously OOP in multiway spots.

4-7 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. Facing a 3-bet reraise from Minni (small blind raised to 1,000) after Flash opened from the button, I'm in a difficult spot. With poor hand equity, out-of-position, and facing 900 more to call into a pot where I'll be dominated by Minni's range, folding is clearly the optimal play. This is hand 83 of 150, so I have time to wait for better spots. There's no value in getting involved with such a marginal holding against aggressive action.

I opened K4o from the button, and Minni 3-bet to 1,000 from the small blind. While I have the chip lead and the positional advantage, K4o is a very weak hand to defend against a 3-bet. Minni's 3-betting range is likely to dominate me, and I don't have enough equity or playability to justify calling 700 more chips. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Three-handed on the button with Ah6d and ~85bb effective. This is a standard open: A6o has decent equity and a valuable ace-blocker, and acting last gives me postflop advantage. I want to take the initiative and avoid a small min-raise that invites callers, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to pick up the blinds or isolate the BB. Plan: vs a single call I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet favorable textures; vs a large 3-bet I’ll fold most of the time (re-evaluate vs small, pot-control 3-bets), since A6o is vulnerable out of position. Overall this raise maximizes fold equity and expected value from my position.

KJ of spades is a strong hand with excellent equity against Minni's likely button-raising range. With Minni's short stack of 8,250 and my healthy 13,900 chips, this is the right moment to 3-bet and build the pot. A raise to 900 (roughly 3x his opening raise) is standard and accomplishes multiple goals: it takes the initiative, defines my hand strength, likely eliminates Flash from the hand, and puts pressure on Minni's dwindling stack. In tournament poker at hand 84/150, I need to accumulate chips with strong hands, and this is exactly the spot to do it.

I am in the Big Blind with T9o. Minni raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind. My hand is a decent connective hand, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet range from the small blind, especially when squeezed between two active players. Minni has shown aggression and Claude has been active with 3-bets. Calling 800 more into a pot of 1300 out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents is not profitable. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I will call the extra 600 (900 total). Facing Claude’s 3-bet to 900 after I opened to 300, A♥6♦ is marginal but playable on the button with ~82bb effective. Calling keeps me in position, lets me realize equity, and uses my Ace-blocker against his 3-bet range. Pot odds on the call are roughly 600 into a ~1300 pot (~31–32% required equity), which A6o has against a reasonably wide 3-bet range from the SB. A small 4‑bet to 1500 would be thin value/bleed with this hand and folding is too nitty given my position and blocker. Plan: see a flop in position, c-bet favorable textures, and fold to strong counter‑action on dry boards or commit more only with clear equity or strong reads.

This is a profitable continuation bet situation. Pre-flop, I 3-bet with KJs and now face Minni's call heads-up. The flop (4d 2c 8s) is relatively dry with no obvious monsters likely in Minni's range. My KJs has excellent equity here with an open-ended straight draw (any Q or 9 makes me a strong hand), two overcard kings, and overcard jacks. Betting approximately 60% of the pot (1,200 into 1,900) accomplishes several goals: (1) maintains my pre-flop aggression to define hand strength, (2) builds the pot with what is likely the best hand currently, (3) keeps Minni's short stack (7,650) under pressure - calling commits significant equity, and (4) protects my hand equity and prevents free cards that could help his range. At hand 84/150, I need to accumulate chips with strong hands and good equity situations. This flop plays perfectly into that strategy.

Call 1200. I’m in position with A♥6♦ on a very dry flop (4♦-2♣-8♠) facing a 3‑better’s continuation bet. The pot is 3100 and Claude’s bet of 1200 means I need ~28% equity (1200/(3100+1200) ≈ 27.9%) to make the call. A6o has enough realization equity here, plus the Ace‑blocker reduces many of Claude’s strong Ax/AK combos. Folding would be too nitty given the pot odds and my positional advantage; a min‑raise to 2400 would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot against a range that includes many hands that continue (pairs and good Ax). Plan: call to see a turn, continue only with clear equity or favorable runouts (A/6/diamond or a blank where I can apply pressure), and fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve.

Checking to see the river cheaply. My KsJs is currently just K-high with limited equity (~13% if called). The 8d turn paired the board and likely connected with Minni's range. While my initial analysis suggested equity from draws, the actual straight draws don't exist on this 4-2-8-8 board. Betting with weak equity here risks committing more chips when I could see the river for free. Given Minni's short stack (6,450 remaining), he's likely either showing down a made hand or folding marginal holdings. Checking is the mathematically sound play at hand 84/150 with limited equity.

I hold A♥6♦ on a paired turn (4♦ 2♣ 8♠ 8♦) and didn’t improve. Claude 3‑bet pre and c‑bet the flop then checked the turn — that line shows a lot of missed broadways/overcards and pot-control hands, though a small portion of his range (sets: 88/44/22) can be trapping. This is a good spot to apply pressure in position: a bluff will fold out many of his marginal callers while not over‑committing if called. The pot is 4300; a half‑pot bet of ~2150 needs ~33% fold frequency to be profitable (2150/(4300+2150) ≈ 33%), which I estimate is achievable given his check. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls my river equity to pair is small (~13%), so I’ll proceed cautiously on the river (check/call or fold to big aggression unless I pair). I prefer a half‑pot size (keeps pressure but preserves stack), so I’ll bet 2150.

Minni's turn raise after the paired 8 came is a significant action that requires careful analysis. With Ks Js, I currently hold K-high, which is a weak holding on a 4d-2c-8s-8d board.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: My K-J is vulnerable here. The paired board 8-8 is unfavorable for K-high, as Minni's range can easily contain an 8, pocket pairs, or other made hands that beat me.

2. Minni's turn action: His raise to 2,150 with 4,300 remaining suggests either:
   - A legitimate made hand (pair, set, two pair with 8x or pocket pairs)
   - A semi-bluff that has superior equity to K-high
   - A desperate short-stack shove attempt

3. Equity analysis: Against his likely range (which includes many 8x combos, pocket pairs, and other holdings), my K-J likely has 20-25% equity at best, which is insufficient given the 3:1 pot odds (need 25%+ equity to break even).

4. Tournament context: At hand 84/150 with 11,850 chips, I can afford to fold this marginal spot and wait for a stronger situation. Minni's 4,300 chip stack will create better folding equity opportunities on subsequent hands.

5. Board texture: The paired board dramatically reduces the effectiveness of my K-J. Even if a K or J hits the river, I may still lose to an 8 or hidden pair.

This is a standard fold. I'll preserve my stack for situations where I have greater equity or initiative.

AJ is a strong premium hand on the button. With blinds at 50/100, raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard. Position is excellent, and I want to build the pot and/or take down the blinds. Both opponents have relatively short stacks - Minni especially at 10,650 with significant blind pressure. This is a clear raising situation to apply aggression from a strong position with a strong hand.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Nine offsuit (J9o) is a marginal hand. From the Small Blind, playing out of position against a Button open from Claude is difficult. Calling is generally discouraged because it invites the Big Blind (Minni) to squeeze or see a flop cheaply, leaving me out of position against both players. While the pot odds are tempting, J9o does not play well enough post-flop to justify the call or a 3-bet bluff against the current stack sizes. Folding preserves my chip lead and avoids a high-variance spot with a mediocre hand.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with 4♣2♠ — a very weak, poorly connected, unsuited hand that is heavily dominated by a button open range. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call, so I need roughly 31% equity to break even. 4-2o has well under that vs a typical button raising range and is miserable to play out of position on most flops. A light 3-bet to 500 could sometimes pick up the pot, but I’d be risking a lot more (and have poor blockers) vs an opponent who calls/4-bets reasonably often. With deep stacks and a heads-up dynamic, the +EV line is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qs 4h. While playing 3-handed requires a wider opening range, Q4 offsuit is still significantly below the standard opening threshold. Both Minni and Claude have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively in previous hands. Given my comfortable chip lead, there is no reason to risk chips with a weak hand like this. I will fold and wait for a higher equity opportunity.

Heads-up from the small blind with A♥5♦. This is a hand I want to take the initiative with rather than limp OOP — A5o has an ace-blocker and decent postflop equity against a wide defending range. I’ll open to a standard heads-up size (3x BB = 300) to deny cheap flops, gain fold equity, and build the pot when I hit an ace. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (fold more often vs large 3-bets, continue vs calls and play aggressively on aces/draws), but open-raising is the +EV line here.

Folding 3-9 offsuit in the big blind against Minni's raise. This is one of the weakest hands in poker - it has no high cards and a poor kicker. Even though I'm getting decent pot odds (need to call 200 into a ~600 chip pot), my hand has almost no equity against Minni's raising range from the small blind. She's been an aggressive player throughout this tournament, and calling with 3-9 out of position would be a substantial leak. With a healthy stack of 11900, I can afford to fold weak hands and wait for better opportunities. Preserving chips for premium spots is the mathematically sound approach.

QQ on the button is a premium hand — I want to build the pot and isolate the blinds while keeping initiative. With 100 to call and the min-raise at 200, a standard 3x BB open to 300 is appropriate: it folds out lots of weaker hands, keeps the pot manageable versus Flash/Claude, and preserves position postflop. I have ~107bb so I can comfortably continue vs a 3-bet (call and play postflop or 4-bet as needed depending on opponent and size). Plan: raise to 300, c-bet most favourable flops, and re-evaluate if heavy action or an Ace-high/connected board arrives.

Pocket nines is a strong hand to play from the small blind. Minni has shown aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament with frequent pre-flop raises across a wide range. A re-raise here accomplishes several things: (1) It demonstrates strength and puts pressure on the action, likely folding the big blind (Flash) and isolating Minni to a heads-up pot; (2) It allows me to leverage my hand strength - pocket nines is ahead of most of Minni's raising range; (3) The raise sizing of 800 is strong but not overcommitting given my stack of 11,850. This aggressive re-raise is well-positioned to build the pot in my favor and take down the hand either pre-flop or post-flop from a position of strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 5c, which is a speculative suited connector. Minni opened from the button for 300, and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind to 800. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot out of position against a strong 3-betting range, and I would also run the risk of Minni 4-betting. Given the stack sizes and the tournament context, it is best to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than committing chips to a bloated pot with a weak hand out of position.

QQ on the button facing a 3-bet to 800 after my open to 300 — I have position and a premium hand. Calling keeps the pot small and lets me use position, but a sizeable 4-bet extracts value from AK/AQ and most pocket pairs, gains fold equity vs weaker Ax/Kx, and takes the initiative postflop. A min-raise to 1300 is too small (gives too good odds to continue and loses fold equity). With ~100BB effective I want a standard value 4-bet that folds out weaker holdings and gets called by worse hands, so I raise to 2600 total. If Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls I plan to c-bet and re-evaluate on connected/A-high boards, and I’ll respect heavy 5-bet shove ranges but QQ still has good equity versus his calling range.

Pocket nines is a premium hand that warrants aggressive play in 3-bet pots. With Minni's stack already depleted to only 5,550 remaining after her 3-bet, shoving all-in applies maximum pressure and forces her to make a critical decision with limited chips. This move accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) It closes the action and prevents further post-flop maneuvering; (2) It puts immediate pressure on Minni's relatively short stack; (3) 99 has solid equity against her likely 3-bet range including AK, AQ, KQ, and other broadway combinations; (4) It allows me to win the pot either pre-flop through a fold or at showdown with a strong hand. Going all-in here is the most direct and powerful way to play this premium pocket pair.

I 4-bet to 2,600 with QQ to build the pot and take initiative; Claude’s 5-bet to 11,100 is a very large shove but looks polarized to very strong hands (AA/KK/AK) with the possibility of some lighter shoves. I must call 8,150 to put my remaining stack in. Break-even equity = 8,150 / (13,800 + 8,150) ≈ 37.2%. QQ is ~20% vs AA, ~33% vs KK and ~43–46% vs AK; against a realistic 5-bet/shove range that includes AK and occasional bluffs my equity is near or above the break-even threshold. I’ve already committed significant chips and folding here surrenders fold equity and likely EV. Given the spot (3-handed, I want to accumulate chips) and the hand strength, I call all-in.

With only 1,150 chips (11.5 BBs) in a 3-handed game, I'm in critical survival mode. Q6o on the button has decent equity in shorthanded play and represents a good steal opportunity. Raising to 350 (approximately 3.5x the raise) applies significant pressure on the blinds to fold, which is my goal. If called, I still have some showdown value. Folding and blinding down is not a viable long-term strategy at this stack depth.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) in the Small Blind. The Button (Claude) is extremely short-stacked with only 800 chips total and has raised to 350. This means he has only 450 chips left behind and is effectively committed to the hand. My hand is significantly stronger than a typical button opening range for a player with 8 big blinds. I will raise to 800, which puts Claude all-in. This move is designed to isolate the short stack and discourage the Big Blind (Minni) from entering the pot with a wider range, allowing me to realize my equity against Claude's entire remaining stack.

I’m in the big blind with K♦4♦ facing a button open to 350 and a 3-bet to 800 from the small blind. The pot is 1250 and I must commit another 700 to call (I’ve already posted 100), which gives me roughly 36% pot-equity to break even preflop. K4o is a weak, frequently dominated holding with poor postflop playability out of position against a 3-bet range. Flash’s 3-bet is likely a fairly strong range and Claude (button) still has only ~800 behind, so this can easily become a multiway, awkward pot or an all-in vs a better hand. With deep stacks I can wait for a much better spot; I won’t invest 700 chips with K4o. Fold.

Extremely short-stacked in critical survival mode. Q6o gets 2.8:1 odds against Flash's 3-bet range, likely having 25-30% equity. With only 11 BBs remaining, folding marginal hands with reasonable equity means blinding down slowly - a losing strategy. Already committed 350 chips (27% of my starting stack), so folding eliminates that investment. Going all-in with 800 total is the correct play at this stack depth with a hand that has some equity and potential to win the pot. This is the necessary risk-taking required when short-stacked in tournament poker.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit on a dry flop of 5-3-9. Claude is extremely short-stacked with only 350 chips remaining in his stack. Given the pot size is 1700, Claude is effectively committed to the hand. I will bet 350 to put him all-in and realize my equity. Ace-high is likely the best hand here against a wide button-opening range from a short stack, and I want to ensure the money goes in while I still potentially have the best hand or strong overcards.

All-in call for my last 350 chips on the flop with Q6o. After already committing 450 pre-flop, folding wastes that investment. Q6o has 25-30% equity against Flash's 3-bet range, which is sufficient for the 6:1 pot odds offered (2050 in pot vs 350 to call). This is a tournament survival spot where I must take the calculated risk with any reasonable equity.

I am on the button (SB) with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) in a heads-up situation against Minni. A9o is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play, well ahead of an average random hand. With a stack of 18450 and blinds at 50/100, I want to play aggressively. The action is currently on me as the small blind. Standard play here is to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to take control of the pot and narrow down the opponent's range. Minni has been active, but A9o is too strong to just call or fold here.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300. I’m Big Blind with 6♦2♠ — basically the worst starting hand. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (pot odds ≈ 3:1, need ~25% equity). Despite the decent pot odds, 6-2 offsuit is heavily dominated by a button’s opening range, has almost no postflop playability (poor straight/flush potential) and I’ll be out of position the whole hand. Raising here would be reckless and calling is a marginal, long-term losing defense. Fold and wait for a better spot to defend or attack.

Heads-up on the button with Ad5h — a hand I want to play aggressively in position. Completing (just calling) gives Flash free odds to see flops and surrenders initiative; a standard open-raise gains fold equity, builds the pot when I'm likely ahead vs BB's wide calling range, and lets me use position postflop. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 (2x BB) to keep the sizing compact and induce calls from worse hands. Postflop plan: continuation-bet most flops and play straightforwardly; if Flash 3-bets large/isolates heavily I’ll be cautious and fold to extreme pressure, but will call smaller, standard 3-bets to realize my positional edge.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. The pot is currently 300, and it costs me only 100 more to call, giving me 3:1 odds. J9o is a standard defending hand in a heads-up situation, especially against a small raise. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my stack depth without bloating the pot out of position.

I am the first to act on the flop as the Big Blind. The flop comes Kc 6c 3h, which is a board that generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Holding Jack-high with no direct draws, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here doesn't accomplish much against Minni's likely range, and checking allows me to evaluate her continuation bet size and decide whether to fold or occasionally float if the situation warrants.

I opened to 200 preflop from the button with Ad5h and got called; I keep position and a wide advantage against Flash’s calling range. Flop Kc–3h–6c is fairly dry but contains a King that beats my Ace; still I block many A-x combos by holding an Ace and face a checked pot. A standard continuation bet here will fold out lots of unpaired hands and small pairs, get value from worse Aces/draws, and take the initiative to control the hand. I’ll make a firm but not oversized c‑bet (~60–65% pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. If called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (checking K’s or club cards that likely improve their range, continuing only when I improve or get favorable action); I’ll fold to a large check‑raise since my hand is only Ace‑high at the moment.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high on a board of Kc-6c-3h. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 250 into a 400-chip pot. I have no pair, no draws, and no significant backdoor equity. While J-high can occasionally float in heads-up play, doing so out of position against a standard-sized bet on a King-high board is generally unprofitable. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Queen-Eight offsuit, which is a strong starting hand for this position. I will put in a standard open raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

Heads-up versus a button open to 300. I have J♠4♦ in the big blind — a marginal but defensible hand heads-up. Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed including my 100 BB) is cheap relative to our stacks (I have 21.5k) and lets me realize equity and see a flop against what is likely a wide opening range. I’ll play cautiously out of position: check/control the pot if I miss, fold to heavy continued aggression, and play aggressively only if I connect (pair, two pair) or pick up strong draws. I avoid 3-betting this weak offsuit OOP to keep the pot manageable.

I missed the flop. On A♠ K♠ 8♣ I hold J♠4♦ — no pair and only a very remote runner-runner spade-based possibility (~4% to make a flush by river). Out of position vs a button opener whose range will include many A/K and broadway hands and who will c-bet at a high frequency. Pot is small (600) relative to both stacks (I ~21.3k vs 18.1k), so there is no reason to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, fold to substantial continued aggression, and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw or if opponent checks the turn.

I have bottom pair (8s) on a very coordinated Ace-King high board. While I have some showdown value, Minni's calling range pre-flop includes many hands that hit this flop hard (Ax, Kx, QJ). Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially see a safe turn card or induce a bluff on later streets.

Out of position on A♠ K♠ 8♣ 6♣ with J♠4♦ I have no pair and no realistic draw (only a remote runner-runner spade chance). The pot is small (~600) relative to deep stacks (≈21k/18k) and Flash checked the flop, indicating pot control or a relatively wide/missed range. Betting OOP with pure air is unnecessary and will often get called by better hands or raised by hands that beat me. I’ll check to keep the pot small, reserve fold equity vs significant aggression, and re-evaluate on the river — only bluff or invest if I pick up real equity or get a favorable reaction from the button.

I have bottom pair with an Eight on an Ace-King high board. While Minni has checked twice, this board hits a wide range of calling hands from the Big Blind. By checking back the turn, I maintain pot control and realize my showdown value without risking a check-raise from a better hand. I am happy to see a free river and potentially catch bluffs or win against weaker holdings like small pairs or missed draws.

On the river I have J♠4♦ on A♠ K♠ 8♣ 6♣ 10♦ — no pair, no flush, just J‑high. Flash checked flop and turn after opening preflop, which suggests either a missed wide raising range or pot‑control with a real pair; the 10 on the river actually improves some holdings (Tx, QJ straights) so a bluff is riskier. Pot is only 600 while both stacks are deep, so there’s no reason to bloat the pot or risk chips with a very weak hand and little fold equity. Checking to showdown preserves chips and avoids getting called by better hands. I check.

On the river, the board has completed with an Ace, King, and Ten. I hold bottom pair with Eights. Minni has checked for the third consecutive street after calling my pre-flop raise. While my pair of Eights is weak, it holds marginal showdown value against missed draws (like suited connectors that didn't connect or small pocket pairs). Betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand like a King or a Ten, and since I checked the flop and turn, my hand looks exactly like what it is: a medium-to-weak holding looking for a cheap showdown. I will check behind to realize my equity and avoid being check-raised off the best hand by a bluff or losing more chips to a slow-played Ace or King.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 4♦T♣ — hand is marginal but raisable in heads-up. I should open rather than limp to take the initiative and use positional advantage postflop. With deep stacks (~200bb) a standard, modest open is appropriate: raise to 300 total (common 3x-ish sizing here). That size gets fold equity, builds a pot when I have position, and keeps things manageable — if Flash 3-bets I can fold this hand without risking much; if he calls I can play postflop in position.

I have Pocket Queens, which is a premium hand, especially in a heads-up scenario. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and I need to build the pot for value and protect my hand. A 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play here. I will raise to 1000 to put pressure on Minni's opening range and take control of the hand.

I opened to 300 heads-up and Flash 3‑bet to 1000. T♣4♦ is marginal but has value in position versus a likely wide-ish heads‑up 3‑bet range. Calling the extra 700 (1000 total) gets ~35% pot‑odds to continue and lets me realize equity and use positional advantage postflop. I don’t want to 4‑bet-bluff out of position or fold too often — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me fold to heavy aggression if I completely miss. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously (fold to large turn/river pressure with no improvement; if I hit a pair or a good draw I’ll start applying pressure).

I have an overpair (QQ) on a very dry board of 5-7-2 rainbow. This board is unlikely to have hit Minni's range significantly unless she holds a set of 7s or 5s, but I am ahead of almost all other hands she would call a 3-bet with, such as TT, 99, 88, and all Ace-high combinations. I will lead with a continuation bet of 800 to extract value and protect against overcards on the turn.

Flash 3‑bet preflop and now fires a small c‑bet (800 into 2800 ≈28% pot). I have 4♦T♣ on a very dry 5♣7♠2♦ board — no pair, only six clean outs (three 4s and three 10s) and basically no real draw to rely on. Calling 800 gives me ~22% pot odds and is a cheap way to use my position: I can float, realize equity, and try to take the pot away on later streets if Flash shows weakness. A bluff‑raise is too thin with this hand, and folding is too tight given the small price and positional advantage. Plan: call now, evaluate the turn — if I pair or pick up legitimate equity I’ll apply pressure; if I miss and face significant turn aggression I’ll fold.

I still have an overpair (QQ) on a relatively dry board, though the 6s turn adds some connectivity (straight draws like 89 or 45 now have more potential, and 34 or 89 would have hit). However, in a 3-bet pot, Minni is more likely to have overcards or mid-range pairs like 88, 99, TT, or JJ. I will continue to bet for value and protection, targeting those pairs and any 7x she might be holding. A bet of 1800 (half pot) is a standard value sizing here to maintain control of the pot and get called by worse hands.

Call 1,800. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop c‑bet, modest turn bet) can be either value or bluff; folding here is too tight given the price and my position. Pot is 5,400 and calling 1,800 gives ~25% pot odds.
