On the turn my hand has meaningful river outs: 3s and 8s complete straights (≈8 clean outs) and there are three remaining 10s and three remaining 4s (≈6 dirty outs) — ~14 total outs (~30% to improve), so my equity is around or above break‑even versus this bet. Raising as a semi‑bluff is too thin with no made hand and would bloat the pot out of position. Plan: call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable; if I hit a pair or a straight on the river I will extract value, otherwise I’ll be prepared to fold to significant river aggression.

I have an overpair (QQ) on a board that ended 5-7-2-6-6. I have been the aggressor throughout the hand, 3-betting pre-flop and betting both the flop and turn. The river 6h pairs the board, which is generally a safe card for me, as Minni's range for calling a 3-bet and two streets of value likely centers on pocket pairs (88-JJ) or top-pair combinations (like A7). While a 6 is possible (such as 67s or 56s), it is statistically less likely than the mid-range pairs I currently beat. I will place a value bet of 2800 to target those pocket pairs and A7-type hands, as Minni has shown a willingness to call down in previous hands. This sizing is designed to look like a standard value bet that can still be called by a stubborn pair of sevens or a middle pocket pair.

Board: 5♣ 7♠ 2♦ 6♠ 6♥. My hand: 4♦ T♣ — no pair, just T‑high. Pot is 10,000 and Flash bet 2,800. If I call I must put in 2,800 to win a final pot of 12,800, so my break‑even equity is ~2,800/12,800 ≈ 21.9%.

Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop c‑bet, turn bet, river bet) is strongly weighted toward value: pocket pairs (77/55/22), hands that improved on a 6, and many broadway/AX combos that beat T‑high. The river pairing a 6 also strengthens many of his ranges (trips/quads/fullhouse possibilities). The subset of pure bluffs or worse high‑card hands that I actually beat is small and almost certainly below the ~22% needed. Calling here would be a large, marginal commitment (~16% of my stack) with very poor odds of being ahead.

Decision: fold — I don’t have enough showdown equity versus his betting range. I’d only consider calling if I had a solid read that Flash bluffs this river extremely often.

In a heads-up situation, while I have position post-flop as the Small Blind, 93o is at the bottom of my range. With a healthy stack and a goal to maximize chips over 150 hands, I prefer to fold this garbage hand and wait for better opportunities rather than entering a pot with a significant mathematical disadvantage.

Heads-up on the button with deep stacks I should be opening very wide — limp/call is too passive. Q5o is marginal but perfectly fine to raise in position to seize the initiative, build the pot when I have fold equity and let me play postflop with position. I’ll make a standard 2BB open (raise to 200 total). If Flash calls I’ll c-bet dry textures and proceed cautiously on coordinated/high boards, extracting value when I pair my queen and folding to clear signs of strength when appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with T7o, which is a marginal hand. Minni has opened for a small 2x raise from the button (200 total). I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. While the hand isn't strong, the price is very favorable in a heads-up situation, and folding would be too tight. I will call to see a flop.

On this flop of Kd-6d-2c, I have Ten high with no direct draws other than a very back-door straight or runner-runner flush possibility. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor on a King-high board, has a significant range advantage. Since I have checked, I will wait to see if she continues. If she bets, I will likely fold as I have no piece of this board and no profitable way to continue against a standard continuation bet.

Heads-up I raised preflop and now see Kd-6d-2c — a relatively dry, K‑high board with two diamonds. I hold Qd5h: no pair, only a backdoor diamond/runner-runner possibilities and a decent blocker (Qd) to some of Flash’s calling hands. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet a high frequency here to capitalize on wide calling ranges and to get folds from unpaired, non-diamond hands. I’ll size to ~50% pot (200 into 400) — big enough to generate fold equity and charge draws, but small enough to keep pots controllable with a marginal hand. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously on turn (check or pot-control to a safe card, barrel selectively on blank turns, fold to substantial raises since my showdown value is weak).

On a board of Kd-6d-2c, I have Ten high with no significant draws or backdoor potential. Minni, being the pre-flop aggressor, has a range advantage on a King-high board. Her continuation bet of half the pot is standard, and with my hand having almost zero equity against a typical betting range, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

I have been dealt Ace-King offsuit on the button, which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. My current stack is 22,000 against Minni's 17,850. In previous hands, Minni has shown a willingness to call raises but also to fold under pressure (Hands #92, #94). I want to build a pot immediately with this strong hand and maintain aggression. I will raise to 300 (a standard 3x open) to extract value and take control of the hand.

Heads-up against the button who opened to 300. I hold 6s3h — a weak, unsuited hand, but facing a standard heads-up open I should defend the big blind fairly wide. Calling is the right play: I only need to commit 200 more (300 total commitment) for decent pot odds against a wide raising range, keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. I don’t want to 3-bet as a bluff with such poor blockers and little postflop playability. Plan postflop: proceed cautiously — check to the raiser on dry boards and fold to large continuation bets, look to realize equity or occasionally bluff-catch when I pick up a pair or a draw.

I’ve flopped two pair (6s and 3h) on 6d Jh 3d — a very strong holding heads-up against a button’s wide opening range. I want to extract value from Jx and single-pair hands and charge diamond/straight draws while protecting my equity. Betting for value + protection is correct; I’ll bet pot-sized to make draws pay and get called by worse. If Flash check-raises big it often represents a set (JJ/66/33) so I’ll re-evaluate (fold to extreme overbets, call smaller/standard raises to reach showdown depending on sizing). If called I’ll continue to extract on later streets when appropriate.

I started the hand by raising pre-flop with Ace-King offsuit, a top-tier starting hand in heads-up play. The flop comes Jack-high with two diamonds. Minni has led out with a pot-sized bet of 600. While I don't have a pair, I have two overcards (Ace and King) and the Ace of Diamonds, which gives me a backdoor nut flush draw. In heads-up play, folding AK to a single lead on this texture is too tight. Calling allows me to see a turn card where any Diamond, Ace, or King could significantly improve my hand or equity. I have a healthy stack and can afford to see how the turn develops before committing more chips or deciding to turn the hand into a bluff.

I have two pair (6s and 3h) on 6d–Jh–3d–5h in a heads‑up spot. Flash opened wide preflop and called my flop raise, so his calling range is full of Jx, single pairs and some two-diamond combos. The turn 5h doesn’t complete the diamond draw and my hand is still very strong against his likely range — I want to extract value and charge any remaining river outs. Bet pot to charge Jx and single-pair hands, deny equity to the few backdoor combos, and build the pot for value; if he check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely a set) and respond by calling a reasonable raise but folding to an overbet jam.

On the turn, the board is 6d-Jh-3d-5h. Minni has led for a full pot bet of 1,800 after also leading the flop. I have Ace-King with the Ace of Diamonds, giving me the nut flush draw (Ad with 6d and 3d on the board) plus two overcards (Ace and King) which are likely outs if Minni is holding a Jack or a lower pair. I have approximately 15 outs (9 diamonds, 3 Aces, and 3 Kings). To call 1,800 into a total pot of 5,400, I need 33.3% equity. My drawing odds for the river are approximately 32.6%. While slightly below the direct pot odds, the implied odds of hitting the nut flush or top pair against Minni's aggressive line make this a clear call. I will not raise here as her range seems polarized towards strong hands (like a Jack or better) or semi-bluffs, and I want to realize my equity and potentially trap her if I hit on the river.

I hold two pair (6♠3♥) on 6♦ J♥ 3♦ 5♥ 9♦. Flash called my flop raise and then my turn raise, so his continuing range is heavy in one-pair hands (Jx, some 9x, Kx) plus some two-diamond flush draws and a few pocket pairs. The river 9♦ completes many diamond flush combos, but most of his calling range (single pairs) still loses to my two pair. Betting for value is correct: I want to charge Jx/9x and deny any free river card to hands that could improve. I’ll make a pot-sized value bet (5400) to extract from worse one-pair hands while accepting the risk of being called by the rare completed flush or a set; if Flash check-raises massively I’ll re-evaluate (large overbets usually represent flushes/sets and I’d fold to extreme shoves, while calling smaller raises depending on sizing).

On the river, the board is 6d-Jh-3d-5h-9d. I hold Ad-Kh, which is only Ace-high. While I had the nut flush draw on the turn, the 9d river card only makes three diamonds on the board, so I missed my draw. Minni has led for a pot-sized bet on the flop, turn, and river. This triple barrel donk line typically indicates a very strong hand, such as a Jack, two pair, or a flush. Even though I hold the Ad blocker, I cannot justify calling a 5,400-chip bet with no pair. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♦J♣ — a top-tier heads-up hand. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and the pot is small (150); calling 50 keeps the pot small but surrenders initiative. I want to take the lead, deny the BB cheap flops, and build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop deep-stacked. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) — that’s a standard heads-up open size that still leaves plenty of maneuvering room postflop. Plan: if BB calls I’ll c-bet favorable textures and pursue value on A/J-high boards; if BB 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to very large 3-bets but calling smaller ones depending on sizing/read).

In a heads-up situation, King-Jack offsuit (KJo) is a very strong starting hand, well within the top tier of my range. Minni has been playing aggressively, as seen in the previous hand. I will put in a 3-bet (re-raise) to 1,000 to take control of the hand, extract value, and define Minni's range. This puts the pressure back on the button open and builds a pot with a hand that has high equity in this format.

Call to 1,000 total. Facing a modest 3‑bet to 1,000 after my 300 open, A♦J♣ is strong heads‑up and I prefer to keep a multi‑street pot with position rather than polarize by 4‑betting. Calling costs 700 more into a 1,300 pot (2000 total after my call), so I need roughly 35% equity to break even — reasonable for AJo versus a likely wide/aggressive 3‑bet range from Flash. Position postflop lets me realize equity, control pot size, and extract value on A/J‑heavy or dry boards; I’ll proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards. If Flash shows extreme strength (a large 4‑bet/shove) I’ll re‑evaluate, but for now call and play the hand in position.

On this dry, low board (8h 2s 7c), my KJo still has significant showdown value and equity against a wide range that Minni calls 3-bets with. Given that I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to maintain pressure and potentially win the pot immediately. I'll bet roughly 35% of the pot to keep the price efficient while denying equity to hands like QJ or JT.

Heads‑up in position with A♦J♣ on a dry 8♥‑2♠‑7♣ flop. Flash (the 3‑bettor) makes a small continuation bet of 700 into a 2700 pot. I have two overcards and the Ace blocker, and in position I can realize equity and control the size of the pot. Calling 700 gives me ~20.6% break‑even equity (700/(2700+700)), which is reasonable vs a wide c‑betting range that includes many bluffs and thin value hands. I don’t want to inflate the pot with just Ace‑high by raising (min‑raising to 1400 is thin and opens me to jams or larger turn aggression from a 3‑bettor), so I’ll call and see a turn. Plan: if I pair an Ace or improve, extract value; if I miss and face significant turn aggression, fold unless the action or card strongly favors continuing.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Deuce of Diamonds (8h 2s 7c 2d). This is a relatively safe card that doesn't significantly alter the equity of our respective ranges. Since I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I have shown considerable strength. However, Minni has called both the 3-bet and the continuation bet, suggesting she has a piece of the board (like an 8 or a pocket pair) or a strong ace-high. My King-high still has some showdown value but is unlikely to get better hands to fold by betting again, nor is it likely to get value from worse hands. Checking here allows me to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. If she checks back, I might be able to get to a cheap showdown or reassess on the river. If she bets, I will have to decide based on the size whether my King-high is still the best hand against her potential bluffs.

I check. Heads‑up with A♦J♣ on 8♥‑2♠‑7♣‑2♦ (pot 3,400, stacks ~19k each). Flash 3‑bet pre, c‑bet the flop and I called; the turn paired the board and he checked. That line looks like a lot of missed c‑bets or pot‑control with medium strength hands — if he had turned trips/quads or a very strong two‑pair he was likely to keep applying pressure. I only have Ace‑high (with an Ace blocker to some Ax combos) so betting here is thin value: it will be called by most pairs that beat me and will fold only pure air. I prefer pot control and to preserve my stack equity — check to see the river and make a decision there. Plan: if Flash checks the river we show down; if he makes a small/medium river stab I’ll call for showdown with blocking value; if he barrels a large, pot‑sized or shove line I’ll fold (unless the river pairs an Ace or gives me a made hand).

The river is the Deuce of Clubs, completing a board of 8h-2s-7c-2d-2c. This means any pocket pair or an 8 now has a full house, and any deuce has four of a kind. My King-high is extremely weak in this context. After checking the turn, betting the river would be a polarized move that is unlikely to force a fold from an 8 or a pocket pair, and even some Ace-high hands might call given the way the hand has been played. Checking is the best option to attempt to reach showdown cheaply against her missed draws or to minimize losses if she has a superior hand.

Board: 8♥ 2♠ 7♣ 2♦ 2♣ — I have A♦J♣: only Ace‑high. With three deuces on board virtually any pocket pair or any 7/8 in my opponent’s hand becomes a full house (and any deuce is quads). Flash was the preflop 3‑bettor, made a small flop c‑bet and then checked both the turn (when the second deuce hit) and the river. That line looks like missed equity or a medium hand, not a value line for quads/full houses. Betting here would be thin value (few worse hands will call) and a bluff risks a large chunk of my stack for little fold equity against a cautious 3‑bettor. Pot is 3,400 and stacks are deep enough that preserving fold equity and stack is important. I’ll check and take the showdown — no reason to throw more chips away on a very bad board with only Ace‑high.

I am on the button with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is one of the weakest hands in heads-up poker. While wide ranges are typically played from the button, T2o is near the bottom of the range and doesn't merit a raise against Minni, who has been active and aggressive in defending the blinds. Limping is an option, but given the hand's poor playability post-flop, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (SB) — open-raising a wide range is standard. 3♦5♦s is suited and connected with good postflop playability in position. Calling 50 is cheap but surrenders initiative; raising to 200 (2x BB) buys fold equity, lets me steer the hand from position, and keeps the pot manageable with a speculative holding. Flash has been active and call-heavy, so a standard 2x sizing balances pressure and pot control. Plan: fold to a large, clearly polarized 3-bet; call or continue vs small 3-bets depending on sizing; if called use position to c-bet favorable low/diamond/connected flops and play cautiously on high-card/paired boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 2s. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Button. While the pot odds are decent (calling 100 to win a total pot of 300), Q2o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given Minni's recent aggression and my stack depth (175 BBs), there is no need to defend such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

In a heads-up situation, Jack-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button/small blind. My goal is to maintain aggression and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 allows me to exert pressure on Minni in the big blind while keeping the pot manageable if she chooses to defend.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300; I’m in the big blind with T♠5♦. This is a marginal hand but against a likely wide HU opening range I can defend. The pot is ~400 and it’s 200 more to call (I’ve already posted 100), so calling gives ~2:1 pot odds. I’ll call to see a flop, fold to a large 3‑bet, and play cautiously post‑flop—only continuing aggressively with a made ten or better or a strong draw; otherwise check/fold to heavy pressure.

I have T5o on a paired board 4h 8c 8d — no pair, no meaningful backdoor draws and very little equity vs a wide HU opening range that contains lots of 8x, pocket pairs and 4x. Pot is 600 and I’m already in — betting here is thin: I’ll be called by better hands and risk a raise out of position. Best play is pot control and induce: check and see how Flash reacts. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to reassess; if he bets a small c‑bet (≈≤200) I’ll consider calling to see a turn because the pot odds are attractive for a bluff-catch; if he fires a medium/large bet (≈≥300) I fold. No raise or lead bluff — board texture and my lack of equity make aggression too risky out of position.

On a dry, paired flop of 4-8-8, a continuation bet is highly effective as it is difficult for the Big Blind to have connected with this board. My Jack-high has decent showdown value against her complete air, and by betting half the pot, I represent a strong range including pocket pairs or an Eight, while forcing her to fold hands that currently have equity against me like lower overcards or weak draws.

Out of position heads‑up on a paired 8 board (4h 8c 8d) with T5o I have almost no showdown value and very few clean outs. Flash’s 300 flop bet is a medium/large c‑bet — per my pre‑flop plan I fold to bets ≥300. Calling 300 to try to win ~1200 requires roughly 20% equity; T5o is well below that versus a HU opening/c‑bet range full of 8x, pocket pairs and 4x. Raising as a bluff OOP here is too risky (will be called or 3‑bet too often). I fold and conserve my stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be playing a very wide, aggressive range. 5♣7♠ (5h7s) is weak but perfectly reasonable to raise from the SB in heads-up because I retain position postflop and can apply fold equity. I’ll raise to the minimum standard open (200 total = 2BB) — this is small relative to my deep stack, preserves maneuverability, and often picks up the pot immediately or lets me play a cheaply-contested pot in position. If Flash 3-bets I’ll respect that and fold most of the time given my weak holdings; if he calls I’ll continue postflop with position advantage and look to C-bet or float favourable flops.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni opened with a small raise to 200 from the button. To take the initiative and extract value from a range that likely includes many weaker Aces and various king/queen highs, I will 3-bet (re-raise) to 700. This builds a pot with a hand that is often ahead and forces Minni to define her hand strength.

I opened wide in heads-up (200 total) with 5♥7♠ to leverage position, but Flash 3-bet to 700. Pot is 900 and it costs me 500 to call — about 500/1400 ≈ 35.7% pot-odds. 5-7 offsuit has poor equity vs a typical BB 3-bet range (often dominated by broadways/pairs), so calling is +EV only rarely. 4-bet bluff is possible in general heads-up, but this hand has poor blockers and the sizing/line suggests real strength; I don’t want to bloat a deep-stack pot out of position preflop with such a weak holding. I’ll fold, preserve my 22k stack, and wait for a better spot to apply aggression in position.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 6h 8c. This is a speculative hand, but given the current tournament dynamic and my stack size, it's worth playing. I'll complete the small blind to see a flop for a small investment. Minni has been active and has a larger stack, so I should be prepared for potential pressure pre-flop, but completion allows me to see more cards cheaply.

I have 9♥4♠ — a weak, unsuited, poorly-connected hand. Flash simply limped from the SB, pot is small (200) and I’m out of position post‑flop (I act first). With deep stacks and little reason to invest extra preflop with this holding, I’ll check and see a flop for free, preserving chips and allowing me to fold to strong aggression or continue only if I hit or pick up good equity (pair, 9 or 4, or strong draw).

I have 9♥4♠ — a complete miss on Ac 7d 3d: no pair, no backdoor diamond, no meaningful draw. Flash limped preflop so his continuing range is wide but is strengthened by the Ace on the flop (he can easily have Ax or a small pair/draw). I’m out of position and the pot is small (200) with deep stacks, so there’s no +EV reason to bluff-raise or lead here. Check and fold to any meaningful bet; if Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn and re-evaluate (look to barrel only if I pick up real equity or the turn gives a very favorable runout/read).

In a limped pot with 8-high and no significant draws on an Ace-high board, there is little value in betting. Minni checked, and while an Ace-high board is often a good bluffing candidate, it is also a board where she could easily be check-calling with a 7, a 3, or a flush draw. I will check back to keep the pot small and see if I can improve on the turn or potentially represent a hand later if the board texture changes.

I have 9♥4♠ — completely missed. The turn (3♣) pairs the board but doesn’t improve my hand or give me any realistic outs. Flash limped and checked the flop, so his range is wide but still contains many Ax or small pairs that will continue to a turn bet. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our deep stacks, so there’s no +EV reason to bluff-block or spearhead the pot here — if he fires back I’ll be forced to fold and shouldn’t invest more with no equity. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and only bluff if the runout or read makes it sensible.

In this limped pot, the board has paired 3s on the turn after an Ace-high flop. Minni has checked twice. While I could attempt a bluff here to represent an Ace or a 7, the pot is very small (200 chips) and I have no equity with 8-high. Checking back is the most sensible play to see a free river and avoid unnecessary risk in a marginal situation.

I have 9♥4♠ on A♣ 7♦ 3♦ 3♣ J♥ — no pair, no meaningful draw, and both of us have shown passive lines (Flash limped preflop and checked flop/turn). The pot is tiny (200) versus deep stacks (my 21,900 / his 17,900). A river bluff would need substantial fold equity (e.g. a pot-sized bet of 200 requires >50% folds; a half‑pot bet requires >33%), which is unlikely against a limp/call player who will often call with any Ace or showdownable hand. Betting here risks being called by better hands or getting raised with little to gain. There’s no +EV reason to create action in this small pot with zero showdown value — conserve chips and wait for better spots. I’ll check and avoid unnecessary variance (plan is to fold to a raise and take the free showdown if Flash checks back).

On the river, the board is Ac-7d-3d-3c-Jh. Minni has checked for the third time in this limped pot. While her triple check suggests a very weak range, my 8-high has almost no showdown value. In such a small pot (200 chips), a bluff is unlikely to be profitable as Minni may call with any small pair or even King-high just to see the showdown. I will check back and concede the pot rather than risking chips on a low-equity bluff.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Q♦J♠ — a strong heads-up holding. With deep stacks (22050) and only one opponent, I should be aggressive to take the initiative and use postflop position. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) pressures weaker hands, isolates Flash’s range, and builds value when ahead while still being small relative to my stack. Plan: c‑bet many flops, re-evaluate on turn/river based on texture and opponent reaction.

Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. I am holding Nine-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand to play out of position. Given the stack depths and the previous patterns of play, defending such a marginal hand against a standard open is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, King-Six offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I want to maintain aggression and apply pressure on Minni. A standard 3x open to 300 has been effective in previous hands to win the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play.

Heads‑up vs the small blind who opened to 300; I hold K♠Q♣ (strong heads‑up hand with good equity and playability). With only two players his raising range is wide, and I’d prefer to take the initiative rather than see a cheap flop out of position. My stack is deep enough to 3‑bet for value and fold equity — a 3‑bet to about 1,000 (total committed) is large enough to fold out many weaker holdings and extract value from hands that continue, while keeping 4‑bet decisions manageable. A min‑reraise to 500 is too small and calling surrenders initiative. I will 3‑bet to isolate and play the hand on my terms.

I opened to 300 with K6o, which is a standard play on the button in heads-up. Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. In previous hands, Minni has shown both aggression (Hand #92, #95) and some willingness to fold to pressure, but a 3-bet here indicates a strong range or a polarized bluff. K6o is marginal against a 3-bet; it does not play well post-flop when dominated and is out of position. However, since the stacks are relatively deep and I am getting 2.4:1 to call, I could consider it, but K6o is structurally weak. Given Minni's recent uptick in aggression, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than bloat a pot with a mediocre hand.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 4s — this is a clear open-raise. I want to take the initiative, build a pot for potential set-mining with deep stacks, and avoid giving the BB a cheap free flop. Raising to 300 (a standard 3x-style open here) applies pressure, keeps the sizing in line with the table, and leaves room to fold to a large 3-bet or proceed postflop with positional advantage. If called, I'll c-bet favorable flops or slow-play sets; if faced with a big 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and reads.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 6h. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. While 8-6 offsuit is a marginal defending hand in heads-up play, Minni has been quite aggressive recently, and playing this hand out of position without a strong lead or connectivity is likely to result in chip bleed. I have 174 blinds remaining and prefer to wait for a more advantageous spot or a hand with better playability to contest the pot.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation. While J6o is a weak hand, standard heads-up strategy involves opening a very wide range from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Minni has been playing aggressively, so I will test the waters with a standard raise to 300. If Minni three-bets, I can easily fold without much loss, but if she folds or just calls, I maintain initiative in the hand.

Heads-up against the SB who opened to 300. I have Td4h in the BB — a marginal hand but with some equity against a wide heads-up opening range. The pot is 400 and I must call 200, so I’m getting ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity) which T4o can often meet vs a wide range. I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with a weak, often-dominated hand and bloat the pot unnecessarily. Plan: call to see a flop, realize equity and fold to significant aggression on dry/unfavorable flops; continue cautiously if I connect.

Heads‑up out of position on A‑3‑5 (two hearts). I have T♦4♥: no pair, a small backdoor heart possibility and a 3‑4‑5 sequence that makes an open straight to a 2 or 6 (8 outs → ~17% turn, ~31% by river). This is a marginal, pot‑control hand OOP vs an opener who often has A‑x or value hands. Leading here bloats the pot with little showdown value. Plan: check and realize equity. If Flash c‑bets small (roughly ≤ ~250–300 into 600) I’ll call (pot odds + implied odds justify chasing the ~30% straights); I’ll fold to larger, value‑sized bets because I’m likely behind to any Ace and cannot profitably continue OOP without hitting. If he checks back I’ll take a free turn and re‑evaluate on further development (raise as a semi‑bluff only if I pick up strong additional equity).

I hold Jack-high on an Ace-high flop after raising pre-flop. This is a classic situation for a continuation bet. Even though I have no direct connection to the board, the Ace is a scare card that favors my perceived range as the initial aggressor. By betting half the pot, I put pressure on Minni to have an Ace or a pocket pair to continue. If she folds, I win a 600-chip pot with nothing; if she calls or raises, I can safely assume she has a piece of the board and I can look to fold on later streets given my lack of equity.

Flash's flop bet is small (300 into a 900 pot, ~1/3 pot) and is consistent with a wide heads‑up c‑bet range. Calling 300 to win ~1,200 requires about 25% equity. With T♦4♥ on A‑3‑5 (two hearts) I have a one‑card gutshot to the wheel (four 2s → ~8.5% on the turn, ~16.5% by river), a tiny backdoor heart flush (~4% by river) and some chance to pair a 10 or 4. Versus a wide c‑bet this combined equity and the pot odds justify a probe call to see the turn while keeping the pot small out of position. Plan: call now, fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve (raise/click for value if I hit the 2 or a clean pair), and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I improved to a lone pair of 4s on the turn (T♦4♥ with board 3♥-5♠-A♥-4♦). That’s second pair at best and still crushed by any Ace (and by many hands in the opener’s likely range). I’m out of position and the flop c-bet was small, showing a fairly wide range — but many of those hands still contain an Ace. Betting now would bloat the pot OOP and get value-checked/called by all the A‑x hands I’m losing to, so it’s a losing line overall. Checking controls the pot, allows me to extract value if he bluffs or checks the river, and keeps options open if I improve. Plan: check the turn, call a small river bet but fold to large aggression unless I improve; if he checks through I’ll re-evaluate on the river for a thin value bet or showdown.

The turn 4d creates a board of Ah-5s-3h-4d. This board is very coordinated, offering straight possibilities. Since Minni called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, it is highly probable she holds an Ace or at least a pair. I hold Jack-high with a 6, which gives me a straight draw (a 2 would give me a 6-high straight, beating a 5-high straight). However, a second barrel is unlikely to fold out an Ace or a strong draw. Checking back here allows me to see a free river card, control the pot size, and potentially hit my draw or reach a cheap showdown.

River: I hold Td4h on 3h-5s-Ah-4d-Ts — that’s two pair (Tens and Fours) with an Ace kicker on the board. Villain opened pre, made a small flop c-bet and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with a wide HU c-bet range (many A-x, some medium pairs, occasional bluffs). My two-pair is strong vs a lot of that range (beats single Aces and missed draws) but is vulnerable to the cleaner value combos: AT (two pair A/T), pocket Tens (trips), or any 4x/sets that make trips — those beat me but are less frequent. With the pot small (~1,200) relative to stacks, the highest EV line is to bet a modest amount for thin value and to charge/deny equity to missed draws. I’ll bet about half-pot (600) to get called by worse one-pair hands and some weaker Tens, while keeping the pot controllable; if villain raises big I’ll assume a stronger value hand and fold to large aggression.

On the river, the board is Ah-5s-3h-4d-Ts. I have Jack-high with no pair or draw. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, then checked the turn and led out for 600 (half pot) on the river. Given that the 4 on the turn completed a potential straight for any hand containing a 2 (A-2-3-4-5), and the Ace remains a very strong part of both our ranges, my Jack-high has no showdown value. Minni's bet is likely a value bet with an Ace, a small pair, or even a Ten that hit on the river. While she could be bluffing with a missed heart draw, most of her bluffs or marginal hands still beat Jack-high. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with T7o. With deep stacks (~231bb) and position I should be opening very wide and seizing the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3bb) — that’s 250 more chips beyond my SB 50 — to get fold equity, price out some of the weaker defenders, and give myself a playable pot postflop. Plan: c-bet dry, A/K high boards; control pot and fold to large, committed 3-bets but call or flat smaller 3-bets and play postflop given deep stacks.

In this heads-up scenario, Minni has been consistently aggressive with pre-flop raises. Although J6o is a playable hand in many heads-up defending ranges, the 3x raise size from Minni reduces my pot odds slightly. Given that I am out of position and Minni has shown a willingness to apply pressure on later streets, I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to contest a pot with a stronger starting range.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with King-Trey suited (Kh 3h). This is a strong enough hand to raise in a heads-up situation to apply pressure and build a pot, or at the very least, it is a mandatory play. Given the stack depths and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (hand 107 of 150), I want to play aggressively. I will raise to 300 to take control of the action.

Flash opened to 300 from the button heads‑up. I have T♥9♥ — a strong heads‑up hand with good flush/straight potential and decent equity against a wide opening range. With both stacks very deep, calling preserves position realities (I’ll be OOP) while realizing equity and keeping the pot manageable; a 3‑bet OOP is fine sometimes but not necessary here. I’ll call the extra 200 (300 total) and play the flop based on texture: press when I hit/hearts or strong draws, otherwise proceed cautiously vs heavy aggression.

I have top pair (T9♥) on a 10♠-5♣-4♣ flop. Against Flash’s wide heads‑up opening range this hand is ahead of a lot of continuing hands (weaker tens, second pairs, missed broadways, suited connectors). The board has two clubs but no obvious strong straight draws, so betting protects my hand and extracts value. I’m out of position, so I prefer to take the initiative rather than check and give Flash a free card. I’ll size about half the pot to get value while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. Plan if raised: re-evaluate based on sizing (call reasonable raises and proceed cautiously on the turn; fold to an overly large shove that represents a made stronger hand). If called, continue based on the turn card and opponent action.

I raised pre-flop with Kh 3h and Minni called. The flop is 5c 4c Ts. Minni has decided to lead out (donk bet) for 300 into the 600 pot. While I don't have a pair, I do have one overcard (the King) and some backdoor potential. Folding to a half-pot lead on this relatively dry board is too tight, as Minni could be leading with a wide range of hands including small pairs or draws. Raising doesn't seem optimal as it might bloat the pot when I am currently behind. Calling allows me to see how the turn develops and potentially catch a King or a heart to improve my equity.

Heads‑up, I have T9♥ on 5♣-4♣-T♠-Q♦ after I was the aggressor on the flop (raised and got called). This is still a reasonably strong hand vs Flash’s wide opening/calling range — I beat a lot of pairs, worse tens, and floats, while the turn Q only improves a subset of his calling range (Qx). I’m out of position, so I want to protect my hand and extract value from worse hands while charging the club two‑card combos that have a one‑card flush draw. A half‑pot bet keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, gets value from worse tens/pocket pairs/mini‑pairs and makes club draws pay to continue. If he check‑raises big I’ll treat it as a strong Q/club/set and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river based on the card and opponent sizing. Bet ~50% pot to balance protection and value.

I currently hold King-high with no draws on a board of 5c-4c-Ts-Qd. Minni led out on the flop and has now fired a second barrel on the turn for half the pot. My hand has very little equity, as I am likely drawing to only three Kings, and even then, I could be behind to two-pair or better. Given Minni's persistent aggression in this hand, it is unlikely that a bluff would be successful, and calling with no made hand and no significant draw is a losing play. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with pocket tens — a strong, value hand in this spot. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to take the initiative and build the pot in position while denying cheap odds to speculative hands. A raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) is a standard, constructive size: gets value from worse Ax/Kx and small pairs, applies pressure to draws, and keeps postflop play comfortable with deep stacks (23850 vs 16000). If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing, but for now raise for value and initiative.

In a heads-up scenario, Q9o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard 3x raise. I am getting 2:1 odds to see a flop, and my hand has decent post-flop playability. Folding would be too tight here, and a 3-bet is unnecessary given the stack depths and the strength of the hand; calling allows me to realize my equity and see how the board develops.

On a flop of Qc-3h-8d, I have flopped top pair with a 9 kicker (Qd 9h). This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. Standard play as the defender in the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donking) here doesn't serve much purpose as it allows her to fold her bluffs and only continue with hands that might have me beat or have significant equity. By checking, I give Minni the opportunity to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands, including bluffs and weaker pairs. I intend to call any standard-sized continuation bet.

I raised preflop and hold pocket tens on a fairly dry Qc-3h-8d flop. As the preflop aggressor I have a range advantage heads-up; Flash checked, so a continuation bet will accomplish value (getting calls from worse pairs, some Qx/8x/3x that check back, and stubborn overcards) and fold equity against missed overcards. The board has no real straight or multi-card flush draws, so a half-pot c-bet is efficient: big enough to fold out equity (overcards) and deny cheap cards, but small enough to keep worse hands and some draws in. I’ll bet 300 (≈50% pot) to take the initiative; if I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously vs significant aggression (folding to very large check-raises depending on sizing and read).

On a board of Qc-3h-8d, I have flopped top pair with a 9 kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni's 300-chip bet into a 600-chip pot is a standard continuation bet. While I have a strong hand, raising here would likely force out her bluffs and weaker holdings while keeping me in against better Queens or sets. By calling, I maintain the pot size, keep her range wide, and protect my own checking range. I will see how the turn develops and likely continue to value-call or bet depending on the card and her subsequent action.

The 5h on the turn is a relatively blank card that shouldn't have improved Minni's range unless she has exactly 64, which is unlikely. Having top pair with a nine kicker in a heads-up pot remains a strong hand. I will continue with the strategy of checking to the pre-flop aggressor to maintain a balanced range and allow her to continue with her bluffs or second-best hands. If she bets, I plan to call as long as the sizing is reasonable, as my hand is too strong to fold but not quite strong enough to raise for value against her likely continuing range.

Turn analysis: I hold TT on Qc-3h-8d-5h. After I c-bet the flop and was called, Flash’s range is wide but contains a lot of hands that currently beat me (Qc, Qx, 8x, sets, some smaller pairs) and many will simply check the turn. The turn 5h doesn’t change much — it’s a relatively dry board with low fold equity against a caller who turned up middle/top pair. Betting here would build the pot when I’m often behind and will get called by better hands, so barreling is low EV. I prefer pot control and to realize my equity cheaply: check the turn, see what Flash does on the river, and then re-evaluate. If Flash bets a small amount on the river I’ll call for pot control; if they shove or make a very large bet I’ll fold (or call only if the sizing and runout make calls correct). Action: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid getting value-bet by hands that currently beat me.

I have improved to two pair (Queens and Nines) on the river. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely has a marginal made hand like a weaker Queen or an 8, or perhaps a hand that was looking to get to a cheap showdown. Since I now have a very strong hand in this heads-up pot, I will bet for value. I am targeting her one-pair hands like QJ, QT, or even a stubborn 8x. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot is a standard value-heavy sizing that should entice a call from the top of her range while maximizing my return.

Call 800. Pot is 2000 and Flash bets 800 on the river (final pot if I call = 2800), so I need roughly 800/2800 ≈ 28.6% equity to break even. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash called and both of us checked the turn. That line forwards a range with a lot of thin value (Qc/Qx, 8x, 9x, occasional sets) but also some missed hands and bluffs. My TT has showdown value versus smaller pairs and bluffs and the river bet is small enough that folding would be too tight given the pot odds. Raising is unattractive — it polarizes my range and will mostly get called by hands that already beat me. I’ll call to realize equity and see the showdown.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding 4-3 offsuit on the button (Small Blind). While heads-up play often requires a very wide opening range, 4-3 offsuit is at the absolute bottom of the starting hand rankings. Minni has been playing aggressively and is currently the chip leader. Committing more chips to see a flop with a hand that has very little playability and low showdown value is not a winning long-term strategy here. I'll preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

Heads-up on the button (also SB) with T8o — a hand worth defending. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to complete to 100 total (good pot odds: call 50 to win ~200 -> ~4:1). I have position post-flop, so I can see a flop cheaply and use position to C-bet good textures or check/control against resistance. A min-raise to 200 is an option to take initiative, but with a marginal hand and a somewhat aggressive BB I prefer to keep the pot smaller and realize equity in position. Plan: see flop, c-bet when favorable, check/fold or check-call to control the pot when not.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Minni has limped in from the Small Blind. While J9o is a strong enough hand to consider an isolation raise, checking allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small with a medium-strength holding, which is a sound strategy in a tournament setting when out of position.

On a King-high board where I have no pair or significant draw, checking as the first actor is the most standard and safe play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board. If she checks behind, I get to see a free turn card. If she bets, I can evaluate based on the size, but likely fold given the weakness of Jack-high here.

Checked to on a K-6-2 rainbow. I have T8o with no pair and only a backdoor spade possibility — essentially air on this board. This texture actually favors the BB’s preflop calling/raising range (lots of Kx and overcards), so betting here would be a thin bluff: I’d win small pots sometimes but when called I have very little equity and no good real draws. With position and deep stacks I prefer pot control and to see a cheap turn. Plan: check and evaluate turn — call a small probe if it looks marginal, but fold to significant aggression. If the turn brings a real improvement for me (pair, two pair, or a spade giving a decent draw) I’ll change gears; otherwise keep the pot small and look for better spots.

I have turned second pair with my Jack. Given that Minni limped pre-flop and checked back on a King-high flop, it is unlikely she holds a King. I will place a value bet to extract chips from weaker pairs or draws that might be calling, while also protecting my hand from potential overcards on the river.

Fold. Facing a 150 bet into ~200 (350 total) I must call 150 to win 500 total (150/(350+150) ≈ 30% break-even equity). With T8o on K-6-2-J I have only about 6 clean outs (3 tens + 3 eights) — roughly 6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve on the river — far below the pot‑odds required. The opponent checked the flop and now fires a smallish turn probe, which is consistent with many Kx/Jx value hands that will continue; a raise would be a thin pure bluff that won’t fold enough of that range and would bloat the pot OOP with essentially no equity. With position and deep stacks I preserve chips for better spots rather than calling a negative‑EV river decision, so I fold.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to put pressure on the Big Blind and build a pot with a superior range. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size in previous hands.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB heads-up. In the big blind with 5♦7♦ I’ll call (total commitment to 300). Heads-up ranges are wide and suited connectors have good equity and implied odds against a typical open; calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds and this hand’s equity vs a wide opening range is generally higher thanks to flush/straight potential. A 3-bet is possible as a bluff, but out of position with a low suited connector I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop. Plan: continue aggressively on favorable flops (flush/straight draws or pair) and fold to heavy strength when completely missed.

Flop checked to me heads-up with 5♦7♦ on 5♠ A♦ 8♥. I have a vulnerable bottom pair plus a very weak backdoor diamond draw. Flash’s preflop open is wide so his check includes lots of missed hands and overcards, but it also includes Ax hands that have me crushed. Betting for value and protection is the right line: a half‑pot bet will fold many pure overcards, charge any straight/diamond backdoors, and extract value from worse pairs/8x/7x. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll give it up (likely an Ace or two‑pair/sets); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue with aggression when I improve. Bet half pot to seize initiative and deny free cards.

I raised pre-flop with King-Queen offsuit and the flop came Ace-Eight-Five rainbow. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 600 pot. While an Ace-high board favors my range as the pre-flop raiser, Minni has demonstrated a frequent tendency to lead into the aggressor in previous hands. My King-high still has some potential to improve on the turn, and the price to see the next card (3:1 odds) is reasonable given the possibility that Minni is betting a middle pair or air. I will call to see if the turn provides more clarity or improves my hand.

I have a vulnerable pair of fives (5♦ in hand + 5♠ on board) and I just picked up a strong diamond draw (A♦ and 6♦ on board + my two diamonds = 4-card diamond draw). Opponent (Flash) opened wide preflop and called my flop half‑pot bet, so his continuing range includes lots of Ax, some 8x, KQ-type hands and medium pairs — many hands I can fold out, and many I’m behind to (A♣/A♥/A♠ or an 8x). With ~14 clean outs to improve to two‑pair/trips/flush (~30% to improve on the river) and decent fold equity heads‑up, the correct line is to press the action now as a semi‑bluff: build the pot when I have equity and fold equity, and deny free cards to his non‑pair overcards. I’ll size up to about two‑thirds pot to give myself meaningful fold equity but not commit the stack — if I face a large raise I’ll concede (likely an Ace or two‑pair), but if called I have good equity to improve and will re‑evaluate the river (value bet big when I make the flush/two‑pair; control/ check‑call or fold to extreme aggression on a blank). Bet ~2/3 pot (~800 into 1,200).

Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn on an Ace-high board. Her turn bet of 800 into a 1200 pot represents significant strength or a high-equity draw. With only King-high and no direct draws, my hand has very little equity to continue. Even if I were to hit a King or Queen on the river, I would still be behind any Ace in her range, and she has shown enough aggression to suggest she is not simply giving up. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 4♣2♥ — a very weak holding, but heads-up preflop ranges are wide and aggression is profitable. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete; the minimum raise is 200 but my usual open size here is ~3× the BB. I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure, steal the blind often, and keep my opening range wide. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold this junk; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to pick up pots or realize showdown equity cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind holding 42s. Minni has raised to 300 (3x) from the Small Blind. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, 42s is near the bottom of my defending range, and playing it out of position against an aggressive chip leader often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Given the current tournament stage and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a heads-up situation, Jack-Eight offsuit is a solid hand to play from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and use my positional advantage throughout the rest of the hand.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a button open to 300. I have Q♠2♣ — a marginal hand but defendable heads-up. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (so total committed will be 300), giving ~33% break-even pot odds. Versus a wide button opening range I have enough equity to see a flop and use position-independent postflop play to realize that equity. 3-betting is too thin here: Q2o has a weak kicker and few blockers, and I don’t want to build a big out-of-position pot or face a 4-bet. Folding is too tight in heads-up dynamics. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed cautiously — value and protect when I pair the Q, bluff or float selectively against an aggressive opponent, and fold to substantial aggression on missed boards. If Flash 3-bets big preflop I’ll let this go.

Heads-up on a Q63 rainbow with two spades on board. I’ve hit top pair (Q♠) but with a very weak kicker (2♣). Flash opened wide preflop so his range includes many worse queens, middle pairs and spade combos. I want value and to deny free cards to spade/backdoor draws, so I’ll make a medium-sized protection/value bet. A ~2/3-pot bet (400 into 600) gets called by worse Qx and many pairs/draws while folding some pure floats; if Flash raises big I’ll respect that and fold/re-evaluate (he’s representing sets/two-pair or strong semi-bluffs). If he just calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I started the hand with a standard pre-flop raise with Jc8s, which is a reasonable hand to play heads-up from the button. On the flop of 6s-3s-Qh, Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 400 into a 600-chip pot. I currently have Jack-high with no pairs and no significant draws—I lack even a backdoor spade draw. Given that Minni is showing strength by betting into the pre-flop aggressor on a board containing a high card (Queen) and a flush draw, I have very little equity. It is best to fold here and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with K♥T♥ — a strong, well-blocking, suited hand that plays very well postflop. Stacking deep (23400 vs 16450) I want initiative and fold equity rather than conceding the pot by limping. Flash (BB) has shown a wide opening/mixing range in prior hands, so a standard 3bb opened size is exploitable and consistent with my table image. Raising to 300 (total) denies correct odds to many of his calling hands, builds the pot with position, and keeps postflop maneuverability. Therefore I raise to 300.

T3s is a weak hand to defend against a 3x open from the small blind. While it has some flush potential, its lack of high-card strength and connectivity makes it difficult to realize equity out of position. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Holding K-high (K6o) on the button in a heads-up situation is a standard opening hand. I have a healthy stack and want to maintain pressure. Based on recent hands, Minni has been defending the big blind but often folds to pre-flop aggression or gives up on flops. I will raise to the standard 300 to take control of the pot.

Flash (button) opened to 300. I’m in the BB with Ac3h heads-up — this is a hand worth defending: vs a wide button open A3o has reasonable equity and the call is cheap. Calling 200 more (300 total from me, including my posted BB) gives good pot odds (~3:1) and keeps the pot manageable out of position. I don’t want to 3-bet light OOP with a weak kicker (a min-3bet to 500 is too small to fold out many hands and commits me out of position). Plan: call and evaluate the flop — extract value if I pair my Ace, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy action on very unfavorable boards.

I defended preflop with Ac3h heads‑up vs a wide button open — a reasonable call to keep the pot small and realize equity. Flop Tc Kh 7c: I only have ace‑high (no pair) plus a backdoor nut‑club possibility (Ac). Out of position with a weak kicker and a hand that’s often dominated by Ax with better kickers, I don’t want to lead or commit chips bluffing. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me call a modest continuation bet to see a turn (and realize my backdoor club/ace equity), and fold to heavy aggression. Plan: check now; call small C‑bets (roughly ≤½ pot) to see a turn, fold to large bets/raises, and only apply pressure later if I improve or get a clear read to bluff.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a board that contains two clubs and some straight potential (10 and 7). In a heads-up pot, top pair is a very strong hand. I will bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws Minni might be holding. Since the pot is 600, a bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard continuation to maintain pressure and extract value from weaker hands like a Ten or a draw.

Heads‑up in the BB with Ac3h — preflop I defended to keep a wide button opening honest. Flop Tc Kh 7c: I have only ace‑high with a small backdoor club possibility. Flash’s bet is small (300 into 900 ≈ 1/3 pot). That size is exactly the kind of continuation bet I want to call and see a turn with rather than folding or turning this into a bluff raise out of position. Pot math: calling 300 to potentially win 1,200 (pot + bet) gives ~4:1 (need ≈20% equity). My pure chances to improve by river are ~12.5% to hit an Ace and ~4.2% to hit a two‑card club flush — ~16–17% combined — a bit shy of the break‑even number, but I also have non‑zero showdown value vs lots of the button’s wide c‑betting air (Qx, Jx, suited connectors, etc.). With effective stacks (mine 23,250 vs his 15,850) and being out of position, calling keeps the pot controllable, realizes equity, and preserves the ability to fold to heavy turn aggression. Plan: call the small c‑bet now; fold to a large raise or very aggressive turn sizing without improvement; if I hit an Ace or get a favorable runout I’ll start building the pot or apply pressure.

Board is Tc Kh 7c 7d, pot 1,200. I have Ac3h — only Ace‑high and a single‑card shot to improve on the river. I’m out of position vs an opponent who opened wide and made a small flop c‑bet, so his range contains a lot of Kx/Tx, pairs, and bluffs. The turn paired the board, which increases the chance he has a 7 (or a hand that improved) and makes my Ace‑high a marginal showdown hand. There’s no compelling reason to build the pot out of position with no made hand. Checking keeps the pot controllable, allows me to realise equity (I have ~15 useful outs to improve to some pair/flush ≈ 30–33% to change the hand on the river, though many improvements are still marginal), and lets Flash barrel bluffs so I can call small bets or fold to clear value/raises. Plan: check. If Flash bets a small-to-medium size (~≤½ pot) I’ll call to see the river; I’ll fold to large, committed raises unless I pair the Ace or make the club; if I hit on the river I can value‑bet/raise. Action: check to keep pot manageable and make a decision on the river with more info.

I have top pair (Kings) on a board that has now paired the 7. Minni checked the turn after calling a flop bet. While the 7 pairs the board, it is unlikely to have improved Minni's range significantly based on her check-call on the flop. I still likely have the best hand, as she could be calling with a Ten, a straight draw, or a flush draw. I will bet 600 to extract value from those hands and protect against the flush draw while the pot is 1200.

Fold. Pot is 1,800 and Flash bet 600 on the turn; calling 600 to win 2,400 requires ~25% equity. With Ac3h on Tc Kh 7c 7d my only realistic clean outs are the three remaining Aces (~3/46 ≈ 6.5%). Pairing the 3 or other rivers rarely beat Flash’s continuing range (Kx, Tx, 7x, pocket pairs) and the backdoor club possibility is dead after the turn. Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet then a turn barrel) is consistent with value, and a raise bluff here is too risky — I don’t block enough of his calling combinations and would face a shove or call I can’t profitably continue. Mathematically and strategically this is a fold to preserve my stack for better heads‑up spots.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB) I should be opening a wide range. Jd4h is marginal but perfectly fine to raise in heads-up to take the initiative and pressure the big blind. Pot is small and stacks are deep so a small standard open to 2bb (200 total, including my 50 SB) keeps the pot manageable while keeping my range aggressive. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this weak holding; if he calls I’ll use positional advantage to play the flop. Raise to 200.

I have K7 suited in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. This is a very favorable price to see a flop with a hand that has good high-card value and flush potential. Given the stack sizes and the current tournament stage, defending this hand is standard. Raising is also an option, but calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity.

I have flopped top pair with King-Seven of spades on a King-Trey-Five board. In a heads-up situation, top pair is a significant hand. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them to see their continuation action. I intend to call most reasonable bets or potentially raise to protect my hand against heart draws and straight possibilities.

I opened wide preflop and still have positional initiative. The flop K♥ 3♥ 5♦ completely misses J♦4♥ — I have only jack‑high and a very remote runner‑runner heart draw. Flash checked, so a continuation bet makes sense heads‑up to take down the pot now or fold out unpaired overcards and weaker holdings. I’ll make a modest c‑bet (≈½ pot) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: if Flash folds we collect; if he calls I’ll proceed very cautiously on the turn (check back non‑threatening turns, fold to strong aggression, and extract value only if I pair or improve). If he check‑raises I’ll fold — this hand has almost no showdown value.

I have top pair with King-Seven of spades on a board of King-Trey-Five. While my kicker is relatively weak, top pair in a heads-up pot is a strong hand. Minni's half-pot continuation bet is standard for a pre-flop aggressor. I will call this bet to keep the pot size manageable and allow Minni to continue with bluffs or weaker hands. Raising here might force out hands I beat while keeping me in against stronger Kings or draws that have significant equity. I prefer to see how the turn develops.

I have top pair (Kings) with a Seven kicker. On the flop, I checked and called a half-pot bet from Minni, which is a standard way to play this hand to keep the pot manageable. The turn is the Queen of Spades, which is an overcard to the lower board cards but doesn't change my relative hand strength much. As I am out of position, I will check again to see Minni's reaction. If she checks back, I am likely ahead; if she bets, I will evaluate based on the size, but top pair is generally strong enough to continue to at least one more bet here, especially with the potential for missed draws like hearts.

I missed the turn completely with J♦4♥ — I have only jack‑high and no realistic draw (the backdoor heart is dead with the Q♠ on the turn). Flash called my flop c‑bet and has shown a tendency to call down, so betting the turn would have poor fold equity and will get called too often. Pot control is the priority: check to keep the pot small, realize showdown equity if I pair, and fold to meaningful river aggression. If Flash fires a small blocking bet on the river I can consider a call for pot odds; otherwise I’ll fold to pressure.

On the river, the board is now Ace-King-Queen-Five-Trey. I have a pair of Kings with a Seven kicker. While I had top pair on the flop, the turn Queen and river Ace are both overcards. Minni checked back on the turn, which could indicate a medium-strength hand or a draw. However, the Ace is a very significant scare card that hits a large portion of a pre-flop raiser's range, including many of the draws she might have been holding (like Ace-high heart draws). My pair of Kings now only functions as a mediocre bluff-catcher. Betting for value is out of the question as I won't get called by many worse hands, and betting as a bluff is unnecessary since I have some showdown value. I will check and see if Minni checks back or attempts a bet.

I have J♦4♥ with no pair and no meaningful showdown equity. Flash checked the turn and the river — he called my flop c‑bet and has shown a willingness to call down in this match, so my bluff fold equity is low and a bet will often be called by A/K/Qx or even loose second‑pair hands. The river A♣ completes lots of better hands (top pair and some two‑pairs/sets), and there’s no thin value I can get when checked to. Pot control and stack preservation are best here: check and take my chances at showdown. If Flash fires a substantial bet I’ll fold; if it’s a tiny blocking bet I can re‑evaluate, but with no bet currently I check.

I have been dealt pocket fives (5h, 5d) on the button in a heads-up situation. Pocket pairs are very strong starting hands in this format. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build value and take control of the hand from position. Minni has shown a willingness to defend but also folds often enough to pre-flop aggression.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) who opened to 300. I hold A♦8♦ — a strong heads-up hand (suited ace) that plays well vs a wide SB opening range. With deep stacks (effective ~17k+) I want to both get value from worse aces/Kx and generate fold equity against his marginal holdings. Calling keeps the pot cheap but surrenders initiative; a small min‑raise is too weak. I 3-bet to 1200 (total) to isolate, pick up the pot when he folds, and build a pot I can play postflop with a hand that has good equity and backdoor flush potential. If he calls I'll proceed cautiously OOP on the flop; if he 4-bets very large I'll re-evaluate (likely fold to heavy 4-bets).

I have been dealt pocket fives (5h, 5d) on the button. I opened for 300 and Minni 3-bet to 1200. With effective stacks at approximately 170 big blinds, I have significant implied odds to call and look for a set. Additionally, playing in position allows me to navigate post-flop situations more effectively. 4-betting would likely force out hands I dominate while keeping in hands that have me crushed, so calling is the most profitable play here to see a flop.

Flop: A♣ 8♣ 5♣. I hold A♦8♦ — two pair (Aces & Eights). Heads‑up this is a very strong hand, but the board is extremely wet: lots of club equity (both made flushes and single‑club flush draws) and a few set/trips combos (88,55, AA) that can beat me. Flash called my 3‑bet preflop, so his range contains Ax, suited clubs and pocket pairs. I want to extract value from worse A‑x and medium pairs while charging club draws and denying free cards. Betting ~2/3 pot achieves that balance: it builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, prices draws uncomfortably, and still leaves room to fold to a polarized large check‑raise. Plan: bet 1600 into 2400. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises big (polarized to flush/set) I’ll strongly consider folding to avoid being crushed, but I’ll call smaller raises where he can be bluffing or thin value.

I have flopped a set of fives on a monotone board. Since the 5c is on the board and I hold the 5h and 5d, I have the bottom set. The presence of the Ac on the board is significant as it blocks many of Minni's strongest nut-flush possibilities. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop suggests a range of high cards and strong pairs, many of which I currently dominate. I am raising to 4000 to get value from strong Aces (like AK or AQ) and high pocket pairs that may contain a club (like KcK or QcQ). This raise also protects my hand from a potential fourth club on the turn. If Minni has already made a flush or holds a higher set, I still have roughly 15-20% equity to improve to a full house or quads.

I have A♦8♦ — two pair (Aces & Eights) on A♣8♣5♣. Flash’s check‑raise to 4,000 polarizes his range to made flushes (two clubs) and sets (AA, 88, 55) but also contains many one‑club/AX and medium pair hands that I currently beat. Folding two pair heads‑up here is too passive and a 3‑bet/raise would overcommit against a range that likely has many nut‑club combos. The raise size gives me attractive pot odds to see a turn (call 2,400 into a ~8,000 pot ≈ 23% break‑even). I’ll call the raise (add 2,400, 4,000 total) to keep worse A‑x and pocket pairs in, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn: if a club comes I will proceed very cautiously (likely check/fold to heavy turn/river aggression); if a non‑club safe card comes I’ll look to extract thin value.

I have two pair (Aces & Eights) on A♣ 8♣ 5♣ — a very strong but vulnerable hand because the flop contained three clubs (many made flushes) and Flash check‑raised big on the flop. The turn J♥ is a non‑club ‘‘safe’’ card that didn’t complete any new club draws, so it’s a good spot to extract thin value from worse A‑x and medium pairs while denying free rivers to weaker holdings. Flash checked the turn, which can be weakness or a trapping line; I want to charge the parts of his range I beat but keep the size reasonable so I’m not forced into a huge commit versus a polarizing raise (made flush/sets). Pot is 10,400 — I’ll bet ~50% pot to get value and fold to large, highly polarized aggression. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (very cautious if a club arrives; continue extracting on safe rivers).

I have flopped a set of fives on a monotone club board. While the flush is a significant concern, especially given Minni's lead on the turn, I have the bottom set and the Ace of clubs is on the board, which blocks the nut-flush possibilities for my opponent. Minni's range for 3-betting pre-flop and calling a flop raise includes strong suited connectors, big pocket pairs, and hands like AJ or AK with a high club. Given that I have 10 outs (three 8s, three Jacks, three Aces, and the final 5) to improve to a full house or quads if she does have a flush, I have significant equity (approximately 22%). If she is holding a hand like AJ or a semi-bluff with the King of clubs, I am a massive favorite. Given the stack sizes, calling the 5,200 essentially commits me to the rest of the chips on the river anyway. Therefore, I will move all-in now to maximize pressure on semi-bluffs and protect against a fifth club or other scary cards on the river.

Flash jammed the turn (raise to 12,250 all‑in). Pot is 27,850 and I must call 7,050, so I need ≈20.2% equity to break even. I have A♦8♦ — two pair on A♣8♣5♣J♥ — which is strong but extremely vulnerable: the flop contained three clubs so the most natural check‑raise/shove line is dominated by made flushes (any two clubs in hand) plus a smaller number of sets/trips (55, 88, AA). Versus a made flush my only clean outs to a full house/quads are the remaining A/8/5 cards (~7 outs ⇒ ~15% on the river); versus sets my equity is even lower. Flash’s line (call pre, big flop check‑raise, shove turn) is heavily polarized toward hands that beat me, and I don’t block the flush combos. Because my realistic equity is below the ~20% break‑even threshold and this shove was exactly the kind of polarized aggression I planned to respect, folding is the +EV play — preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♣6♥ and deep stacks — this is a clear open-raise spot. I want initiative, fold equity, and to avoid giving the big blind cheap flops, so I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing that matches my table image. If Flash 3-bets small I’ll frequently call and play postflop (I have ace-blockers and reasonable postflop playability); I’ll respect a large 3-bet and fold to avoid playing a big pot OOP with a marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. K9o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind in a heads-up scenario, especially given that Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises. With a significant chip lead (27,750 vs 11,850), I can afford to see a flop and use my position and hand strength to navigate the post-flop play. 3-betting is an option, but calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity with a hand that frequently makes top pair or a strong second pair.

I am out of position in a heads-up pot after defending my Big Blind. The flop is Ten-Seven-Deuce rainbow. This board is relatively neutral but favors the pre-flop raiser's range of high cards and overpairs. I have King-high with one overcard and a backdoor spade draw. My best strategy here is to check to the aggressor. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but leading out myself (donk betting) would likely be inefficient as I don't have enough equity to represent a strong hand convincingly on this texture.

I opened preflop and have position; Flash checked the dry T♠ 7♣ 2♠ flop. A♣6♥ is not strong but has a valuable Ace-blocker and good heads‑up equity vs a wide defending range. I want to apply pressure, deny free cards to many of his chasing combos, and extract value from worse turned-over hands, so I’ll continuation-bet about 50% pot. 300 into 600 keeps the pot controllable versus a deeper BB while still offering fold equity. Plan: if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (continue with useful cards or check back to control size); fold or heavily re-evaluate if met with a large raise.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. The flop is Ten-Seven-Deuce rainbow. Minni has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot (a half-pot sized bet). While I haven't connected with the board, I have one overcard (the King) and a backdoor flush draw with the King of Spades. In a heads-up situation, folding King-high to a single c-bet on such a dry board is likely too tight, as Minni could be betting with a wide range of air or weaker overcards. I am getting 3:1 odds to call. I will call this bet to see if I improve on the turn or if Minni checks back on a later street, allowing me to potentially take the pot down or win at showdown.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit on a board of Ten-Seven-Deuce-Eight. On the flop, I called a half-pot continuation bet with King-high and a backdoor spade draw. The turn Eight of Clubs gives me a gutshot straight draw (a Jack would complete a straight). In heads-up play, maintaining a checking range is important, especially when out of position against an aggressive opponent like Minni. I will check to see how Minni reacts to the turn card. If Minni checks back, I likely have decent showdown value with King-high or a free chance to hit my straight. If Minni bets, I will evaluate the pot odds offered to see if calling with my gutshot and overcard is profitable.

I check. On the turn I only have A‑high (A♣6♥) and the 8♣ is a mild blank for my range — it doesn't give me a backdoor spade or pair. Flash called my small c‑bet on the T♠7♣2♠ flop, which indicates a wide continuing range: tens, small pairs, floats with spade draws and some backdoor hands. A second barrel here would be a thin bluff: many of his callings hands (pairs and spade draws) will still continue or call a small bet, and a bet also exposes me to a sizing/raise shove from his much deeper stack. Pot control and position are my priorities with a marginal holding, so I’ll check to realize equity and avoid bloating the pot out of position. Plan: if he checks back we get to showdown; if he bets the river I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing (fold to large pressure, consider calling small/medium bets vs obvious bluffs).

On the river, the board is 10s-7c-2s-8c-6d. I hold the King of Spades and Nine of Hearts, which gives me a straight (6-7-8-9-10). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. On the turn, the action went check-check after Minni's flop continuation bet, which often indicates she might have a marginal pair or was slowing down with a drawing hand. By betting now, I can extract value from her pairs (like a Ten, Eight, or Seven) or any two-pair combinations. While a Jack-Nine would make a higher straight, it is statistically less likely than her having a pair or a missed draw. I will bet 900 into the 1200 pot to maximize value while making the bet look like it could potentially be a missed spade draw or a bluff.

I have A♣6♥ — the river 6♦ pairs my 6 for a single pair (sixes) with a strong Ace kicker. Pot is 2,100 after Flash’s 900 river bet, so a call of 900 requires ~42.9% equity to break even. Flash’s line (called my flop c‑bet, checked the turn, then leads the river ~75% pot) is consistent with a mixed range: value hands (T‑x, 7s, 8s, sets, occasional two‑pairs) and missed hands/float bluffs (overcards, missed spade combos, KQ/KJ-type floats). My pair of sixes + Ace kicker beats a lot of the thin bluffs and missed draws and loses to the obvious value hands. Raising would be highly polarizing and risky — I’m often behind versus his value and would only get action from worse rarely — while folding surrenders too much equity given the pot odds. With a healthy stack and reasonable pot odds, the +EV line is to call and see showdown.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Seven is a strong starting hand. I'm on the button with a significant chip lead. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure on Minni and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up vs a 3x button open with A♠7♠ in the big blind — a strong, playable hand with good blocking and post-flop playability. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to a wide opening range, while keeping the pot manageable given the effective stacks. A 3-bet to 900 (~3x the open) gives solid fold equity vs many weaker Ax, broadways and small pairs, isolates the button, and still leaves plenty of stack for postflop play if called. If Flash 4-bets big (near all-in) I’ll fold; if it’s a reasonable 4-bet sizing I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on size). If called, plan to c-bet favorable flops and use position/aggression to win the pot.

Minni has 3-bet my opening raise to 900. Ace-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to continue with heads-up, especially since I have position and a significant chip lead. Folding is too conservative given the pot odds and my stack size. I will call the 600 difference to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

Heads‑up after my 3‑bet preflop the flop is 3♠ K♥ T♣ — a fairly dry, high card texture. I hold A♠7♠: I have the Ace blocker (reduces combos of strong A‑x like AKo/AQo), modest showdown value as Ace‑high, and a backdoor spade plan. As the preflop aggressor I should apply measured pressure to a wide calling range (lots of missed broadways and small pairs), so I make a small continuation bet to buy the pot now and preserve fold equity. I size ~40% pot (700 into 1800) to fold out unpaired hands while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. Plan: if Flash calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check behind to realize the Ace if checked to, or barrel small again only on favorable turns); if he raises large I’ll fold most of the time since my hand has limited showdown value and the raise indicates strong K/T/pair or a slowplayed big hand.

On a board of Kh-Tc-3s, my Ace-high has very little equity against Minni's pre-flop 3-betting range, which frequently includes Kings, Tens, or big pairs. Minni's lead of 700 into the 1800 pot is a standard continuation bet on a texture that favors her range significantly.
