While I have a massive chip lead, calling here would be a 'float' with no real draw and only a marginal hope that Ace-high is currently the best hand or that I can bluff later streets. Given the board connectivity and the pre-flop action, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than chasing with Ace-seven offsuit on a board where I am likely crushed.

Heads-up on the button with Kd Jc — a strong, playable hand with position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want initiative and fold equity against the big blind’s wide range. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total) — a standard sizing here that builds the pot and pressures weaker hands. Plan: if Flash flats I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play position; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on sizing and read).

I am in the Big Blind with 2s 8d, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. Although I have a significant chip lead and the price to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot, 2-8 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands and performs poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the trashy nature of my cards, there is no reason to defend here.

I am on the button with a significant chip lead over Minni. [Th 5h] is a suited hand that plays well in position. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to maintain aggression and take control of the hand from the button.

Call 300 total (200 more). QJo is a strong, defendable hand heads-up and Flash’s 3x open from the SB is wide. I’m out of position postflop (button will act last), so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal OOP 3‑bet; calling gets ~3:1 pot odds and lets me realize equity with deep stacks (~115bb). Plan: see the flop and play aggressively when I hit (pair, two pair, strong draws) or exploit opponent weakness; on dry boards I’ll give up to heavy action or proceed cautiously.

Flop: 3♠ A♥ K♠. I hold Q♥ J♦ — no pair, only ~6 pair-outs + 4 tens to make Broadway (about 10 outs ~38% to improve to river), but the preflop raiser’s range contains many A/K combos that currently beat me. Pot is 600 and I’m OOP; with deep stacks I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: keep the pot under control and realize equity. If Flash c-bets small I’ll call to see a turn; if they fire big (pot or polarized sizing) I’ll fold. If they check back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn for a delayed bluff or a value improvement. Checking preserves flexibility and avoids committing with a mediocre hand.

I have Ten of Hearts and Five of Hearts on a board of Three of Spades, Ace of Hearts, King of Spades. Minni checked to me. This board is very heavy for a pre-flop raiser's range, containing both an Ace and a King. While I only have middle-low equity with a backdoor heart draw and a gutter (though extremely weak), Minni's check indicates she likely missed or is playing cautiously. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent the Ace or King and take down the pot immediately.

Heads‑up vs the button (Flash) — I’m OOP with Q♦J♦ (Qh Jd). Flop 3♠ A♥ K♠, opponent c‑bets 300 into a 900 pot. My hand has ~10 clean outs (3 Qs + 3 Js + 4 Tens) ≈ 38% to improve by the river, and the c‑bet size gives me ~25% immediate pot odds (300 into a 1200 pot after my call). With deep stacks (~113bb) and a wide SB/button opening/CB range, calling to realize equity and keep the pot manageable OOP is the correct +EV line. I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy raises or large turn bets unless I pair or make the straight/clear equity.

Turn gives me a pair of jacks (Jd) with a Q kicker on 3♠ A♥ K♠ J♠. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range has plenty of A/K value hands that still beat me, but also lots of bluffs and broadways I currently outrank. I’m out of position and stacks are deep — I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand that can be crushed by A/K or a made two‑card spade flush. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in his betting range, and lets me call reasonable turn bets to realize value. Plan: check here; call small/medium bets, fold to very large raises or shoves, and re‑evaluate on the river for a thin value bet or showdown.

The turn card, the Jack of Spades, is a very coordinated card that completes several potential draws and makes the board extremely dangerous. My hand, Ten of Hearts and Five of Hearts, has almost no showdown value and only a thin gutshot draw to a straight (which would also likely complete a flush for my opponent if they hold any spade). Since Minni called my flop bet on an Ace-King high board, she likely has a piece of it or a draw. Firing a second barrel on such a wet board into a range that has already shown interest is risky. I will check back to control the pot size and see a free river card, maintaining my significant chip lead without committing more chips to a hand with low equity.

The river completes A–K–Q–J–T — with Q♥ J♦ in my hand I’ve made the Broadway straight (A–K–Q–J–T). That’s almost always the best hand heads‑up here; the only realistic hands that beat me are a five‑card spade flush (three spades on board) or very rarely another player also holding a Q (which ties). I hold a Q so I block many Q combos, and Flash’s line (preflop open, small c‑bet on the flop, then checking the turn) looks more like A/K, missed broadways or draws rather than a trap flush. With pot = 1,200 I want to extract thin value rather than check back. I’ll make a value bet of ~2/3 pot (800): big enough to get called by worse hands (Ax, Kx, some pairs/missed draws) but not so large that a single raise would commit a disproportionate chunk of my stack to a potential flush. If Flash check‑raises very large I’ll re‑evaluate (a large check‑raise is often a flush and I’d fold to an all‑in), but this sizing maximizes expected value against his likely range.

On a board of 3s-Ah-Ks-Js-Td, there is a four-card straight (A-K-J-T) and three spades. My hand is Th-5h, which only gives me a pair of Tens (pairing the board's Ten). Minni called my flop bet on an Ace-King high board, which suggests she has a piece of the board. After checking the turn, she has now led out for 800 into a 1200 pot on the river. Given the coordination of the board, any Queen makes a straight and any two spades make a flush. Even a simple Ace or King beats my pair of Tens. There is very little chance my hand is best here, and calling a significant bet on such a dangerous board with only a pair of Tens is not profitable.

Heads-up on the button (also small blind) with KcJs — a strong, playable hand that plays well postflop in position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete; rather than limp, I’ll open to a standard size for this table to take the initiative, isolate the BB and deny cheap odds to speculative hands. Raising to 300 (common open size here) is big enough to fold out very weak holdings and keep the pot manageable with deep stacks (12200). Plan: if called I’ll c-bet on dry, favorable textures and play cautiously on highly connected or Ace-runout boards; fold or narrowly continue against a large 3-bet depending on sizing.

I am holding 7s4d, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind. While I have a substantial chip lead and the price to call is only 200 more, playing 7-4 offsuit out of position is generally a losing play. I want to maintain my chip lead as we approach the end of the tournament (hand 122 of 150) and avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Six offsuit is a hand that can be opened from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Given my significant chip lead (27,600 vs 12,250), I want to continue playing aggressively and win small pots without letting Minni see free flops. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button heads-up. I hold J♣3♣ — a marginal but suited hand with backdoor flush/straight potential. Folding surrenders my posted BB and is too tight heads-up; 3-betting light is possible but J3s is too weak out of position to 3-bet profitably here. Calling 200 more (300 total) gives excellent pot odds (~200 to win ~600, ~3:1) and lets me see a flop with decent implied odds and the chance to realize equity. Plan: call preflop, then on the flop continue with any pair, a jack, two pair/sets, or strong club draws; on dry, high-card flops I’ll often check-fold to a standard c-bet, and selectively float or check-raise with strong draws or disguised made hands. Avoid committing big stacks with this hand preflop.

Check. I’m out of position on a paired flop (9♠ 4♥ 9♣) with J♣3♣ — no made hand and only a backdoor club flush/runner-runner potential. Flash opened wide preflop heads‑up and will c‑bet a lot, but this board is poor for a bluff lead OOP: betting here as a bluff hands me a lot of tough decisions on turns and rivers and unnecessarily bloats the pot with very little equity. Checking preserves stack and lets me see how Flash reacts. Plan: check and fold to a standard/sizeable c‑bet; only continue if I pick up meaningful equity (a jack/trey pair, or a club on the turn giving a real draw) or if Flash checks back (then can consider a delayed bluff/value).

On a board of 9s-4h-9c, I have Ten-high. Minni checked to me. This paired board is generally good for the pre-flop aggressor as it is hard to hit. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to try and take it down immediately, representing a Nine, a pocket pair, or even just high-card strength that beats her current holding.

Call 300. The pot is ~900 so calling 300 gives about 4:1 immediate pot odds (need ~20% equity). J♣3♣ has ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (3 Jacks + 3 Treys) — ≈24% to hit by the river — plus roughly a 4% chance of a runner‑runner club flush, so my raw chance to improve (~28%) comfortably beats the break‑even threshold. Flash opened and is likely c‑betting a wide heads‑up range; his bet size here is small and will include bluffs, so calling keeps those hands in and lets me float OOP. Plan: see the turn — if I hit a Jack/3 or pick up a real club draw I will proceed/raise; if I miss and face a meaningful turn bet I will fold. A check‑raise/bluff here is too risky OOP with no current equity, and folding outright surrenders good pot odds, so a simple call is the +EV line.

Board is 9♠ 4♥ 9♣ 4♦ and I hold J♣3♣. I have no pair and my backdoor club draw is now dead (with one card to come I cannot make a five‑card club flush). My only real outs are three Jacks and three Treys (6 outs) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river. Flash checked the turn to me, so I can see a free river and preserve my stack. Betting as a bluff OOP here is risky: I have very little equity, and a bet will often get called by worse hands or raised by any 9/4 or full house (which exist in his range). With healthy stacks and such poor equity, the +EV line is to check and evaluate the river — fold to a meaningful bet if I miss, or consider a value/bluff on the river only if the card and action make it believable. Therefore: check.

The board has double-paired (9-4-9-4), which makes it very static. Since Minni called my flop bet, she likely has some piece of the board, a pocket pair, or an Ace-high. My Ten-high has very little showdown value, but betting again on this texture is unlikely to force a fold from the hands that called the flop (like a small pocket pair or Ace-high). I will check behind to see a free river and potentially evaluate a bluff or a cheap showdown depending on the final card.

I have J♣3♣ on a very wet paired board that now reads 9♠ 4♥ 9♣ 4♦ A♦. I missed the river — my only showdown outs were J/3 which didn't hit — and I have essentially no value. Many hands in Flash’s preflop/c-bet range (any 9 or any 4, plus pocket pairs that turned into full houses/quads) beat me, and hands that might fold to a bluff (weak offsuit overcards) will often call a small river bluff here heads‑up. With both players deep and the turn checked, bluffing OOP into a player who opened and c‑bet the flop is thin EV. Best line is to check and take the free showdown; avoid bloating the pot with a marginal bluff. If Flash now bets, I’ll fold to a reasonable-sized river bet — proceed to showdown only if it checks through.

The board is double-paired with an Ace (9-9-4-4-A). My Ten-Six does not improve this board, so I am playing the board as my best five-card hand (Two Pair, Nines and Fours, with an Ace kicker). Any opponent hand that includes an Ace, a Nine, a Four, or a pocket pair of Fives or higher beats me. Since Minni called the flop and has checked through the turn and river, she likely has a hand that either beats me or chops with the board (like K-high or Q-high). There is no value in betting here, as better hands (like an Ace or a pocket pair) will not fold, and hands that are chopping will likely call. Checking is the most sensible play to reach a showdown or a chop.

Heads-up on the button with pocket tens is a clear value/standard-open. I’ve already posted the SB (50); calling for 50 is too passive and lets the BB see cheap flops with a wide range. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) to build the pot, get value from worse hands (broadways, suited connectors, Ax) and to earn fold equity against weaker holdings. With 12,300 behind this sizing keeps the pot controlled while leaving plenty of stack for postflop play. If Flash calls I’ll play the flop aggressively when it’s favorable and proceed cautiously to big action; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and tendencies (likely call smaller 3-bets, fold to very large overbets).

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 2d, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. I have a significant stack advantage (27,550 vs 12,050). However, 8-2 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. While I could defend against a wide button range, it's better to conserve chips and wait for a more favorable spot given that I am in the lead and Minni is the shorter stack.

I have JTo on the button in a heads-up situation. My stack is significantly larger than Minni's (27,500 vs 12,350). JTo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average range. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand.

3♦5♥ is a very weak, out-of-position hand vs a SB open. A 3-bet is unnecessary and poorly blocked, so I won't bluff-raise. Folding would be too passive given the pot odds (200 to call into ~400 — about 33% equity required) and the SB's opening range is fairly wide. I’ll call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable. Postflop plan: fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, but continue/raise if I hit a pair, two pair, or a strong straight/flush draw.

Flop: 5♦ A♦ 2♥; I hold 3♦5♥ — second pair (fives) with a useless backdoor diamond. Flash opened from the button so his continuing range is wide but contains a lot of A‑x combos that crush me. Out of position with a vulnerable pair, I don’t want to face a big check‑raise or barrel myself off my stack by leading. Checking controls the pot, lets me see what Flash does, and preserves my ability to call modest c‑bets from bluffs or worse hands. Plan: check now. If Flash fires a small‑to‑medium c‑bet I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn (fold to large, polarizing raises). If he checks back we go to showdown or look to extract value on later streets only if I improve or he shows weakness.

On this Ace-high flop of Ad-5d-2h, I have Jack-high with a backdoor straight draw. Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. While this board favors the pre-flop raiser's range, especially since I can easily have Aces, Minni has shown a willingness to defend and check-call. Given the texture and the fact that I have zero current showdown value, a small continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate to represent the Ace and try to take the pot down immediately. If called, I have some equity to improve or check back on later streets.

Flop: 5♦ A♦ 2♥. I hold 3♦5♥ — second pair (5s) with a small backdoor diamond. Flash opened wide from the button and now fires a modest c‑bet of 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds. Out of position and facing a wide continuing range that contains plenty of bluffs and weaker pairs, I prefer to call to control the pot, realize equity, and keep worse hands in. I won’t check‑raise with a vulnerable pair; I’ll fold to a large, polarized raise on the turn without improvement, but will call small/medium turn barrels and value‑bet/raise if I hit trips or two pair. Calling 300 is the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play here.

I have second pair (5s) with a one-card flush draw on the turn. The Qd completed a three-diamond board (5d Ad Qd) so I now have 9 diamond outs to a flush and 2 additional outs to trips (the remaining 5s) — ~11 outs total ≈ 24% to improve on the river. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop, so his continuing range contains many Ax, some Qx, and both single- and two-card diamond hands. Betting here would build the pot with a marginal hand that loses to the most of his value range and is crushed by any made flush (or by an A/Q). I also have limited fold equity and would be vulnerable to a check-raise/raise shove from a big stack. Pot control and free showdown equity are the priority — check and see a river. Plan: if Flash fires a small-to-medium river bet I can call with my pair+draw; fold to a large, polarized bet unless I hit. If he checks back I’ll see the river for free and re-evaluate only if I improve.

The turn is the Queen of Diamonds, which puts three diamonds on the board and adds another overcard. I have Jack-high with no pair and no flush draw. Minni called my flop bet on an Ace-high board, which strongly suggests she has an Ace, a pair of fives/deuces, or a diamond draw. While the Queen is a good card for my perceived range, Minni's calling range on the flop is quite sticky. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see a free river card. If she checks again on the river, I might consider a bluff depending on the card, but right now, I have very little equity and don't want to inflate the pot against a range that likely contains an Ace.

I missed my river outs — I only have second pair (5s) with 3♦5♥ on 5♦ A♦ 2♥ Q♦ 6♣. Flash’s line (preflop open, small flop c-bet, check on turn and check on river) represents a wide range that contains a lot of A‑x and some bluffs; many of his hands beat my pair and my fold equity is low. Betting here would build the pot with a vulnerable hand that is often behind to any Ace or a made diamond flush, and it risks getting check‑raised or called by many better hands. With the pot small relative to stacks and no river improvement, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to take showdown — check and show. If Flash had fired a small river bet I’d consider calling; I’d fold to a large polarized shove.

On a board of Ad-5d-2h-Qd-6c, my Jack-high holds almost zero showdown value. Minni check-called my continuation bet on the Ace-high flop, which suggests she has a piece of the board, likely an Ace, a middle pair, or a diamond draw. Although she checked both the turn and the river, her calling range on the flop is likely to contain hands that will call a river bet to see a showdown, especially given the pot size relative to her stack. Since I have a significant chip lead and it is late in the tournament (hand 125 of 150), I will opt to check back and concede the pot rather than attempting a high-risk bluff against a range that is likely to have me beat.

Heads-up on the button (also SB) with KcTh — a strong, playable hand in position. I want initiative and fold equity vs the big blind, and the table standard here is a ~3x open. With a deep 130 BB stack I’ll keep the sizing reasonable to allow post‑flop play. I raise to 300 (3×BB) to narrow Flash’s range, build the pot when ahead, and play positionally if called. Plan: fold to a very large 3‑bet (near pot or shove sizes), call smaller 3‑bets and proceed post‑flop with aggression on favorable boards or pot‑control on bad ones.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Minni has raised to 3x from the small blind/button. Given our stack sizes and the heads-up nature of the game at this stage, J9o is a standard hand to defend the big blind with. I have a significant chip lead and can afford to see a flop. Calling is better than folding or raising here as it keeps the pot controlled with a medium-strength holding while seeing if I can connect with the board.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on a board of Jc-5d-3d. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to her to see if she follows up with a continuation bet. This allows her to bluff with a wider range of hands or bet with draws that I currently beat. I plan to check-call most standard bet sizes to keep the pot manageable while I have a strong but not invincible hand.

I was the preflop aggressor (opened to 300) and Flash checked the dry-ish J♣‑5♦‑3♦ flop. Heads‑up this is a great spot to continuation‑bet: his check range contains lots of weak hands and small pairs I can fold out, and my K♣T♥ has one overcard (K) plus some showdown value. There are diamond backdoors on the board so I don’t want to give free turns, but I also don’t need an oversized bet. I’ll c‑bet about half the pot to generate fold equity, charge draws, and get value from worse hands that will call. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if called I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and barrel when I improve or the texture favors aggression, otherwise control the pot.

I have flopped top pair on a relatively safe board. Minni's bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet that she would make with a wide range of hands, including bluffs and draws. By calling, I keep her range wide and allow her to continue bluffing on future streets while keeping the pot size manageable. Raising here might fold out hands I currently dominate and would only get action from better hands or strong draws. I'll call and see how the turn develops.

I have trip Jacks with a nine kicker on a board of Jc-5d-3d-Js. This is a very strong hand. Since I have been check-calling and Minni has been the aggressor, I will check again on the turn. This allows her to continue betting with her bluffs, semi-bluffs like diamond draws, or smaller pocket pairs. Leading out now might reveal the strength of my hand and cause her to fold hands that I currently dominate. I plan to check-call or potentially check-raise depending on her bet size.

Turn pairs the board (Js). I have KcTh — essentially K‑high with only a few river outs (Ks/Kh/Kd and the Tens), and no meaningful backdoor draw. Flash called my half‑pot flop c‑bet and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with either a trapped Jack (now trips) or with a small pair/draw that missed. Betting here would often put me in tough spots vs trips or get called by better showdown hands, so I want to control the pot and use position to see the river for cheap. Check behind to avoid being check‑raised/bluffed off the hand and preserve the option to bluff or value on the river depending on the card and Flash’s action.

I have quad Jacks, which is the absolute nuts on this board. The board shows three Jacks and I hold the fourth in my hand. After Minni checked the turn, she may be holding a pocket pair or an Ace, giving her a full house (Jacks full of whatever her kicker or pair is). Since she checked behind on the turn, she might check back the river if I check again. To extract value, I will lead out with a half-pot bet of 600. This size is large enough to get value from a full house but small enough that it might be interpreted as a thin value bet or a bluff, potentially inducing a call or even a raise.

I call 600. The river bet is very small (600 into a 1800 pot) so I only need ~25% equity to break even. Flash’s line (check-call on the flop, check on the turn, small bet on the river) is ambiguous — it contains both thin value (sets that turned into full houses, trips/quads in a few combos) and missed-draw/medium-card bluffs. Given the tiny sizing and the fact I blocked some overcards, folding here surrenders good pot odds to bluffs; a raise would be a pure, high-variance bluff against a range that contains many full houses/quads and thin value hands. I’ll take the cheap look and call to realize equity — losing 600 to a legitimate full house is acceptable versus winning a decent pot when Flash is bluffing.

I have been dealt Ace of Hearts, Trey of Hearts (Ah3h) on the Button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand in this context, offering both high-card value and strong post-flop playability (nut flush potential). With a significant chip lead over Minni (28,100 to 11,750), I want to play aggressively and exert pressure. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot early and capitalize on my positional advantage.

Facing a button open to 300 with 3♣2♦ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and it costs 200 to call, so I need ~33% equity to make a break-even call (200 / (400+200) ≈ 33%). 3-2 offsuit is extremely weak, often dominated by a button’s opening range and very hard to play out of position post-flop. A thin 3-bet (min to 500) would be a weak bluff with little fold equity against a wide, aggressive opponent and a larger 3-bet would unnecessarily risk chips with a poor hand. With 117+ BB effective and only 23 hands left in the tournament, preserving stack and waiting for a better spot is the highest EV line. Fold preflop.

Heads-up on the button with Kc7d. Stacks are deep (~117bb vs 281bb) and I have positional advantage postflop, so I want to take the initiative. K7o is a reasonable open in heads-up — I’ll raise to 300 total (includes my 50 SB) which is a standard ~3x BB open: it gets fold equity, avoids giving a cheap flop, and keeps postflop maneuverability. Plan: take the pot down preflop if Flash folds; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position (c-bet dry boards, control pot size vs aggression). Fold to large 3-bets with this hand; consider calling small 3-bets only with favorable odds or SPR.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have a significant chip lead (28,150 vs 11,450) and the pot odds are 2:1 to call, 8-2 is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Defending such a hand out of position usually results in losing more chips post-flop. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip advantage.

I currently hold a significant chip lead over Minni (28,100 to 11,750). While 5c 4d is a weak hand, raising from the button/small blind in a heads-up scenario is a standard strategy to exert pressure and take down the pot pre-flop. Minni has shown a tendency to fold to several open raises recently (Hands 128, 127, 124, 122). I will raise to 300 to continue this trend and maintain control of the table pace.

Heads-up in the big blind with Q♦J♣ against a standard open to 300: this is a strong, playable hand but I'm out of position postflop. Flash opens a lot and has a bigger stack, so a 3-bet OOP will often get called and can bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Calling keeps the pot under control, realizes equity against a wide opening range, and lets me proceed postflop with a plan (raise/lead when I connect well, fold or pot-control when I miss). Pot odds are acceptable to defend, so I will just call the 200 more (300 total committed) and evaluate the flop.

Flop A♣-8♠-2♦; I hold Q♦J♣ and missed. Flash opens wide from the button, so his range has a lot of Aces and many weaker hands, but I’m out of position and have poor equity vs any Ax or overpair. I called pre to keep the pot under control — I should do the same now. Check to control the pot and realize equity; if Flash bets small I can call and re-evaluate on the turn, but I’ll fold to a large bet/raise since QJ has poor equity vs strong made hands. If he checks back I’ll look to take a free card or bluff on a favorable turn. Preserve my 11,550 stack rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand OOP.

As the pre-flop aggressor, an Ace-high board (2d 8s Ac) is very favorable for my perceived range. Minni's check indicates she likely hasn't connected strongly with the board. I will place a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) to represent the Ace and attempt to take the pot down immediately. If she calls or raises, my hand (5-high with a weak gutshot draw) is easily foldable.

Heads-up I’m out of position with Q♦J♣ on A♣-8♠-2♦ — I completely missed. Flash is opening wide and c-bets small (~1/3 pot), so his range contains many A‑x, some small pairs, and lots of air. I checked to control the pot and keep weaker hands and bluffs in. Raising OOP here with QJ is thin and will bloat the pot with poor equity; folding is too weak since I beat much of his bluff/weak c‑bet range. I’ll call the 300 to realize equity, keep pot manageable (pot will be ~1.5k), and re-evaluate on the turn: continue/raise if I hit a Q/J or the turn is favorable, fold to heavy aggression on a blank turn.

Out of position with Q♦J♣ on a turn that paired the Ace (board A♣-A♦-8♠-2♦). I completely missed and am crushed by any Ax and many value hands in Flash’s wide opening/c-bet range. I called the flop to keep the pot small and realize equity; on the turn I should continue to control the pot rather than bluffing out of position with a marginal hand. I will check to see what Flash does. Plan: if Flash checks back I get a free river and can evaluate; if he bets a smallish amount I can call to realize equity vs some bluffs, but I will fold to large turn bets/raises that commit a significant portion of my stack. Preserve chips and avoid bloating the pot with QJ off-suit OOP.

I am currently holding 5-high with an inside straight draw (needs a 3). On the flop, I represented the Ace with a continuation bet and Minni called, suggesting she likely has a piece of the board (perhaps an 8, a low pocket pair, or a weak Ace). The turn pairs the Ace. While this is often a good card to bluff because it is mathematically harder for her to have an Ace, her call on the flop means she is likely prepared to go to showdown with whatever she has. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, see a free river card to potentially hit my gutshot, and avoid being check-raised off my marginal equity.

Board: 2♦ 8♠ A♣ A♦ 8♣. I hold J♣ Q♦ — no pair, no backdoor. Flash opened pre, c-bet a small flop bet and we called; both checked the turn. The river paired the 8, which now gives any Ace or any 8 in Flash’s range a full house. My hand has effectively no showdown value beyond the board. Betting here would be a pure bluff that needs significant fold equity versus a player who called the flop — but A/8 combos (which beat me) will never fold and many weaker holdings that might fold aren’t frequent enough to make a bluff +EV. With pot = 1,200 and I have no value, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-return line is to check and go to showdown (or fold to a river bet). I’ll check and preserve my stack rather than bluff into a range that often has full houses.

The board has double-paired (Aces and Eights), meaning both players currently hold Two Pair, Aces and Eights. The winner will be determined by the fifth card (the kicker). My highest hole card is a 5, which is an extremely weak kicker in this situation. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop bet, which strongly suggests she has at least some high-card value (K, Q, or J) or a piece of the board (an 8 or a 2). Almost any hand she would call with has me beaten on the kicker or has improved to a Full House (if she has an 8 or an Ace). Since I have almost no showdown value and a bluff is unlikely to succeed given her interest in the pot on the flop, checking behind is the most prudent play to minimize losses.

Heads-up on the button with Tc5c (suited, medium-low strength). Pot is 150 and a call is only 50, but in heads-up position is a big edge and I want to take the initiative. I’ll open to 300 (standard 3xBB / table sizing) to get fold equity, build a pot I can play postflop in position, and avoid giving the BB a cheap free flop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand most of the time; vs a small or medium 3-bet I may call given the deep stacks and implied odds. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable club/low-connected flops, otherwise proceed cautiously and leverage position.

I am currently holding Jack-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind, which has been a consistent pattern for them. While I have a significant chip lead (over 2:1) and there are only 20 hands remaining in the tournament, J2o is one of the weakest hands in the range. Defending out of position with such a weak hand is generally unprofitable, even against a wide opening range. Since my primary goal is to maintain my stack lead to ensure a tournament victory, I will fold this hand and wait for a more favorable spot to engage.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant advantage. Holding 4c 2c on the button in a heads-up situation, I want to maintain aggression and apply pressure to Minni's stack. Although 42s is a weak hand, its suitedness provides some post-flop potential, and a standard 3x raise often forces folds or allows me to take control of the pot early.

Heads-up vs Flash who opened to 300 from the SB. I’m in the BB with 7♠10♦. Pot is ~400 and I must call 200 (i.e. commit 300 total) which gives me ~33% pot odds. Against a wide heads-up opening range this hand has reasonable equity and good implied odds with deep stacks. 3‑betting here with T7o is suboptimal — I’d be OOP and often dominated; a minimum 3‑bet to 500 is too thin. I’ll flat-call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and proceed cautiously: fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, continue with pair/strong draws or favorable runouts, and use position reads for later streets.

I have T♦7♠ on Tc-7h-6d — made two pair (Tens and Sevens). Heads‑up vs a wide SB opener, this is a very strong flop hand: the board is relatively dry (no flush), and the only real villains that currently beat me are rare sets (66/77) or the uncommon 9-8 straight. Against Flash’s wide raising range a lot of his holdings (single tens, single sevens, weaker pairs, overcards) will call a value bet. I want to extract value and deny a free turn that could pair or complete backdoor possibilities, so I’ll bet for value — sizing to about 2/3 pot to get called by worse and charge any turn cards that could help him. If he raises big I’ll reassess (large raise often indicates a set or the rare made straight).

I have 4-high on a board that likely connects with Minni's defending range. She led into me for 400, which suggests she either has a piece of the board or a strong draw. With only 20 hands left in the tournament and a commanding chip lead, I see no reason to contest this pot with no immediate equity and very limited backdoor potential. Folding preserves my stack and avoids unnecessary variance against a shorter stack who is showing aggression.

Heads-up on the button with 5♦9♥ — a weak but playable hand. In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range to apply pressure and use positional advantage postflop. A minimum raise to 200 is a low-risk attempt to steal the blind and build initiative while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand (5-9o has poor equity vs a strong 3-betting range); if he calls I can play the flop in position and c-bet/fold based on texture. Raise to 200 to seize initiative and maximize fold equity.

Minni has been raising the button frequently to pick up the blinds. While Q4o is a weak hand, it is facing a min-raise, offering me 3:1 odds to call. However, Q4o performs poorly out of position and has low playability. Given my significant chip lead (27,050 vs 12,650) and the fact that we are approaching the end of the tournament (hand 132 of 150), I prefer to maintain my lead by avoiding marginal spots out of position with weak kickers. I will wait for a better hand to challenge her aggression.

I am on the button with J2o. This is a weak hand, but in heads-up play, the button should be raising or folding a high percentage of the time. However, Minni has been quite active and is short-stacked relative to me. Folding is safe, but calling the extra 50 is an option. Given the hand strength and the goal of preserving the lead, folding is the most disciplined choice to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if she decides to raise, or simply to avoid losing chips with a hand that has poor equity.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Tc Jd. With only Flash in the BB and deep stacks (~129 BB), JTo is a strong, playable hand—I want the initiative and fold equity rather than passively seeing a flop. A standard 3BB open (to 300) is bigger than the min-raise and discourages too-wide defenses/squeezes while still keeping the pot manageable for postflop play. Calling 50 is too passive; folding surrenders a blind I’ve already posted. I will raise to 300 (total chips committed) to take control of the hand and leverage position.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a stack of 26,900 against her 12,700, I have a significant chip lead. T8o is a decent hand to defend in a heads-up situation, especially getting 2:1 on a call. Minni has been quite active with pre-flop raises, and I want to see a flop to potentially outplay her or hit a piece of the board without over-committing pre-flop.

I have an open-ended straight draw with T8 on a 4-9-7 rainbow board. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I will check to the pre-flop raiser to see her action. This allows me to either see a free turn card or potentially check-call to realize my equity.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called; pot is 600 on 4s 9h 7d. With Tc Jd I have two live overcards plus a gutshot to an 8 (4 outs, ~16% to hit by the river). Flash checked from the BB, so his range is wide (pairs, underpairs, missed overcards). I want to use my initiative: a continuation bet pressures his marginal holdings and extracts value when I pick up equity. I’ll make a half‑pot c-bet (300) — big enough to get folds from weak hands but small enough to keep the pot controllable with only ~16% raw draw equity. If he raises large I’ll re-assess (likely fold to a big 3-bet; consider calling a small raise depending on sizing).

I have an open-ended straight draw with T8 on a 4-9-7 rainbow board. Minni has bet 300 into a 600 pot. To call, I need to put in 300 to win a total pot of 1200, which gives me 3:1 (or 25%) pot odds. My probability of hitting the straight by the river is approximately 31.5%, which makes this a mathematically profitable call based on pot equity alone, not even considering implied odds if I hit. Since I am the chip leader, I will call to see the turn and try to realize my equity.

I am currently out of position on the turn with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). The deuce of diamonds is a relatively blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Given that I am acting first post-flop in this heads-up match, I will check to Minni to see if she continues her aggression. This allows me to see how she values her hand and gives me the opportunity to potentially realize my equity by calling a reasonably sized bet, while keeping the pot size under control.

Deep-stacked heads‑up, pot is 1,200 and I’ve got Tc Jd. After my half‑pot flop c‑bet I was called — Flash’s range is wide (top pairs like 9x, some 7/4s, underpairs, floaters and occasional draws). The turn 2d is a blank for my primary equity but it does give me extra backdoor possibilities: I have 3 tens + 3 jacks = 6 outs to make a pair, plus 4 eights to complete the gutshot straight (10 unique outs ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river; only ~8.7% for the straight). With a marginal hand and position, I want pot control and to realize equity rather than barrel again into a range that will call with many better hands. A second barrel here will often be called by 9x or two pair/sets and risks bloating a pot where I’m a big dog most rivers. I’ll check back, keep the pot manageable, and plan to value-bet or bluff on the river depending on the card (bet for value if I pair or hit a favorable runout; consider a delayed bluff only if the river and opponent’s behavior suggest it can get folds).

I missed my open-ended straight draw on the river, leaving me with Ten-high and zero showdown value. The river brought an Ace, which is a classic scare card. However, my previous actions (check-calling the flop and checking the turn) don't strongly represent an Ace. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely has a marginal pair (like a 7 or a 9) or a missed draw of her own. While a bluff could potentially force a fold from her weakest pairs, Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky in past hands. Given my substantial chip lead, I will check and concede the pot if she bets, rather than risking chips on a low-frequency bluff.

Heads‑up on the button, pot is 1,200 and I hold Tc Jd. I c‑bet the flop, was called, then both of us checked the turn. The river A♠ completes the board and I have missed — no pair, no reliable showdown value. Flash checked the river, which usually indicates a check‑down line with at least some showdown hands (underpairs, 9x/7x, or occasionally A‑x) or a pot‑control line with a made hand. A river bluff would need substantial fold equity to be +EV (e.g. a 600 bet needs >33% folds, 400 needs >25%); given his passive line after calling the flop, his range likely contains too many hands that won't fold (pairs and A‑x), and I don't block many of his bluff combos. With no value and limited fold equity, the lowest variance, highest EV play is to check and take it to showdown. If he fires a sizable river bet I will fold — calling one small probe is possible only if sizing is tiny, but overall I prefer to conserve chips and avoid bluffing into a checking opponent who defended the flop.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with a significant chip lead over Minni. Ten-Deuce suited is a weak hand, but in heads-up play, it's standard to open a wide range from the button to maintain pressure and pick up the blinds. I will put in a standard raise to 300. If Minni shows aggression with a 3-bet, I can easily fold without hurting my stack significantly.

Heads-up vs a small‑blind open to 300. I hold Tc8h — a marginal but playable hand in the big blind. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (300 total), so I only need ~33% equity to make the call. Against a wide SB opening range Tc8o has roughly that equity and with very deep stacks I have good implied odds if I connect. A minimum raise to 500 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and I don't want to inflate the pot out of position with a speculative hand; a larger 3‑bet would commit too much with marginal equity. I’ll call, see a flop, and then fold to heavy pressure when I miss or play aggressively if I hit/realize fold equity on favorable textures.

Out of position with Tc8h on Qs9c7c: I have no pair, only a weak runner-runner club possibility and straight outs (J or 6 give me a completed straight by the river — ~8 outs overall, ~31% to improve by river). Leading out OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and has little fold equity. I’ll check and keep the pot under control: call a small continuation bet to realize equity and try to hit, fold to heavy pressure, and re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up a pair or a clear draw.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a flop of Q-9-7 with two clubs. Although my hand is only Ten-high, a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) is a high-frequency play here to take down the pot immediately against a wide range of hands that missed. If Minni calls or raises, I can evaluate the turn, but for now, I'm representing a strong hand or a good draw.

The flop is Qs-9c-7c and I hold Tc-8h — I have a made ten-high club flush. Flash’s small ~half-pot continuation bet (300 into the ~600 pot) is standard and his raising range contains lots of non-club hands (Qx, 9x, medium pairs, broadways) plus some club combos (Kc, Ac, Jc, Qc) that would beat me. Calling 300 is cheap relative to the pot and keeps worse hands and thin value hands in the pot; a small raise will often fold the hands I want value from and a large raise unnecessarily bloats the pot where I could be behind to higher clubs. With deep stacks I prefer to call, control the size of the pot, and re-evaluate on the turn (raise for value if he gives a blank or if he checks, be cautious if he shows heavy strength/raises). Call 300.

I’m first to act on the turn with Tc8h on 9c Qs 7c Qc. I have no pair but a very strong drawing hand: 9 remaining clubs + 8 distinct straight cards (Js or 6s) with a 2-card overlap (Jc/6c) → 15 unique outs (~15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve by the river). Pot is 1,200 and I face no bet right now, so a semi‑bluff is the right play — I want to (1) take down the pot now against Kx/Ax/mediumpairs that will fold, (2) get called by worse hands where my ~33% equity is slightly ahead of the break‑even needed if called. A bet of ~2/3 pot (800) gives good fold equity but keeps the price reasonable if I get called; if Flash check‑raises big I’ll respect that line (likely trips/sets) and fold to avoid committing with only a draw. Betting 800 balances fold equity and positive equity when called, while preserving most of my 13k stack for later hands.

Minni leading out on the turn after calling my continuation bet on the flop is a strong signal. The turn card pairs the Queen and completes several possible club flush draws. With only Ten-high and no real equity or draws, there is no justification for calling or raising. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a high-variance spot against an opponent who is showing aggression on a board that likely connected with their range.

Heads-up, I'm on the button (small blind) so I get positional advantage postflop. 7♥4♣ is a very weak, marginal holding, but the price to see a flop is tiny — I only need to put another 50 to make my total 100. I’ll call to keep the pot small, use position to realize equity and fold to strong aggression on dry, high-card boards. Raising would be too loose with this rag and could put me in tough spots vs a wide but capable big blind; folding is too tight for the cheap price. Plan: continue only with meaningful improvement (pairs, two pair, strong draws) and proceed cautiously otherwise.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. Minni, on the button, has merely completed the small blind to 100. This often indicates a wider, potentially weaker range, or an attempt to see a cheap flop. King-high has decent showdown value and playability in a heads-up pot. Since I have already contributed 100 to the pot and it is only 100 more to see the flop (actually 0 more as it's a check), checking is the most standard play here to see a free flop with a hand that has high-card strength but isn't strong enough to raise for value or as a bluff.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker on a very low board (5-4-2). In a limped pot, my goal is to keep the pot small and reach showdown cheaply, as my hand is likely ahead of many of Minni's air hands but behind any piece of the board or pocket pairs. Checking is the most standard and effective play here to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the flop.

I connected with the flop — I have a pair of fours (4c + 4d) on 5♠-4♦-2♥. Heads-up on the button I have positional advantage and this qualifies as a meaningful improvement from my preflop plan. Flash checked, showing a wide, often weaker range, so I should seize initiative: a value/protection bet extracts calls from A/K/Q/J-high hands and small pairs, and denies free cards to overcards or running straight draws (e.g. a 3 or 6). I also want to keep the pot controlled because my pair is vulnerable to 5x or sets. Bet half-pot (100 into 200) is the right balance — good immediate EV, keeps the pot manageable, and gives a clear decision if Flash check-raises big (I’ll fold to large check-raises, call smaller ones and re-evaluate on the turn).

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a very dry board. In a limped pot, Minni's range is wide, and her small bet of 100 into a 200 pot represents a wide range of hands, including total air, straight draws (A3, 36, 67), or marginal pieces of the board. My hand is strong enough to call one street to see if I can improve or reach a cheap showdown. Raising would likely only be called by better hands (higher pairs, sets), and folding is too tight given the pot odds and my pair. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to evaluate the turn.

On the turn, the 8 of clubs is largely a blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold bottom pair with a King kicker. In a limped pot, my goal is to keep the pot small and reach showdown with my marginal hand. Checking is the most effective play here to control the pot size and allow Minni to continue bluffing with her air hands or check back if she also has a marginal hand. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the bet size, but for now, checking is the standard move to maximize my efficiency with a weak-medium strength hand.

I have second pair (4s from my 4♣ + board 4♦) and position. I bet the flop and got a call, so Flash’s range is wide (overcards, small pairs like 5x/2x, some pocket pairs, occasional draws). The turn 8♣ didn’t change my hand much and Flash checked, so I want to extract thin value and deny a free river card while keeping the pot controllable because my pair is vulnerable to 8x or sets. A half-pot continuation bet is the right balance: 200 into the ~400 pot will get called by worse hands and fold pure air, but won’t overcommit me. If Flash check-raises big I’ll seriously consider folding; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the river and pot-control or extract further when appropriate.

I hold bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker on a 5-4-2-8 board. In a limped pot, Minni's range is extremely wide. Her betting pattern of 100 on the flop and 200 on the turn (half-pot bets) is consistent with a wide range of hands, including straight draws (3x, 67, A3), overcards, or perhaps a weak middle pair. With pot odds of 3:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 800), my bottom pair has enough equity against her semi-bluffs and air to justify a call. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final card and her subsequent action, aiming to reach a cheap showdown.

I hold bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker. The board has run out 5-4-2-8-Q. In this limped pot, I have check-called small bets on both the flop and the turn, which characterizes my hand as a marginal bluff-catcher. The Queen on the river is an overcard that doesn't significantly help my hand but could potentially be a scare card. I should check to Minni to see if she wants to check behind with her own marginal hands or if she continues to bet. Leading out here doesn't serve much purpose as I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I want to reach a cheap showdown to see if my pair of Deuces is sufficient to win the pot.

Heads-up on the button with second pair (4s with 7 kicker) and position. Flash called both flop and turn but checked the river — his range is wide: small pairs (5x, 2x), some Ax/Kx, missed overcards, and occasional slow-played pairs. The Q on the river is an overcard that can have given him top pair, but his check makes that less likely to be a strong value line. A modest half-pot bet (~400 into 800) will extract value from worse pairs and some Ax hands while folding out pure air, and it keeps the pot controllable if he reacts. If he check-raises big I’ll assume a stronger hand and fold. Bet 400 to get thin value and avoid bloating the pot with a vulnerable hand.

On the river, the board is 5-4-2-8-Q. I hold bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker. Minni has followed a consistent betting line of half-pot on the flop (100 into 200), turn (200 into 400), and now the river (400 into 800). While the pot odds are 3:1, requiring 25% equity to call, triple-barreling in a limped pot typically indicates a hand with value rather than a pure bluff. My pair of Deuces is at the very bottom of my range and is beaten by any other pair (4s, 5s, 8s, Qs, or pocket pairs). While Minni could be bluffing with missed straight draws like 67 or A3, her persistence across three streets suggests she is more likely to have a marginal made hand that beats my bottom pair. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.
I hold Ace-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure, taking advantage of my position and card strength.

I’m in the big blind with pocket tens facing a 300 open from the button. Heads-up the opener’s range is wide and TT is well ahead of most of it, but vulnerable to overcards if I just call. I want value and to apply pressure/deny equity to his speculative holdings, so I’ll 3‑bet. Raising to 1,200 (total) is a clear value/isolating sizing — large enough to get folding equity and extract from worse pairs and Ax/Kx while keeping plenty of stack behind to play postflop. I’ll reevaluate if he 4‑bets (fold or proceed based on sizing and reads).

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has 3-bet to 1200. While I could 4-bet here to exert pressure, calling is a very strong play in position. It keeps Minni's bluffs in her range and allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop to navigate the hand. Given my significant stack lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament, calling the 1200 total keeps the pot manageable while I still hold a high-equity hand.

I 3‑bet preflop to 1,200 and was called, so heads‑up my perceived range is strong. I hold T♦T♣ — a solid one‑pair hand that currently beats a lot of his calling range (broadways, many Ax/Kx, many small pairs and suited connectors), though it is vulnerable to 9x/sets and there are two diamonds on board (7♦‑4♠‑9♦) so some flush draws exist. Given that, I want to both extract value from worse hands and deny equity to draws. A standard 50% pot c‑bet (~1,200 into 2,400) is the right balance — credible as the 3‑bettor, charges diamonds/straight draws and gets called by worse pairs/Ax, while leaving room to fold/raise on a turn reaction. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (barrel non‑diamond/low cards for protection/value, check pot‑control if a diamond or a clean overcard comes). If he check‑raises large (big polarizing raise/shove) I’ll give it up — that line will usually show very strong holdings (sets/9x/A♦x).

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit, which is still a very strong hand in heads-up play even after missing the flop. Minni's lead of 1200 into a 2400 pot is a standard continuation bet after her pre-flop 3-bet. The board (7-4-9) is relatively low and doesn't heavily favor a 3-betting range. By calling, I maintain my positional advantage and can see a turn card to see if I improve or if Minni slows down, which would allow me to potentially take the pot away later. With a significant chip lead, I can afford to see another card to determine the strength of her hand without overcommitting.

I 3‑bet preflop with TT and continued on the flop; villain called. The turn Q♦ is both a clean overcard to my tens and the third diamond on board. That card materially improves hands that beat me (Qx, sets) and is the card that gives any two‑diamond holding a made flush on the turn. I currently have a pair of tens with a four‑card diamond combination (need a river diamond to make a flush), so my hand is now marginal versus the turn range. I want to control the pot out of position and keep weaker hands and bluffs in — betting here would bloat the pot and commit me more often when the Q♦ realistically helped villain. I’ll check to keep options open: if Flash checks back I can target a safe river; if he bets small I can call to deny equity and see a river; if he fires a large bet or raises I’ll be prepared to fold because that line usually represents the hands that now beat me (Qx, sets or two‑diamond flushes).

Flash holds Ace-high (Ac Jh) on a board of 7d-4s-9d-Qd. After Minni 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, her check on the turn suggests either a trap with a strong hand like a flush or a loss of momentum with a hand like AK or a medium pocket pair that is now wary of the third diamond or the overcard Queen. Ace-high has decent showdown value in heads-up play, and checking back ensures Flash sees the river for free without risking a check-raise. This keeps the pot size manageable and avoids unnecessary variance given Flash's significant chip lead.

I have TT on 7♦-4♠-9♦-Q♦-Jc. I 3‑bet pre and c‑bet the flop; Flash called both and then checked the turn and checked the river. His line is consistent with medium pairs (9x, 7x, 4x), Kx/Ax that missed or pot‑controlled, and the occasional slow‑played Qx — a made diamond flush is unlikely since he never bet the turn when the board went three‑diamond. TT has decent thin showdown value versus a large portion of his calling range and will still beat many holdings that will check‑back. I want to extract value from worse pairs and K/A type hands while not bloating the pot against the smaller range of hands that beat me. A half‑pot river value bet (~2400 into 4800) is the right balance: big enough to get called by worse and small enough to fold out only the bluffs I’m happy to let go. I’ll bet 2400.

I hold Ace-Jack (Jh Ac) on a board of 7d-4s-9d-Qd-Jc, giving me second pair with the top kicker. The betting sequence is telling: Minni 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, but checked the turn when the board became dangerous with three diamonds and an overcard Queen. Her 2400 bet on the river after the Jack falls is polarizing; she could be value betting a flush or a Queen, but she could also be bluffing with missed high cards like AK or smaller pairs, especially since she has shown a willingness to bluff in previous hands (e.g., Hand #95). Given that I have improved to a pair of Jacks and am getting 3:1 pot odds, I have a strong bluff-catcher in a heads-up situation. Calling is the correct play to maximize my long-term expected value and prevent exploitation, while still maintaining a healthy chip lead even if I am beat.

Heads-up on the button with K♥9♥ and 97.5BB effective — this is a clear open-raise spot. Limping gives the big blind great pot odds and misses the chance to seize initiative; folding is too passive. I’ll raise to a standard 3xBB (300 total) to apply pressure, build the pot with a playable suited hand, and define Flash’s range. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or pot-control depending on sizing); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops, leverage my suitedness and K-high, and control pot size on bad textures.

I am holding 5d 7d, which is a respectable suited gapper. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind. I am in the big blind and only need to call 200 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds (2:1) and the post-flop playability of suited connectors/gappers, this is a standard defend. My stack is significantly larger than Minni's (30,100 vs 9,500), so I can comfortably afford to see a flop and look for a favorable board or a drawing opportunity. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting might be unnecessary risk with this specific hand strength.

I am the Big Blind and act first on the flop. I have middle pair (5s) with a 7 kicker. This is a solid marginal hand, but not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop aggressor. I will check to see how Minni proceeds. If she bets a standard continuation size, I plan to call to see a turn and evaluate the strength of her range.

Heads-up on the button vs a caller — flop 5♥ T♣ 3♣ is very dry and heavily favors the raiser’s range. My K♥9♥ has no pair but blocks many Kx hands and has a tiny backdoor heart; it’s not worth checking and giving up initiative. I want to c-bet to fold out unpaired overcards and to charge club/straight draws while keeping pot controllable. I’ll bet ~50% pot (300 into 600) — large enough to apply pressure and deny free cards, but small enough to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If he folds I win the pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and his action.

I have middle pair (5s) with a 7 kicker on a 10-5-3 board. Minni's 1/2 pot bet is a standard continuation bet from her pre-flop range. Given the pot odds (300 to call into a 900 pot, providing 4:1), I have more than enough equity to continue. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Tx or overpairs) and would force out her bluffs. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and see how the turn develops.

The Turn card is an Ace of Hearts, which is a significant scare card. As the pre-flop raiser, this card is much better for Minni's perceived range (which includes many AX hands) than it is for mine. I currently hold middle pair (5s), but its value has decreased with the Ace on board. Leading out here would likely only get called by better hands. I will check to see how Minni responds; checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate whether she continues her aggression on a board that favors her range.

I opened preflop and c-bet the very dry flop; Flash called, so his range is wide (pairs, Kx/Qx, some floats, rare club combos). The turn A♥ both improves my equity and changes the story: I now have K♥9♥ with a 4-card heart combo (9 outs, ≈36% to hit on the river) and Flash checked the turn, showing weakness or pot-control. Betting now is a strong semi-bluff — it folds out many Kx/Qx/overcards and floating hands, continues to represent a strong Ace/hand, and builds the pot when I have real equity to hit. I’ll apply measured pressure while controlling risk: bet ~50% pot (600 into 1200). If I get a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold given stack dynamics); if he calls I’ll reevaluate the river based on the card and his action.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a major scare card that significantly favors Minni's pre-flop raising range. My hand, a pair of fives with a weak kicker, has now been demoted to third pair. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, calling a second barrel on an Ace turn with a marginal made hand often leads to difficult river decisions, especially since I'm out of position and Minni's stack is short enough that she may commit the rest on the river. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that this board hits her perceived range much harder than mine, folding is the safest way to preserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands of the tournament.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With a substantial chip lead and only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to commit chips with a marginal hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially against an opponent who has been aggressive and willing to defend their big blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to protect my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (SB) — open-raising very wide is standard. Q5o is a marginal holding but fine to open for position and to pressure the big blind. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 total to seize initiative and keep the pot controllable (that’s +150 more chips from me). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet since the hand is easily dominated; if called, c-bet favorable, dry boards (or a queen) and play small pots / pot-control on scary, coordinated boards.

I am holding Pocket Tens (Ts Tc), which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. Minni has min-raised from the button (Small Blind). Given my significant chip lead and the strength of my hand, I will 3-bet to 700. This builds the pot while I likely have the best hand and forces Minni to commit more of her 100 BB stack to see a flop. Since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I want to take control of the betting lead now.

I opened wide from the button with Q5o, but facing Flash’s 3-bet to 700 this hand is easily dominated and has very poor multi-street playability out of position. The 3-bet sizing represents a range heavy in pairs, broadways and stronger queens; Q5o has weak equity and the pot odds (~35%) don’t justify continuing. A 4‑bet bluff is out of the question with this holding, so I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

With only 9 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip lead of nearly 3:1, there is no reason to play a very weak hand like 3d 5c. While I have position post-flop, the hand has very little equity and does not perform well in a raised or even a limped pot. Preserving my stack and waiting for better spots to utilize my chip lead is the most prudent strategy to ensure I finish the tournament at the top of the leaderboard.

Heads-up on the button with K6s — a playable, positional hand. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call; I open to 3x the BB (300 total) to seize initiative, deny the big blind cheap equity, and isolate. This sizing keeps the pot manageable with a hand that can realize equity well in position; I'll fold to a large 3-bet but plan to c-bet and apply pressure on most flops if called.

I am holding pocket sevens (77), which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has opened for a standard 3x raise from the button. Given that I am out of position for the rest of the hand and have a significant stack lead, I will put in a 3-bet (re-raise). This allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or build value if called. I have noticed Minni has folded to several 3-bets recently (Hands #135, #140), so this is a high-frequency winning play here.

I raised to 300 on the button with K6s but Flash’s re‑raise to 900 is a large 3‑bet. K6s is a marginal hand here — heavily dominated by the sorts of hands Flash will 3‑bet (AK/AQ, Kx broadways and strong suited hands) and won’t have good realizable equity in big pots. A 4‑bet bluff into his deep stack would be thin and could easily get 5‑bet; calling 600 to play postflop keeps me in a difficult, often dominated spot. I’ll stick to my preflop plan and fold to this large 3‑bet, preserve my 10k stack, and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am holding QhTh on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand (suited broadway). I have a significant chip lead (nearly 3:1) and there are only 8 hands remaining in the tournament. I will put pressure on Minni by opening with a standard 3x raise to 300.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with K♠8♠ and ~99bb effective. K8s is a very defendable hand heads-up: suited, a King-blocker, and reasonable equity vs a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (making my total committed 300) — I only need ~33% equity to break even, which K8s generally has vs a wide button range. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled out of position and realize equity; 3-betting is an option but with this hand I prefer to see a flop and play based on texture (value/charge with top pair or strong draws, fold or pot-control when missed).

Out of position with K♠8♠ on A♣ J♠ 9♣. I have no pair — just K‑high — and only a runner‑runner spade flush possibility (very unlikely). The preflop raiser’s range contains a lot of A‑x, Jx and pair combos that dominate me, and this is a textbook spot to control the pot rather than bluff into a range that is often ahead. I’ll check to keep the pot small, plan to call small continuation bets to realize equity, fold to large pressure, and re‑evaluate on the turn (raise or lead only if I pick up a real improvement or a very favorable card).

On a flop of Ac-Js-9c, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King for a broadway straight). This board strongly favors my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, as I am more likely to hold an Ace or a strong Jack than the player in the Big Blind. Minni has checked to me, which is expected. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain initiative, represent a strong hand, and potentially take the pot down right now or set up a semi-bluff on later streets if my draw improves.

Out of position with K♠8♠ on A♣ J♠ 9♣. Pot is 900 and Flash’s flop bet is 300, so I only need ≈25% equity to break even (300 into a 1200 pot). K8s has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening/c-bet range (K‑blocker, chance to hit a King for top pair, small backdoor spade possibilities) and the bet is a small ~1/3‑pot probe. Raising OOP with no pair would be bloating the pot and committing stack with little fold equity against a deep opponent. I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot controlled, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to large turn aggression if still unimproved, but value or build the pot if I pair the K or pick up a strong draw.

Turn (A♣ J♠ 9♣ 7♣), I hold K♠8♠ and am out of position. I missed — no pair and my backdoor spade possibility is gone after the turn club. Flash opened wide and made a small flop c-bet; his continuing range contains many A‑x, Jx and pairs that dominate me. With no real equity to drive a bluff and poor fold equity OOP, raising or leading here would just bloat the pot with an inferior hand. I’ll check to control the pot, preserve my stack, and re‑evaluate on the river (only consider calling very small bets to see the river or folding to medium/large turn aggression; if I pair a King or hit a favorable river I can then bet/raise).

The turn 7 of clubs completes potential flushes and also creates more straight possibilities. My hand (Queen of Hearts, Ten of Hearts) currently only has Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. Given the wetness of the board and the fact that I do not hold a club, betting here is risky as Minni could be slow-playing an Ace, a flush, or a Jack. Checking back allows me to see a free river card, preserves my significant chip lead, and gives me a chance to realize my equity if a King or potentially a Queen or Ten falls, without risking more chips against a board that has become quite dangerous.

Board: A♣ J♠ 9♣ 7♣ 7♥. I hold K♠8♠ and missed — no pair, no flush. Flash opened and c‑bet small on the flop then checked the turn, a line that contains many A‑x, J‑x, 9‑x and medium pairs; many of those hands will call a river bluff. Pot is 1,200 and my stack is 9,400 — a river bluff would need significant fold equity (≈25–33% depending on sizing) to be +EV, and given the line I don’t expect that level of fold frequency. There’s no bet to call so the high-ev play is to check down and avoid risking chips on a low-odds bluff; if he bets I can fold to reasonable pressure (or occasional thin-call vs a very small bet). I’ll check and go to showdown (or fold to river aggression).

On the river, the board is Ac-Js-9c-7c-7h. I have Queen-high (QhTh). Minni has checked to me on the river after we both checked the turn. My pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet represented strength, but my turn check likely indicated a cap on my range or a draw. Minni called the flop bet, suggesting she has a piece of this board (likely an Ace, a Jack, a Nine, or a draw). While the river 7h pairs the board, it doesn't change much for my Queen-high. Given my massive chip lead and the fact that we are very close to the end of the tournament (Hand 143/150), I see little value in bluffing here. Minni is likely to call a river bet with any pair, and I have very little showdown value. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with 8♠6♦ — this is a marginal but playable hand in position. Calling 50 is passive; I want to seize initiative and use my positional advantage to apply pressure and steal the blind. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to build the pot when I get action from worse hands, gain fold equity, and keep postflop playability with deep stacks (~105 BB). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot control on dry/hostile boards.

I am holding Queen-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. This is a strong hand for heads-up play and is well ahead of Minni's likely opening range from the Button. Minni has a shorter stack (about 100 BBs), and I have a significant chip lead. While I have successfully 3-bet Minni in recent hands, calling here allows me to see a flop with a solid hand while keeping the pot size controlled. I'm getting 2:1 on the call, making it a mathematically easy decision.

I have flopped trips with a strong kicker (Queens) on a T-T-9 board. Given the board texture and my holding, I block most of the hands that would continue against a lead (the other Tens). It's highly unlikely Minni holds the last Ten. By checking, I allow Minni to follow through with a continuation bet with her air, overcards, or a Nine, which maximizes value. I am significantly ahead of her range, and playing this slow is the optimal way to extract chips from her remaining stack.

Flop is Td Ts 9c — I hold 8♠6♦: no pair, only a one-card gutshot to a 7 (4 outs). Heads-up my preflop raise gave me initiative and Flash’s calling range is wide (many missed overcards, K/Q/Jx, weak Ax, small pairs). This is a very good spot to c-bet for fold equity on a relatively dry paired board. I’ll size to about 50% pot to deny equity and fold out weaker holdings while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: bet 300 to take it down now. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — continue only if I hit the 7 or the turn is very favorable; fold to a large check-raise.

I have flopped trip Tens with a strong Queen kicker on a T-T-9 rainbow board. This is an exceptionally strong hand. Minni's 300-chip bet into a 600-chip pot is a standard continuation bet that could represent a wide range of hands, including air, straight draws (like QJ or 87), or a Nine. By calling instead of raising, I keep her range wide and encourage her to continue bluffing or value-betting weaker hands on the turn. Raising now would likely scare away everything but the few hands that beat me (like 99 or a better Ten), so slow-playing is the most profitable line to maximize value from her remaining 10,000 chips.

I have trip Tens with a Queen kicker on a board of T-T-9-2. This is an extremely strong hand. My objective is to extract maximum value from Minni's 10,000-chip stack. On the flop, I checked and called her continuation bet to keep her range wide and encourage bluffs. Since I am first to act on the turn, I will check again. This maintains the appearance of a marginal hand (like a Nine or a mid-pocket pair) and allows her to continue betting with bluffs, straight draws (like Q-J or 8-7), or a Nine. Leading out here would likely cause her to fold everything except a Ten or 9-9, whereas checking allows her to commit more chips to the pot with a weaker range.

I have 8♠6♦ on Td Ts 9c 2c — no pair and only four outs (any 7) to make a straight (~8.7% to hit the river). Flash called my flop c-bet, then checked the turn, which suggests a fairly sticky, often better range (T/9/pocket pairs, Ax/Kx, some club candidates). With the pot at 1,200 and deep stacks, a turn double-barrel has poor fold equity and will be called by most hands that currently beat me; I also risk a costly check-raise. Best line is pot control: check and see the river for free. Plan: if I hit a 7 on the river I value-bet; if I miss I’ll fold to meaningful aggression and only consider a river bluff when the card and opponent tendencies make it credible.

I have completed a full house (Tens full of Queens) on the river with [Th Qd]. The board is [Td Ts 9c 2c Qs]. I have played this hand deceptively by check-calling the flop and checking the turn, which has kept the pot small and Minni's range wide. Minni checked behind on the turn, suggesting she likely doesn't have a Ten. The Queen on the river is a great card because it improves me to a full house and might have given Minni a top pair (if she has AQ, KQ, or JQ) or a straight (if she has KJ). Since I am first to act, I will lead out with a value bet. A check risks her checking behind with a Nine or a mid-pocket pair. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot is a standard value sizing that looks like I am trying to protect a marginal hand or buying the pot, which might induce a call from a Nine or a Queen.

I have 8♠6♦ on Td Ts 9c 2c Qs — no pair and my hand has no river outs (final street). Opponent called my flop c-bet and checked the turn, which is consistent with a sticky, often-better range (T/9/medium pocket pairs, some Qx). Flash’s small 800 river bet into the ~2000 pot requires ~40% equity to justify a call (800/(1200+800)=40%), which I don’t have. Raising as a bluff is possible but high-variance: Flash has shown willingness to call down and has a deep stack, so a raise will be called or re-raised too often. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

We are currently on hand 145 of 150 with a substantial chip lead over Minni. Holding 5-3 offsuit, which is among the weakest hands in heads-up play, there is little reason to commit chips. While I could attempt a raise to steal the blinds, Minni has shown some resistance recently, and with a nearly 3-to-1 chip advantage and only a few hands remaining, I will opt for the low-risk play and fold this garbage hand to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Th4c — a marginal but playable hand in position. In heads-up play I open a wide range and prefer to take the initiative rather than limp. Raising gives fold equity, wins the 150 chip pot immediately if BB folds, and lets me play postflop in position if called. I choose a standard 3xBB open to 300 to be consistent with table sizing and to avoid giving too-good price to a wide defend. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold most of the time (hand has poor equity vs strong 3-bets); if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on obvious overcards.

I hold AQo, a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been opening frequently from the button and has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets recently, as seen in Hands #140 and #142. I will raise to 1000 to extract value and maintain aggression, leveraging my significant stack lead as we approach the end of the tournament.

I opened to 300 with Th4c heads-up and got a 3‑bet to 1000. The pot is 1300; calling 700 to play for 2000 requires ~35% equity. Th4o is a weak, frequently dominated hand vs a 3‑bet range (Ax, Kx, pairs, broadways) and won’t have the necessary equity or dependable postflop playability despite having position. 4‑bet bluffing here is also wrong given stack depth and opponent’s likely calling/4‑betting ranges. The 300 is sunk cost — preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Action: fold.

I am currently the chip leader by a significant margin with only 4 hands remaining in the tournament. King-Five offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the Button in a heads-up situation. Minni has recently shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression (Hands #146, #142, #140).
