By raising to 300, I exert pressure on her stack and can likely take down the blinds or play the hand in position if she calls. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, I want to maintain my lead without taking unnecessary risks.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB) in heads‑up. I’m on the big blind with Th3c — a marginal offsuit hand but with reasonable equity versus a very wide HU opening range. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% break‑even equity and costs only a small fraction of my 9,650 stack, so I can realize equity and potentially pick up the pot or hit a pair. I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with a weak offsuit hand — a raise to 500 would be a thin bluff and unnecessarily inflates the pot. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with any T/3/top‑pair or meaningful draws, and fold to heavy pressure on dry boards. Call 300 total (including my BB).

Check. I have T3o on a dry Q♣‑7♦‑2♠ flop with no pair and no meaningful draw — very little showdown value and no real equity to continue with aggression OOP. Flash opened wide preflop and will c‑bet frequently, so checking controls the pot size, lets me realize my equity, and induces bluffs I can call or fold to depending on sizing. Plan: check‑call small continuation bets to see a turn (look to continue only if I pair the Ten/3 or see clear weakness), fold to large pressure, and re‑evaluate if he checks back.

I have a significant chip lead and we are down to the final few hands of the tournament. I raised pre-flop with King-Five offsuit and Minni called. The flop of Deuce-Seven-Queen is quite dry and likely missed a large portion of Minni's defending range. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot). This allows me to take down the pot immediately if she missed, or at least define where I stand in the hand. Given my stack size, this is a low-risk way to maintain pressure.

Heads‑up on a dry Q♣‑7♦‑2♠ flop with Th3c — I checked and Flash c‑bet a small 300 into a 900 pot. Calling 300 requires ~25% equity to break even. I have ~6 clean outs (three Tens + three Treys) for ≈24% to hit by the river, plus some backdoor club/runner possibilities, so my two‑street equity is roughly on the break‑even line and improves versus a wide SB c‑bet range. The call is a small portion of my 9,450 stack (~3%), lets me realize equity and fold to real turn aggression; I’ll only continue unless I pair or pick up a clear draw or see a very weak turn action. Therefore I’ll call the 300 and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to large pressure, raise/value if I improve).

I missed — holding T♥3♣ on Q♣‑7♦‑2♠‑2♣. I have only 6 clean outs (3 Tens + 3 Threes) ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river and no realistic backdoor flush. Pot is 1,200 and there is currently no bet, so betting OOP is a thin bluff that bloats the pot when I have very little equity and little fold equity on a paired turn. Best line is to check and see a cheap river; if I pair on the river I'll value bet, otherwise I can fold to meaningful aggression.

With only a few hands left in the tournament and a substantial chip lead, my primary objective is to minimize unnecessary risk. The turn card is a second deuce, which is largely a blank. Since Minni called my flop continuation bet, she likely has a piece of the board, such as a seven or a queen, or a medium pocket pair. Checking back the turn allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially realize my showdown value with King-high if she is on a draw or holding a weaker hand. There is no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand given the current tournament standings.

I have T♥3♣ on 2♠-7♦-Q♣-2♣-6♣. I never improved — I don’t have a pair or a flush (there are three clubs on board but I hold only one). Flash bet the flop and then checked the turn; that line looks like either a single-pair/top-pair type or a missed hand controlling the pot, not a hand I can credibly fold out a lot of river range. My 3♣ is a very weak blocker to two‑club combos (the only hands that have a made flush here), so my bluff equity is limited. Pot is only 1,200 and my stack is ~9,150 (SPR ~7.6), so risking chips to bluff a thin spot with no showdown value is unnecessary. I stick to my earlier plan: check and fold to any river aggression — don’t bluff in a spot with poor blockers and an opponent who likely calls light heads‑up.

I have a substantial chip lead with only three hands remaining in the tournament after this one. On the river, the board is 2s-7d-Qc-2c-6c, and I hold King-high. Minni has checked to me for the third time (pre-flop, turn, and river). While I could attempt a bluff to take down the 1200-chip pot, there is significant risk in doing so if she has a marginal hand like a seven or a small pocket pair that she intends to check-call with. Given my primary goal of protecting my massive lead to ensure a tournament win, checking back to see a showdown is the most prudent and low-risk play. I preserve my stack and avoid any potential for a check-raise that would force me to fold.

Heads-up on the button with K♦7♠ — marginal but playable. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and the pot is 150; calling 50 just gives up initiative. Against one opponent in the BB I should be aggressive and try to steal the blind/isolate. Standard open size in this game is ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 total. Plan: take the initiative — c-bet many flops if called; fold to a large 3-bet unless the sizing and reads justify continuing. With only a few hands left in the tournament, I prefer to be aggressive from position to build my stack.

We are currently on hand 148 of 150 with a substantial lead (over 3:1 in chips). Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind in a heads-up situation against a 3x open. Calling allows us to see the flop and maintain control of the pot size, which aligns with our strategy of protecting our lead in the final hands of the tournament without taking unnecessary risks.

I have middle pair (Queens) on an Ace-high board. Since I am the chip leader and the tournament is nearing its end, I want to avoid inflating the pot unnecessarily. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the board. If she bets, I can decide whether to continue based on the size, but for now, checking is the most cautious and effective way to play this hand.

I was the preflop aggressor (I opened to 300) and Flash just checked the Ace‑high, two‑hearts flop (Ah 8c Qh). I hold K♦7♠ — no hearts, no pair, only a King‑high backdoor. Heads‑up the BB’s calling range is wide (broadways, small pairs, suited connectors, some A/Qx), and many of those hands missed this flop or have only a draw. I want to use my initiative to take the pot down now while keeping the pot controllable against a much deeper stack. A half‑pot c‑bet (300 into 600) gives enough fold equity to win the pot vs missed hands and denies equity to heart draws, but isn’t so large that I’m committing my stack with a weak holding. If Flash raises I’ll give up and preserve chips; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with real equity or a good read. Betting 300 represents strength (Ax/Qx) and maximizes my short‑term fold equity here.

I have the middle pair (Queens) on an Ace-high board. Minni's 300-chip bet is a standard continuation bet. With a substantial chip lead and only three hands remaining in the tournament, I should play cautiously but not fold a hand this strong. Calling is the best move to keep the pot manageable while seeing the turn. It allows me to catch potential bluffs or lower pairs without risking too much of my stack if she actually holds an Ace.

I am in a very strong position as the chip leader with only three hands remaining in the tournament. My goal is to minimize risk and protect my stack. I have middle pair (Queens) on an Ace-high board. By checking, I am exercising pot control and avoiding a situation where I might be forced to commit too many chips against a potential Ace or better. If Minni checks back, I get a free look at the river; if she bets, I can evaluate the situation based on the bet size while keeping the pot manageable.

I have K♦7♠ on Ah 8c Qh 6d — I missed entirely (no hearts, no pair). I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash called the flop and then checked the turn. His called range is heavy with hands that beat me (Ax, Qx, pocket pairs) plus some floats; I have almost no showdown value and no meaningful blockers. Betting the turn would be a thin double‑barrel that can be check‑raised or called by much of his range, risking a portion of my stack with little fold equity. With the pot already 1200 and my stack ~8550, the prudent line is to control the pot and see a cheap river — I’ll check behind, preserve chips, and re‑evaluate on the river (fold to a sizable river bet; consider a river bluff only if the card and line justify it).

I have middle pair (Queens) on a board that has remained relatively static since the flop. Minni's check on the turn suggests she may not hold a strong Ace or is playing a marginal hand cautiously. With a substantial chip lead and only three hands remaining in the tournament, my priority is to minimize risk and protect my stack. Checking the river is the safest way to get to showdown and realize my hand's value without potentially inflating the pot against a trapping hand or a better Queen.

Board: Ah 8c Qh 6d 2c. I hold K♦7♠ — no pair, no hearts, essentially zero showdown value vs a caller of my flop c-bet. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with hands that beat me (Ax, Qx, medium pairs) and with controlled floats that will often call a river bluff. I have no meaningful blockers to A or Q and therefore very little fold equity. Math check: with a 600 half‑pot bluff I’d need ≈33% fold equity; given his line I expect a lower fold frequency, so a bluff is +EV only rarely and is high variance with stacks this deep. Tournament context (2 hands left, my stack 8.55k vs his 30k) favors preservation over a thin bluff. Plan: take showdown for free — check behind. If he bets into me I’ll fold to any non‑trivial sizing (call only a very small blocker bet).

In this heads-up situation, I'm on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit, which is a very strong hand. With a significant chip lead and only two hands remaining in the tournament, I want to apply pressure on Minni. A standard opening raise to 300 is appropriate to either take down the blinds immediately or play the pot in position if called.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 4h3h — a small suited connector with good postflop playability vs a wide heads‑up opening range. Pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (total committed 300) to call. Raising here would put me out of position postflop (Flash has button), and a 3‑bet bluff with 4‑3s is suboptimal against a deep, willing caller. Calling gives ~33% immediate pot odds and strong implied odds with 84bb effective — I can realize equity on many flops (flush/straight/pair) and fold to heavy action when I miss. Plan: call to see the flop and play aggressively only when I connect; otherwise pot control/fold to large aggression.

I missed this flop. With 4h3h on Kh-8s-5d I have no pair, no meaningful straight draw, and only a backdoor (runner-runner) heart possibility — very low equity. Flash is a wide preflop opener and will c-bet frequently, but out of position I should not build the pot with a hand this weak. Pot control is correct: check and fold to significant pressure. I may call a very small probe (cheap turn price), but I will not invest more without improving. Checking preserves chips, keeps bluffs in my range, and follows my preflop plan to realize equity and fold to heavy action when I miss.

On this dry King-high flop, I will lead with a standard continuation bet after my pre-flop raise. The board [Kh, 8s, 5d] is generally better for the pre-flop aggressor's range. Minni checked to me, and a half-pot bet of 300 is likely to take the pot down if she has missed the flop, while also allowing me to control the size of the hand given my substantial chip lead with only two hands remaining in the tournament.

I missed this flop with 4h3h — no pair and only a backdoor heart possibility. Flash’s c-bet is small (300 into a 900 pot), which gives me ~25% immediate pot odds, and with deep stacks it’s cheap to see a turn card. Raising OOP with this junk is a losing line (I don’t block relevant strong hands and would be committing unnecessarily). I’ll call the 300 more (bringing my total committed to 600) to realize equity and see a turn, but will fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve.

Turn 6h dramatically improves the hand: with 4h3h I now have a four‑card heart draw (9 hearts left → ~19.6% to hit a flush on the river) plus additional straight and pairing outs. Flash checked the turn after c‑betting the flop, which looks like weakness or pot‑control with a marginal hand. This is a good spot to semi‑bluff — I can win the pot immediately by folding out Kx/Ax and other unpaired hands, and if called I still have meaningful equity to improve. I don’t want to overcommit OOP, so a half‑pot bet balances fold equity and pot control. If Flash raises significantly I will fold this draw; if he calls I’ll go to the river with a plan to evaluate based on the card and sizing. Action: bet ~50% pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable.

On the turn, the board is King-Eight-Five-Six. I hold Ace-Seven, giving me a four-card straight (5-6-7-8) and eight outs to a straight (any 4 or 9). Minni has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot. With 3:1 pot odds and a massive chip lead in the penultimate hand of the tournament, calling is the most logical choice. It allows me to see the river and potentially hit my straight or an Ace, while keeping the pot manageable and protecting my dominant stack position.

I missed — I have Q: 4♥3♥ on K♥-8♠-5♦-6♥-9♣: no pair, missed my flush and straight. I turned a semi‑bluff (600 into the pot) and Flash called, so his calling range now includes Kx, medium pairs, and sticky made hands/draws. With the pot at 2400 and him showing willingness to call turn, my river bluff has very limited fold equity (I’d need a high fold frequency to make any sizing +EV), and I have no showdown value to extract. Betting risks a significant portion of my effective stack with poor equity vs his calling range; the correct, low‑variance line is to check and fold to any river raise. Action: check and preserve chips.

I have completed a nine-high straight (5-6-7-8-9) on the river with the Nine of Clubs. Minni's turn lead of 600 into a 1200 pot after the Six of Hearts appeared suggests she either had a made hand like a King or was semi-bluffing with a straight draw (like 7-x). Her check on the river is likely either a cautious play with a pair of Kings or an attempt to check-trap. However, based on Hand #137, where Minni called a large river bet with a single pair, I should bet for value here. While 10-7 would make a higher straight, the vast majority of her range consists of Kings or other straights (using a 7) that I either beat or chop with. A half-pot bet of 1200 is a standard value size to extract chips from a King.

I hold 4♥3♥ on K♥‑8♠‑5♦‑6♥‑9♣ — I have no pair and I missed the flush/straight on the river. Flash’s line (preflop open → flop c‑bet → check turn → call my turn semi‑bluff) looks like a fairly sticky value range (Kx, medium pairs or single-pair hands) rather than a pure bluffing line. The pot is 3,600 and he bet 1,200; calling 1,200 would create a 4,800 pot and requires ~25% equity to breakeven. My actual equity with no pair is essentially negligible vs his calling/raising range (<~10%), so a call is clearly -EV (e.g. at 10% equity EV ≈ 480 − 1,200 = −720). A raise would be an overblown bluff OOP with little fold equity and large downside. Preserve chips for the final hand — fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) I’ll have position postflop. 4♣3♥ is weak, but in heads-up open-raising a very wide range is standard to apply pressure and play in position. I’ll make the minimum open to 200 (2x BB) — it’s small, keeps the pot manageable given my ~73BB stack, and often steals the blind. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand; if he calls I can use positional advantage to play the flop. Total commitment shown is 200.

On the final hand of the tournament, I hold Ace-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has a relatively short stack of 7,150 compared to my 32,550. Since the goal is to maximize my final stack and I have a significant range advantage over a standard button opening range, I will put in a 3-bet to 600 to extract value or take the pot immediately. If she chooses to shove, A7o is a clear call in this heads-up dynamic.

Flash 3-bet to 600 after my 200 open — a 3x 3-bet that polarizes toward strong value hands (and some bluffs). My hand 4♣3♥ is very weak: poor showdown equity and terrible post‑flop playability vs a 3‑bet range. To call I must put in 400 into a 800 pot (≈33% pot odds) but 4‑3o’s equity vs a typical 3‑bet range is well below that. 4‑betting to 1000 would be an unnecessary bluff with little fold equity against a deep, aggressive opponent. I’ll follow my preflop plan and fold — preserve my ~71BB for better spots.
